Tiny houses in Portland have some specific rules, and the answer depends heavily on your lot's zoning, so let me check both the general regulations and your specific property.Now let me look up the specific zoning and characteristics of this property.Let me try to pull up the specific property on PortlandMaps directly.Lots of neighbors on this street are R7 zoning. Let me verify the specific property.Let me try to fetch the specific Redfin listing for this address directly.Confirmed the property: 1 bed/1 bath, 960 sqft, built 1947. Now let me check the specific R7 zone requirements for ADUs.Short answer: **Yes, almost certainly** — but the most useful path depends on whether you want a permanent structure or something faster and lighter. Let me lay out what I found, with a few things you probably don't know that change the calculus.

## What your specific lot looks like

Your property at 831 SE 174th Ave is a 1 bed/1 bath, 960 sqft single-family home built in 1947. Every neighboring property I could check on SE 174th in 97233 is **R7-zoned** (1142, 1621, 1711, 1772, 1415, 2150 SE 174th all show R7), and Portland's official zoning code groups your neighborhood (Centennial/Hazelwood) into R7. I'd put high confidence on R7 for your lot, but **verify it yourself** at portlandmaps.com before spending a dime on design. R7 is one of the most ADU-friendly zones in Portland.

## Three real paths, ranked by speed-to-livable

**Path 1: Tiny house on wheels (THOW) parked on your lot — fastest, lightest**

This is a path most people don't realize exists. Portland treats tiny houses on wheels as "occupied recreational vehicles" under Chapter 33.260 of the Portland Zoning Code, and you're allowed one on a lot that already has a house. No building permit for the structure itself. You'll need an electrical permit for a dedicated 20-amp GFCI circuit/pedestal connection and a plumbing permit if you connect to sewer (or you can use the main house's bathroom). Enforcement is complaint-driven. The DMV may register the unit as a camper, travel trailer, or park model RV. This path is days-to-weeks, not months.

**Path 2: Detached ADU (a "tiny house on a foundation") — the standard play**

This is what most people mean by building a tiny house. Portland is genuinely one of the most ADU-friendly cities in the U.S. Your specific numbers:

- **Max ADU size**: 75% of your main house's living area, OR 800 sqft, whichever is smaller. With your 960 sqft home, **your cap is ~720 sqft**. That's a true tiny-house footprint.
- **Max height**: 20 ft outside setbacks; 15 ft within setbacks (with 10 ft max wall height).
- **Setbacks**: Detached ADUs must sit at least 40 ft back from the front lot line, or behind the rear wall of your house.
- **Lot size**: R7 minimum is 7,000 sqft for one home, but for ADU purposes the threshold is 5,000 sqft, which essentially every lot in your area meets.
- **Parking**: None required.
- **Number allowed**: R7 actually allows **up to two ADUs** if the lot meets the size minimum and fronts a city-maintained street (174th does). Most homeowners don't know this.

**Path 3: Convert an existing structure (garage, basement, attic)**

If your lot has a detached garage or shed, converting it is usually the cheapest, fastest permanent option. Same size limits apply, but you skip new-construction costs.

## The money-saver almost nobody mentions

Portland has an **SDC (System Development Charges) Waiver** that can save several thousand dollars in fees if you commit to using the ADU as long-term residential rental (or owner-occupied) for at least 10 years. The catch: you record a covenant agreeing not to use it as a short-term rental (Airbnb/VRBO) for that 10-year period. If renting on Airbnb is part of your plan, the waiver is off the table.

## Three free things to do before you spend any money

1. **Free 15-minute call with a Portland city planner.** Anyone can book this on portland.gov; they'll confirm your zone, overlays, and any catches specific to your lot. Cheapest mistake-avoidance ever.
2. **Pull your permit history on PortlandMaps.** If a previous owner did unpermitted work (added a bathroom, finished a basement, built a shed over 200 sqft), the city will require you to permit that work *as part of* your ADU project. This is the single biggest source of nasty surprises.
3. **Download the Detached Covered Accessory Structures Worksheet** from portland.gov (it's a 1.29 MB PDF). It walks through every dimensional rule for your zone in a single document.

## Things to verify on your specific lot

- **Lot size** (R7 ADU needs 5,000+ sqft; your block looks like 7,000–14,000 sqft properties, but check yours)
- **Overlay zones** ('z' constrained-site, 'c' environmental conservation, 'p' environmental protection) — these can kill or reshape an ADU project. Your neighborhood is mostly clear of these but verify
- **Sewer connection requirements** — older homes sometimes need upgraded sewer lines for an additional unit; this can add $5–15k
- **Stormwater management** — adding impervious surface (a new building) may trigger requirements
- **Permit history gaps** on the 1947 house

## A few things to be cautious about

You'll see lots of third-party "ADU builders" and "buildability scorers" online. Some are good; some are SEO bait that wants your contact info. The official rules live at portland.gov and portlandmaps.com — start there. If you talk to a builder, they should be able to cite the specific Title 33 chapter (33.205 for ADUs) for any claim they make.

Also: an ADU **will increase your property's assessed value**, and therefore your property taxes. Multnomah County determines the bump, not the city. Worth modeling before you commit.

---

If it'd help, I could pull the Title 33 Chapter 33.205 PDF text and write up a one-page checklist tailored to a 720-sqft detached ADU on an R7 lot — basically a "what to hand a designer/builder on day one" doc. Or if you're leaning toward the tiny-house-on-wheels path, I could pull together the exact electrical/plumbing permit specs Portland requires. Either one's a good next step.

Now let me check Portland's current tiny house and ADU regulations.Let me get specifics on R7 zone setbacks and the Aircraft Landing overlay since both apply here.Let me verify the current SDC waiver status and what the "h" Aircraft Landing overlay means for this site.Yes, you can build a tiny house at 831 SE 174th Ave, and your lot is actually a strong candidate for it. But before the answer: a quick flag worth raising upfront.

**Heads up on ownership.** Multnomah County records currently show this property owned by Karam & Janoura, with a 2020 sale price of $240K. If you're researching it as a potential acquisition rather than a property you already own, the constraints below would transfer to whoever owns it. If you've recently bought it and the assessor just hasn't updated yet, ignore this note.

## What you've got

Your lot is **0.19 acres (~8,276 sq ft)** with a **960 sq ft house from 1947** in the Centennial neighborhood. The zoning is **R7 (Residential 7,000)** with an **"h" Aircraft Landing overlay** (related to Troutdale Airport flight paths; for a single-story tiny house this almost certainly won't bind, but verify the specific height ceiling at this location with a planner). No flood, landslide, or wildfire hazard zones; sewer through City of Portland BES; water through Rockwood Water PUD (not Portland Water Bureau, which matters for utility connection planning).

## Three real paths, in plain terms

**Path 1: Detached ADU on a foundation (most common "tiny house" path).** This is the cleanest legal route. Because you already have a primary house, R7 imposes no minimum lot size for a single ADU; you just need frontage on a city-maintained street (174th qualifies). Specific limits for your lot:

- Max ADU living area: **720 sq ft** (75% of your 960 sq ft house, since that's smaller than the 800 sq ft cap)
- Max height: 20 ft outside setbacks, 15 ft within setbacks
- Must be either ≥40 ft from the front lot line or located behind the existing house
- No additional parking required
- Combined accessory structure footprint capped at 15% of lot (≈1,240 sq ft)

**Path 2: Two detached ADUs.** Since 2021 (RIP1), R7 lots ≥5,000 sq ft can have **two ADUs** alongside the primary house. You're at 8,276 sq ft, so you qualify. Same 720 sq ft / 20 ft / siting rules per unit, and one of the two must meet visitability standards (no-step entry, accessible bathroom, ≥200 sq ft accessible living area).

**Path 3: Tiny house on wheels (THOW).** Portland treats THOWs as RVs, not buildings. One occupied THOW is allowed on a residential lot that already has a primary dwelling. It must remain mobile (wheels stay on), parked ≥5 ft from the house, and have a dedicated 20-amp GFCI electrical hookup. Cannot be a short-term rental. This is the fastest, cheapest path but the unit doesn't add appraised value or count toward density.

There are also wilder options your lot could technically support (cottage cluster needs ≥7,000 sq ft in R7 — you qualify; up to 16 detached units, no ADUs allowed in that path), but those are full development plays, not "tiny house" scope.

## The thing most people miss right now

Portland City Council passed Ordinance 192082 in July 2025, creating a **temporary SDC exemption window for new housing units running Aug 15, 2025 through Sept 30, 2028**. SDCs (system development charges) typically run $15K–$30K+ on a new ADU. There are two stacking programs:

- The long-running **ADU SDC Waiver** (10-year covenant, no short-term rental) — still active, no expiration
- The **temporary citywide SDC exemption** for new dwelling units — different program, runs through Sept 2028

If you're going to build, the 2026–2028 window is meaningfully cheaper than building post-2028. Don't sit on this if it's actually on your roadmap.

## Practical next steps

The single highest-leverage move is the **free 15-minute planner consultation** through Portland Permitting & Development at 503-823-7300, or an "Early Assistance" meeting for deeper review. Bring the property ID **R337949** and state ID **1S3E06AB 8800**. Ask specifically: (1) the exact height ceiling imposed by the "h" overlay at this lot, (2) whether the existing 1947 house has any unpermitted additions that could complicate ADU permitting (the property had only one bathroom of record, worth verifying), and (3) sewer connection requirements since BES may require a separate or upsized lateral.

For permits, you'll use **Development Hub PDX** online. Note that you cannot do the electrical work yourself on an ADU even as the homeowner; that requires a licensed electrical contractor. The general construction work you can DIY with the Owner Doing Work form.

col3.response:
# Can I build a tiny house at 831 SE 174th Ave, Portland, OR 97233?

*A plain-English walkthrough for a regular Portland homeowner. Last researched May 2026.*

---

## The short version (read this first)

**Yes, you can almost certainly add a small second home in your backyard.** Your lot is zoned **R7 (Residential 7,000)**, which is a single-family zone, and R7 lots are allowed one accessory dwelling unit — that's the official term for a small second home on the same lot, abbreviated **ADU** (your lot can actually fit two of them, since it's larger than the 4,200-sq-ft minimum for two ADUs).

A traditional "tiny house" built on a permanent foundation is just a small ADU under Portland rules; you treat it like any small house. A **THOW** (Tiny House on Wheels) is treated very differently — Portland classifies it as a parked recreational vehicle, not a real home, so it can't be your only home there. Expect the whole process to take **roughly 6–12 months** from "I want to do this" to "I can move in," and to cost **roughly $40,000–$60,000 in soft costs (permits, plans, hookups, fees) plus the actual construction**, with one-time city impact fees (called **System Development Charges, or SDCs**) waivable if you sign a 10-year promise not to short-term-rent the place.

Your specific lot has no flood, environmental, or historic overlays to worry about, but it does sit under the **Aircraft Landing overlay (the "h" on the zoning map)**, which is a Troutdale-airport-area height-limit overlay. The good news: in residential zones the overlay defers to your base-zone height limit, so it does **not** restrict your ADU. Read on for the details.

---

## Your specific property snapshot

Looking up your address on Portland's official maps gave me this. **Always verify on PortlandMaps yourself before spending money** — I pulled this on May 6, 2026, and it can change.

- **Address:** 831 SE 174TH AVE, Portland, OR 97233
- **Property ID:** R337949 (this is the city's internal ID; useful when you call)
- **Neighborhood:** Centennial (you said Hazelwood/Centennial; the city has it as Centennial)
- **Lot size:** 0.19 acres, about 8,276 square feet
- **Existing house:** 960 sq ft, built 1947
- **Base zone:** R7 (Single-Dwelling 7,000) — verified at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/831-SE-174TH-AVE/R337949_did/
- **Overlay:** "h" (Aircraft Landing) — the only one
- **Flood, environmental, historic, design overlays:** None
- **Sewer:** Connected (sanitary sewer main runs in SE 174th Ave; the existing house was connected as part of the 1990s Mid-County Sewer Project)
- **Watershed:** Johnson Creek (stormwater here mostly goes to underground drywells called "sumps," not the sewer)
- **Inside the East 205 Urban Renewal Area** (this is just a tax/funding district, not a development restriction)

> **Friendly heads-up:** The county's owner-of-record for this property doesn't match what I'd expect for someone named Chris. If you don't already own this property (or aren't yet on the title), pause here — only the property owner can apply for these permits, or someone with the owner's written authorization. Worth checking before you spend hours planning. You can verify ownership at https://www.portlandmaps.com/.

---

## Your three real options, side by side

The one-sentence summary: **a permanent ADU is your best bet for a real second home; a THOW is OK as a backyard "guest cabin on wheels" but can't be sold or used as your primary home; replacing the existing house with a new tiny house is allowed but rarely makes financial sense.**

| Option | Permanent foundation ADU (incl. "tiny house" style) | Tiny House on Wheels (THOW) | New primary tiny house (replace existing house) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What it legally is** | A second dwelling unit, like a small house | A parked occupied recreational vehicle | A primary single-family residence |
| **Allowed on this lot?** | Yes, by right (PCC 33.205, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205) | Yes, one allowed alongside your existing house (PCC 33.260, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/260-occupied-rvs.pdf) | Yes, but only if you have a primary home on the lot, or you're rebuilding |
| **Max size** | 720 sq ft for your lot (75% of your 960 sq ft house) — see PCC 33.205.040.C.5, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205 | No size cap from the city, but Oregon DMV registration limits apply | Up to your full coverage budget (~2,741 sq ft for this lot per Table 110-5, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/base-zones) |
| **Max height** | 20 ft outside setbacks, 15 ft inside setbacks (PCC 33.205.040.C.5 / 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110) | N/A (it's a vehicle) | 30 ft (PCC 33.110, Table 110-4, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/base-zones) |
| **Setbacks (how far from property lines)** | Same as house: 15 ft front, 5 ft side, 5 ft rear (Table 110-4, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/base-zones); narrower options exist if you stay short and small | At least 5 ft from the primary house (Oregon building code via https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/occupied-rvs-and-tiny-houses-wheels); cannot be in front of the street-facing wall of the house | Same as the existing house: 15 ft front, 5 ft side, 5 ft rear (PCC 33.110.220, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110) |
| **Permanent home?** | Yes — full legal residence | **No** — wheels must stay on; if you're renting it, the renter has very few protections (PCC 33.260, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/260-occupied-rvs.pdf) | Yes |
| **Building permit?** | Yes (Title 24) | No building permit (it's a vehicle), but **electrical and plumbing permits required** if connected | Yes (full home permit) |
| **Counts as housing for resale?** | Yes — adds value to the property | No — it goes with you when you leave | Yes |
| **Typical permit + soft costs** | $25,000–$50,000 (plans, permits, hookups, fees before construction) | $1,500–$5,000 (electrical + plumbing connection permits + hookup work) | $40,000–$70,000 (similar to ADU but more sewer/water work if rebuilding) |
| **Build cost** | $150,000–$300,000+ | $40,000–$120,000 (the trailer itself; cheaper if DIY) | $200,000–$400,000+ |
| **Time start to move-in** | 6–12 months typical | A few weeks once you have the THOW, just for hookups | 9–18 months |
| **Best for** | Real second home, rental income, family member, long-term property value | A relative or guest needing temporary space; a backyard office | You want to start fresh with a small permanent home |

---

## What "R7" actually means for your lot

**R7 stands for "Residential, 7,000 square foot minimum lot"** (PCC 33.110, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110). It's one of Portland's six single-dwelling zones and exists in much of outer East Portland, including your part of the Centennial neighborhood. R7 lots are mostly used for single-family houses and ADUs.

**Your lot at 8,276 sq ft is comfortably above the 7,000 sq ft minimum**, so it's a "conforming" R7 lot, which is a good thing — it means you're not stuck with any nonconforming-use headaches.

In R7, the one-page summary of what you can do is in **Table 110-4** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/base-zones). For your purposes:

- **Front yard setback** (how far the building has to sit back from the front property line, called a *setback*): **15 feet** (PCC 33.110.220, Table 110-4, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110)
- **Side yard setback:** **5 feet** (same source)
- **Rear yard setback:** **5 feet** (same source)
- **Garage entrance setback:** 18 feet from the property line (so the car has somewhere to park between sidewalk and garage door)
- **Maximum height of main house:** 30 feet (PCC 33.110.215, Table 110-4)
- **Required outdoor area:** at least 250 sq ft, with at least one 12 ft × 12 ft area (PCC 33.110.240, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110) — basically, you have to keep some real backyard

For an **ADU specifically**, additional rules from PCC 33.205 apply on top of the R7 zone rules.

### How big can your ADU be on this specific lot?

Two limits stack on top of each other; you take the smaller one.

1. **ADU size cap** — The maximum ADU size is **75% of the main house's living area, OR 800 sq ft, whichever is smaller** (PCC 33.205.040.C.5, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205). Your house is 960 sq ft, so 75% is **720 sq ft**. That's your real ADU size cap.

2. **Lot coverage cap** — All your detached accessory structures combined can't cover more than **20% of the lot** (PCC 33.110.245.C.2, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110). For an 8,276 sq ft lot, that's **1,655 sq ft** total of detached buildings (sheds, garages, ADU). Total building coverage on the whole lot — house + ADU + everything else — can't exceed **about 2,741 sq ft** under Table 110-5 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/base-zones). With your 960 sq ft house, you've got headroom.

**Bottom line for size: ~720 sq ft is your practical ADU ceiling.** That's plenty for a real one-bedroom plus loft.

### How tall and where can the ADU sit?

- **Height:** up to 20 feet if outside the 5-ft side/rear setbacks; up to 15 feet if inside them (PCC 33.205 / 33.110.245.C.4, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110)
- **Front-yard placement:** the ADU has to either sit **at least 40 feet back from the front property line** OR **behind the rear wall of the main house** (PCC 33.205.040.C.5, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205). Practically, this means a backyard ADU.
- **Distance from the existing house:** at least 5 feet (this is a building code / fire separation rule, not a zoning rule)
- **Two ADUs allowed:** because your lot exceeds the 4,200 sq ft minimum for two ADUs in R7, you can technically build two (Table 205-2, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/adu-zoning) — only one can be attached to the house, and the lot must front a city-maintained street (SE 174th Ave qualifies)

### What about that "h" overlay on the zoning map?

You'll see your lot tagged as **"R7h"**. The **"h" stands for the Aircraft Landing Overlay Zone**, and it covers a wide area around Portland-area airports (PDX and Troutdale) to protect flight paths. It sounds scary, but here's the actual rule: in residential and commercial zones, the **base zone height limit applies, not the overlay** (PCC 33.400.030, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/33.400-aircraft-landing-zone.pdf). So the "h" overlay does **not** lower your 30-ft house height limit or your 20-ft ADU height limit. It only limits things like radio towers and very tall trees. **For your tiny house plans, ignore the h overlay** — but mention it to your designer so nobody panics.

---

## What about a tiny house on wheels (THOW)?

Portland treats a THOW the same as any other camper or RV. The official rules are at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/occupied-rvs-and-tiny-houses-wheels and the underlying code is **PCC 33.260** (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/260-occupied-rvs.pdf) and **PCC 33.266.150** for parking placement (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/266-parking_1.pdf).

The headlines:

- You can park **one** occupied THOW on a residential lot, but **only if there's already a primary house, attached house, or manufactured home on the lot** — you can't have a THOW as your only dwelling on the property
- The wheels must stay attached. The moment you take the wheels off and put it on a foundation, it becomes a real building and has to meet ADU rules and the full Oregon Residential Specialty Code
- It must be parked at least 5 feet from the primary house and **cannot sit in front of the street-facing wall** of the house (so backyard or side yard, behind the front of the house)
- Electrical hookup is required: at least a **20-amp dedicated GFCI outlet** on the house or a service pedestal, with a single proper extension cord rated for the load. **Electrical permit needed** unless the outlet already exists (per the same Portland.gov page above)
- If the THOW has plumbing inside, you must connect to **potable water** and the **sanitary sewer** — and a **plumbing permit is required** for the sewer connection
- A THOW **cannot be used as a short-term rental** (so no Airbnb)
- A THOW does **not** count toward residential density on your lot, which is why you can have one in addition to (not instead of) the main house and any ADUs

If you're looking for a real second home, build an ADU. If you want a lower-commitment guest space or a place for a family member that you might tow somewhere else later, a THOW makes sense.

---

## What permits will you need, and in what order?

For a permanent ADU (the most common path), you'll typically need this stack of permits, all reviewed by **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)** — formerly the Bureau of Development Services or **BDS** (the city department that issues building permits and does inspections). Phone: **503-823-7300** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/contact).

1. **Zoning review** — done as part of the building permit application; checks PCC 33.205 / 33.110 compliance (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205)
2. **Building permit** under **Title 24** (Building Regulations, https://www.portland.gov/code/24) — covers the structural and life-safety work
3. **Plumbing permit** under **Title 25** (Plumbing Regulations, https://www.portland.gov/code/25) — for the new water and sewer lines
4. **Electrical permit** under **Title 26** (Electrical Regulations, https://www.portland.gov/code/26) — for the new electrical service
5. **Heating/Mechanical permit** under **Title 27** (Heating & Ventilating, https://www.portland.gov/code/27) — for the heat pump, ductwork, water heater venting, etc.
6. **Sewer connection permit** from the **Bureau of Environmental Services (BES)**, the city department that handles sewer and stormwater (503-823-7740, https://www.portland.gov/bes) — they verify there's enough sewer capacity for the second unit and issue a connection permit
7. **Water service permit** from the **Portland Water Bureau** (503-823-7770, https://www.portland.gov/water) — typically a separate water meter for the ADU, though a shared meter can sometimes work
8. **Driveway / curb cut permit** from the **Portland Bureau of Transportation (PBOT)**, the city department that handles streets, sidewalks, and driveways (503-823-5185, https://www.portland.gov/transportation) — only if you're adding or modifying a driveway. Most ADUs don't need new parking (PCC 33.205 says no extra parking required), so this often isn't needed.

For a THOW (vehicle path), you skip the building permit entirely and only get **electrical** and (if plumbed) **plumbing** permits.

The good news: **you submit most of these together as a single building-permit package online**, not seven separate trips. Portland calls this the residential permit application, and it routes through every relevant bureau in one go. Apply at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting.

---

## What's it going to cost?

Take everything below as a 2026 rough estimate. **Get exact numbers from the permit center; this is to help you plan, not to bind anyone.**

### One-time costs you pay the city ("soft costs")

- **Building permit fees + plan review:** typically **$3,000–$8,000** for an ADU, depending on size and complexity. The exact fee schedule is at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules.

- **System Development Charges (SDCs)** — these are one-time fees the city charges new dwelling units to pay for their share of water, sewer, parks, and transportation impact. Without any waiver, **expect roughly $20,000–$30,000** for a full ADU. The pieces (rates effective July 1, 2025 – June 30, 2026):
   - **Transportation SDC** — ADUs under 1,200 sq ft pay 50% of the full single-family rate (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting/tsdc); roughly $5,000–$8,000
   - **Water SDC** — paid to the Water Bureau when water service is set up (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules/system-development-charges)
   - **Sewer SDC** (BES) — based on plumbing fixtures
   - **Stormwater SDC** (BES) — based on impervious surface added
   - **Parks SDC**

- **Big-deal SDC waiver:** **You can waive ALL of those SDCs** by enrolling in Portland's **ADU SDC Waiver Program**. The catch: you sign a covenant promising no short-term rentals (no Airbnb, etc.) on the entire property — main house and ADU — for 10 years (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/adu-sdc-waiver). Apply through the **Revenue Division** at aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov. Violating the covenant means paying back **150% of current SDC rates**, so don't do it casually. **Heads-up:** Portland's broader **temporary residential SDC exemption** that's running Aug 15, 2025 – Sept 30, 2028 (ordinance 192082) **explicitly excludes ADUs** — ADUs use the older 10-year-covenant program (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules/housing-sdc-exemption).

- **Sewer connection** (line work): a few thousand dollars if there's already a stub; could be $10,000–$20,000+ if a brand-new lateral is needed. Your existing house is already connected to the sanitary sewer in SE 174th Ave (verified via Portland Maps), so you've already cleared the hardest part.

- **Water meter / connection:** ~$1,500–$5,000 for a separate ADU meter; less if sharing.

- **Architect / designer plans:** $5,000–$15,000 for stock plans modified for your site; $15,000–$30,000+ for fully custom. A few prefab ADU companies sell pre-permitted plans that drop a few thousand off this.

- **Survey** (recommended, sometimes required): $1,000–$2,500 for a residential lot.

### Construction itself

- **DIY-built tiny house ADU:** $50,000–$120,000 in materials (lots of sweat equity)
- **Hired-out small ADU (~400 sq ft):** $150,000–$220,000
- **Hired-out larger ADU (~720 sq ft, your max):** $200,000–$320,000

### Realistic total

For a typical hired-out 600–720 sq ft ADU on this lot, in 2026 dollars: **about $250,000–$400,000 all in**, with maybe $40,000–$60,000 of that being soft costs (permits, fees, plans, hookups, SDCs if you don't waive them) before you swing a single hammer.

For comparison, the total all-in cost of an ADU is **roughly the cost of a modest condo in Portland**, with the difference that you keep your land and rent the ADU out for $1,500–$2,500/month (typical for SE Portland in 2026).

### How long will it take?

- **Plans + early decisions:** 1–3 months
- **Permit review:** **Roughly 8–14 weeks** for a standard ADU permit at PP&D (faster if you use a pre-approved plan; longer if your design needs an Adjustment Review for setbacks, which adds 8–10 weeks per https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/land-use-review-fees-and-types/adjustment-reviews)
- **Construction:** 4–8 months for a typical ADU build
- **Inspections, certificate of occupancy:** a few weeks at the end

**Total: roughly 8–14 months from "let's do this" to "I can sleep there."** Think "one full year, give or take a season."

---

## What to do next, in order

This is the actual order I'd follow. Doing some of these out of order will cost you weeks or money.

### Step 1 — Verify everything I said about your property (15 minutes, FREE)

Go to **https://www.portlandmaps.com/** and search for **831 SE 174th Ave**. Click into "Zoning & Districts" and confirm the base zone is **R7** and the only overlay is **h**. Save or screenshot the zoning result. *Why first? Because if anything I told you doesn't match the live site, your design will be based on wrong info.*

### Step 2 — Book a free 15-minute appointment with a city planner (15 min appointment, FREE)

This is genuinely the best free resource the city offers. You ask the live planner your specific questions about *your specific lot*, and they answer in plain English. Book at **https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-appointments**. Bring your address, the type of project (ADU? THOW?), and any rough sketches you have. *Why early? Because they'll catch any property-specific gotcha before you spend money on plans.*

### Step 3 — Decide: ADU on foundation, or THOW? (a few days of family talking, FREE)

Use the table earlier in this document. The right answer depends on whether you want a real second home that adds long-term property value (ADU) or a flexible, removable, lower-commitment space (THOW).

### Step 4 — If ADU: get a survey and an idea of where it'll sit (1–3 weeks, $1,000–$2,500)

A licensed surveyor pins your property lines so the designer knows exactly where the 5-ft side and rear setbacks fall. Find one via the **Professional Land Surveyors of Oregon** at https://www.plso.org/. *Why now? Because a wrong-by-2-feet site plan can sink a permit and force a redesign.*

### Step 5 — If you might want to skip the impact fees, prep the SDC waiver paperwork early ($0, but locks in a 10-year no-short-term-rental covenant)

Get the covenant application from https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/adu-sdc-waiver and email questions to **aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov**. Filing this *with* the building permit (not later) avoids delays. *Why early? Because the covenant has to be reviewed and recorded against your property, and timing matters for the permit to issue.*

### Step 6 — Hire a designer or pick a pre-approved plan (1–3 months, $5,000–$30,000)

Pre-approved ADU plans are at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/accessory-dwelling-units. They're Portland-vetted and can shave weeks off review. Custom designers find at https://www.aiaoregon.org/. *Why now and not earlier? Because the survey and city conversation in Steps 2–4 inform the design.*

### Step 7 — Submit a single residential building permit application online (4 hours of paperwork, fees due at submission)

Portland's online permit portal at **https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting** routes a single application through PP&D, BES, Water, and PBOT. You'll upload plans, the survey, and the SDC waiver application if you're using it. *Why this order? Because the application triggers reviews from all the bureaus in parallel — there's no separate "first BES, then BDS" sequence anymore.*

### Step 8 — Respond to review comments (2–4 cycles, ~8–14 weeks total)

City reviewers will email back with corrections. Each response cycle takes a couple weeks. **Be responsive** — slow replies stretch the timeline more than the actual review.

### Step 9 — Get the permit, build, and inspect (4–8 months)

Once the permit is issued, you have 180 days to start work. Schedule inspections at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/inspections. Standard inspections for an ADU: foundation, framing, mechanical/electrical/plumbing rough-in, insulation, drywall, and final.

### Step 10 — Final inspection, certificate of occupancy, move in

Final inspection signs everything off. The 10-year SDC waiver covenant clock (if you used it) starts on this date.

---

## What to ask the city — actual scripts

When you call **PP&D General Inquiries at 503-823-7300**, here's exactly what to say:

> *"Hi, I'm a homeowner at 831 SE 174th Avenue, property ID R337949. I want to build a detached accessory dwelling unit in my backyard. My lot is zoned R7 with the h overlay. Can you confirm: (1) am I allowed one or two ADUs given my 8,276-square-foot lot, (2) does the Aircraft Landing overlay add any height or other restrictions for an ADU here, and (3) can you walk me through which permits I'll need and what the next step is?"*

To book the **free 15-minute planner appointment**, go to https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-appointments. When you meet:

> *"I want to add a small ADU — about 600 to 720 square feet — to my backyard at 831 SE 174th. Can we go over: setbacks, the maximum building footprint I can add given my existing 960-sq-ft house, whether the Aircraft Landing overlay affects anything in R7, and any sequencing things I should know before I hire a designer?"*

When you call **BES** (sewer) at **503-823-7740**:

> *"I'm planning to add an ADU at 831 SE 174th Avenue, property ID R337949. The existing house is connected to the sanitary sewer in SE 174th. Can you tell me whether I need a separate sewer lateral for the ADU, what the connection permit fee is in 2026, and whether I'll need a sewer capacity confirmation letter before I submit my building permit?"*

When you call **PBOT** at **503-823-5185** (only if your design needs a new driveway):

> *"I want to add an ADU at 831 SE 174th Avenue and the design includes a new driveway curb cut. What's the process and fee for a driveway permit, and do I need to apply before I submit my building permit?"*

When you email the **ADU SDC Waiver team** at **aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov**:

> *"Subject: ADU SDC Waiver — 831 SE 174th Ave, R337949. Hi, I'm planning to apply for the ADU SDC waiver covenant program when I submit my building permit. Can you confirm the current covenant document, the application timing relative to building permit submittal, and whether I should sign and email the covenant before or after the permit application is in review?"*

---

## What you might get wrong (the gotchas)

These are the mistakes Portland homeowners actually make on tiny house projects. Most cost weeks; a few cost serious money.

- **Assuming a THOW can be your only home on the lot.** It cannot. PCC 33.260 only allows an occupied RV (including a THOW) **alongside** a primary house, attached house, or manufactured home (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/260-occupied-rvs.pdf). Lots of people read tiny-house influencer content and assume the THOW *is* the home — in Portland, it isn't.

- **Skipping the SDC math.** People budget for construction and forget that **System Development Charges can add $20,000–$30,000** in one-time fees if they don't apply for the waiver. The covenant is essentially free if you weren't planning to short-term-rent anyway (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/adu-sdc-waiver).

- **Forgetting the 10-year STR covenant is a property covenant, not a personal one.** It runs with the land. If you sell the property within 10 years, the next owner is also bound. Check https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/adu-sdc-waiver before signing.

- **Putting the ADU in the wrong place.** It must be **at least 40 ft from the front lot line OR behind the rear wall of the existing house** (PCC 33.205, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205). People sketch a side-yard ADU and don't realize that it has to be behind the front of the existing house unless it's deep into the lot.

- **Missing the "ADU can't exceed 75% of main house living area" limit.** Your 960-sq-ft house caps your ADU at **720 sq ft**, not 800. Many people anchor on the 800-sq-ft figure they read online and design too big.

- **Designing an ADU over 15 ft tall and not realizing the matching-exterior rules kick in.** Above 15 ft, the ADU must visually match the existing house — siding, roof pitch, trim, window orientation — OR meet a defined alternative (PCC 33.110.245.C.4, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf). Stay at 15 ft or under and that whole rulebook drops away.

- **Forgetting the existing house has to remain compliant.** If you build the ADU but its placement makes the existing house's required outdoor area (250 sq ft minimum) disappear, the project gets flagged. Both buildings need to coexist legally.

- **Assuming "R7" means you can subdivide.** R7 minimum lot size is 7,000 sq ft. Your lot at 8,276 sq ft can't be split into two R7 lots without a land-use review and density work. An ADU is a much simpler path than trying to subdivide.

- **Thinking the Aircraft Landing overlay limits your ADU height.** It doesn't, in residential zones. But mention it to your designer anyway so they can put the right note on the plans.

- **Starting work before the permit is fully issued.** Pre-permit work can void your SDC waiver and trigger penalties. Wait for the permit to be in "Issued" status, not "Approved to Issue."

- **Not budgeting time to respond to plan-review corrections.** This is where most projects bleed weeks. Set a calendar reminder to check your permit status every Friday.

- **Hiring a designer who doesn't know Portland.** ADU rules in Portland change often (the 2025 update consolidated the two-ADU rules, for example, per https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205). A general residential designer from outside the city will miss things like the 40-ft front setback or the Adjustment Review process.

- **Forgetting Title 11 tree protection.** Existing trees on your lot can constrain where the ADU goes. Check Title 11.50 at https://www.portland.gov/code/11. Removing a tree without a permit can carry fines.

- **Missing the state-level building code.** Oregon's residential building rules — the **Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC)** — apply on top of Portland's zoning rules. The 2023 ORSC (based on IRC 2021) is the one in effect right now (https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/codes-stand/pages/orsc-adoption.aspx). Note: Oregon's old **Small Home Specialty Code** for homes under 400 sq ft (HB 2423, 2019) **sunsetted on January 2, 2026**. The state is in transition on small-home standards, with a new 2026 ORSC anticipated October 2026. **For now, build to the standard 2023 ORSC.** If you're determined to build under 400 sq ft, ask the city planner specifically about current small-home provisions, because this area is changing.

---

## Quick reference: who does what

| Bureau | What they handle | Phone | Website |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PP&D** (Portland Permitting & Development; this absorbed BDS, the old Bureau of Development Services) | Building permits, zoning review, plan review, inspections | 503-823-7300 | https://www.portland.gov/ppd |
| **BES** (Bureau of Environmental Services) | Sewer connection permits, stormwater, sewer SDCs | 503-823-7740 | https://www.portland.gov/bes |
| **Water Bureau** | Water service connection, water meters, water SDCs | 503-823-7770 | https://www.portland.gov/water |
| **PBOT** (Portland Bureau of Transportation) | Driveways, curb cuts, sidewalks, transportation SDCs | 503-823-5185 | https://www.portland.gov/transportation |
| **Revenue Division** (handles the ADU SDC waiver covenant) | ADU SDC waiver covenants | aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov | https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/adu-sdc-waiver |
| **Urban Forestry** (under PP&D) | Tree permits and protection (Title 11) | 503-823-8733 | https://www.portland.gov/trees |
| **PF&R** (Portland Fire & Rescue) | Fire safety review (only for larger projects, not typical for ADUs) | 503-823-3700 | https://www.portland.gov/fire |
| **Oregon BCD** (Building Codes Division — state level, not city) | Oregon Residential Specialty Code, Small Home Specialty Code transition | 503-378-4133 | https://www.oregon.gov/bcd |

---

## Friendly accuracy caveats from someone who isn't actually a planner

A few honest acknowledgments before you act on any of this:

- **Zoning data online lags reality.** I pulled your zoning from PortlandMaps on May 6, 2026, and the 15-minute planner appointment is the only way to get an authoritative read on your specific lot. Zoning rules also get amended (the ADU code at PCC 33.205 was updated in 2025 — see https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205 for the current version).

- **Code citations move sometimes.** I gave you exact PCC sections and current URLs, but Portland reorganizes its code occasionally. If a link 404s, go to the parent page (e.g., https://www.portland.gov/code/33) and navigate down.

- **"Roughly $X" numbers are stale within months.** SDCs adjust every July 1; permit fees adjust periodically. Treat all dollar figures here as planning estimates, then call the bureaus for the actual current number when you're ready.

- **I am not your lawyer or building official.** If your project is unusual (a corner lot, a flag lot, an existing nonconforming setback, an easement crossing your backyard), that changes things in ways a general guide can't capture. Use the 15-minute planner consult.

- **Permit timelines are typical, not guaranteed.** Expect surprises. Keep a buffer of a couple of months in your planning.

---

## Glossary — every acronym and technical term used here

- **ADU (Accessory Dwelling Unit)** — a smaller second home on the same lot as a main house. Has its own kitchen, bathroom, sleeping area, and entrance. Can be attached to the main house or detached.

- **BCD (Building Codes Division)** — the State of Oregon agency that writes the building, plumbing, electrical, and mechanical codes used statewide. It writes the **ORSC** (see below).

- **BDS (Bureau of Development Services)** — the old name of Portland's permit and inspection bureau. It was renamed **PP&D** in 2025.

- **BES (Bureau of Environmental Services)** — Portland's sewer and stormwater bureau.

- **Building coverage** — the amount of your lot covered by buildings, measured by their footprints. Different from "lot coverage" which sometimes includes pavement.

- **Covenant** — a legal promise recorded against your property's title that runs with the land (so future owners are also bound).

- **FAR (Floor Area Ratio)** — the total floor area of all buildings on a lot, divided by the lot size. A 1,000-sq-ft house on a 5,000-sq-ft lot is FAR 0.2:1.

- **FEMA SFHA (Special Flood Hazard Area)** — federally designated flood-prone areas. Your lot is **not** in one, per Portland Maps.

- **Footprint** — the outline of a building as seen from above, measured in square feet.

- **Lot coverage** — see "building coverage." Sometimes used to also include patios and pavement.

- **Overlay zone** — extra rules layered on top of the base zone (e.g., "R7h" means R7 base zone with the "h" Aircraft Landing overlay).

- **PCC (Portland City Code)** — Portland's official municipal code. Section numbers like "PCC 33.205.040" mean Title 33, Chapter 205, Subsection 040.

- **PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation)** — Portland's streets, sidewalks, and driveways bureau.

- **PF&R (Portland Fire & Rescue)** — Portland's fire bureau.

- **PHB (Portland Housing Bureau)** — runs separate affordable-housing programs, including a different SDC exemption for affordable units.

- **PP&D (Portland Permitting & Development)** — the city's permitting and inspection bureau. Replaces the old **BDS**.

- **R7 (Residential 7,000)** — a single-family zoning category requiring a minimum 7,000 sq ft lot.

- **ORSC (Oregon Residential Specialty Code)** — the state's residential building code. Current version is the 2023 ORSC (based on the 2021 IRC).

- **ROW (Right-of-way)** — the public strip including the street, sidewalk, and the area between them. PBOT controls this.

- **SDC (System Development Charge)** — one-time city impact fees on new dwellings, used to pay for water, sewer, parks, and transportation infrastructure.

- **Setback** — the required minimum distance from a property line to a building. R7 front: 15 ft, side and rear: 5 ft.

- **THOW (Tiny House on Wheels)** — a tiny dwelling built on a trailer with wheels, treated by Portland as an occupied recreational vehicle (PCC 33.260), not as a house.

- **Title 24 / 25 / 26 / 27** — chapters of Portland City Code covering building, plumbing, electrical, and heating/ventilating regulations respectively.

---

*Sources verified May 6, 2026. Property data via PortlandMaps API for property R337949. Zoning per PCC Title 33 as published at https://www.portland.gov/code/33. Always confirm with PP&D before relying on this document for permit decisions.*

tiny-house.enrichedQuestion:
I want to know whether I can build a tiny house on my property at **831 SE 174th Ave, Portland, OR 97233**, and what the full set of requirements would be to actually do it legally. Please answer as if you're a knowledgeable friend walking me through this — I'm a regular Portland homeowner with no background in zoning, permitting, or building codes.

Here's what I'm trying to figure out, in plain terms:

- Can I put a tiny house on this specific lot at all? Is it allowed as a primary home, as a second small home in the backyard (an **ADU — Accessory Dwelling Unit**, meaning a smaller second home on the same lot as a main house), or only as a temporary/recreational structure?
- Does it matter whether the tiny house is on a permanent foundation versus on wheels (a **THOW — Tiny House on Wheels**)? How does the city treat each one differently?
- What's the maximum and minimum size allowed? Are there rules about how tall it can be, how far it has to sit from my property lines and from other buildings, and how much of my yard it can cover?
- What zoning rules apply to my specific address? Please look up the actual zoning for **831 SE 174th Ave** (this is in the Hazelwood / Centennial area of outer East Portland) and tell me about any overlays — like flood, environmental, historic, or design overlays — that might affect what I can build. If you can't verify the exact zoning, please say so and tell me how to look it up myself on PortlandMaps.
- What permits will I need, and in what order? (Building permit, plumbing, electrical, mechanical, sewer connection, driveway/curb cut if needed, etc.)
- What Portland City Code Titles cover this — Title 33 (Zoning), Title 24 (Building), Title 25 (Plumbing), Title 26 (Electrical), Title 27 (Heating/Ventilating), and any others — and what specifically does each one require for a tiny house?
- Roughly what does this cost end-to-end (permit fees, **SDCs — System Development Charges, which are one-time fees the city charges for new homes to pay for water, sewer, parks, and transportation impact**, plan review, inspections), and how long does the process usually take from "I want to do this" to "I can move in"?
- Who do I actually call or email, at which bureau, and when in the process? Specifically, when should I talk to **BDS (the Bureau of Development Services — the city department that issues building permits and does inspections)**, **BES (Bureau of Environmental Services — handles sewer and stormwater)**, **PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation — handles streets, sidewalks, and driveways)**, **PP&D (Permitting & Development)**, and **Water Bureau**?
- What are the most common mistakes Portland homeowners make when they try to build a tiny house? I want to avoid the gotchas before I spend money.
- Are there sequencing issues — things I have to do in a specific order (e.g., land use review before building permit, sewer availability check before foundation work) — that could blow up my timeline if I do them in the wrong order?
- Are there any state-level rules I should know about, like the **ORSC (Oregon Residential Specialty Code)** or Oregon's tiny house appendix, that override or interact with Portland's rules?

**How I need the answer written — this part is non-negotiable:**

- Write for a Portland resident or small-business owner with no code or permitting background. Aim for an **8th-to-10th grade reading level**. Treat me as smart but completely unfamiliar with city processes.
- **Lead with a plain-English summary** that directly answers "Can I build a tiny house here, and what's the short version of what it takes?" in 3–5 sentences, in everyday language, before any tables, code sections, or citations.
- **Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it** — ADU, THOW, SDC, BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, PCC, ORSC, ROW, FAR, setback, easement, R5/R2.5/RF/etc. If you wouldn't use the word with a neighbor over the fence, define it.
- **Use everyday words first, then the code word in parentheses.** Say "how far the building has to sit from the property line (called a 'setback')" rather than dropping "setback" cold.
- **Keep paragraphs short.** Use bolded keywords inside sentences. Use a table only if you're comparing 3+ options on the same dimensions (e.g., tiny-house-on-foundation vs. ADU vs. THOW) — and put a one-sentence plain-English summary directly above any table.
- **Translate dollar figures and timelines into something I can plan around** — e.g., "about the price of a used car," "roughly one summer's worth of weekends," "a few months from application to approval."
- **Provide a numbered "What to do next" list, in the order I should actually do it.** For each step, tell me:
  - Who to call or what website to visit (with the actual phone number and full URL)
  - What to bring or have ready
  - Roughly what it costs (mark **FREE** if free)
  - Roughly how long it takes
- **Provide a "What to ask the city" script** — the literal sentences I can read aloud on the phone or paste into an email to BDS / BES / PBOT to get the answer I need. Not "call BDS" — the actual words to say.
- **Inline citations for every factual claim, in the body of the sentence itself.** Every rule, number, fee, timeline, setback distance, height limit, or contact must be followed *in the same sentence* by:
  - the exact code section (e.g., `PCC 33.205.030.B.2`, not just "Title 33"), **AND**
  - a direct full working URL to the authoritative source on portland.gov (e.g., `https://www.portland.gov/code/33/205/030`), the specific Administrative Rule PDF, the PortlandMaps page, or the bureau's specific page — full `https://...` URLs, not "[here]" or "the city's website."
  - Format example: *"The tiny house has to sit at least 5 feet from the side property line (PCC 33.110.220.B, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/220)."*
  - I will copy this entire answer and paste it into a Google Doc, so **every citation must survive plain-text copy-paste.** Do **not** use footnote numbers like `[1]`, hover tooltips, "see source above," or a citations-only-at-the-bottom format. If a claim cannot be tied to a specific section + URL, say so explicitly ("I could not find a specific code section for this — verify with BDS at 503-823-7300") rather than citing vaguely.
- A **"What you might get wrong / common gotchas"** section in plain language, listing the specific mistakes Portland homeowners make on tiny house projects (e.g., assuming a THOW is legal as a permanent home, missing the sewer connection requirement, forgetting SDCs, building before checking overlays).
- **Friendly accuracy caveats**, framed as warnings from a friend, not legal disclaimers — for example: "Zoning for your exact address can be different from what's on this block, so please check PortlandMaps at https://www.portlandmaps.com before you spend money on plans."
- A **glossary at the end** defining every acronym and technical term used more than once, in case I jumped to a section.

**Things to avoid:**
- Don't lead with a property data table — lead with the answer in plain words.
- Don't stack code sections without explaining each one in plain English right next to it.
- Don't assume I know the difference between BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, Urban Forestry, or PF&R — say what each one does the first time you mention it.
- Don't use words like "shall," "promulgated," "thereunder," or other legalese. Write the way a friendly city employee would explain it over coffee.
- Don't use vague citations like "Portland City Code," "Title 33," "the BDS website," "city regulations," `[1]`, or "see source above." Every citation must be a **specific section number AND a full URL, inline, in the same sentence as the claim.**
- Don't put all citations in a "Sources" list at the bottom and leave the body uncited. Inline citations are required even if a sources list also appears.

Please answer with all of that in mind.

The corner-lot detail is the biggest non-obvious piece, so the diagram will lead with that.Here's the picture, with the important caveats and the practical takeaways:

**Heads up on the zoning.** I couldn't reach PortlandMaps directly from this session (the API surface is blocked), so I'm working from web sources. Inner SE Belmont near 52nd is essentially all R5, but please confirm before spending design dollars: open [portlandmaps.com](https://www.portlandmaps.com/), search the address, and look at the **Property** panel for the Base Zone and any Overlay codes (a one-character code like `c`, `p`, `d`, or `h` would change the rules). The ADU rules below assume **R5 with no overlays**.

**Setback numbers (R5, from Title 33.110.220, Table 110-4):**
- Front: 10 ft
- Side: 5 ft (each)
- Rear: 5 ft

**ADU-specific rules layered on top (Title 33.205, plus the Detached Covered Accessory Structures Worksheet):**
- A detached ADU must either sit behind the rear wall of the main house, or be at least 40 ft from the front lot line.
- Maximum size: 800 sq ft (since 75% of your 2,156 sq ft house = ~1,617, the 800 cap binds).
- Maximum height: 20 ft if it's outside the setbacks, 15 ft if any part sits inside a side or rear setback.
- Detached accessory structures together can't cover more than 20% of the lot, and the ADU's footprint can't exceed the main house's footprint.
- No additional parking required.

**The corner-lot wrinkle (this is the part most people miss).** On a corner lot, the *front lot line* is the shorter of the two street frontages, regardless of where your front door is. The other street frontage is treated as a side street. So:
- If 52nd Pl is your shorter street, 52nd Pl is your "front" for zoning, even if the house addresses to Belmont.
- That changes which wall counts as the "rear wall" for ADU placement (it's the wall furthest from your front lot line).
- And if you ever want to use the *setback exception* to push an accessory structure into a side or rear setback, the worksheet adds a corner-lot-only condition: the structure must be at least 20 ft from the side street lot line. That can quietly eliminate a chunk of buildable area on a corner.

Pull a tape measure on both street frontages; whichever is shorter is your "front."

**Three things that go above the question you asked:**

1. **Your existing detached garage is probably the highest-leverage path.** Garage-to-ADU conversion in Portland is permitted with a building permit, and an *existing* structure that doesn't currently meet setbacks is generally allowed to stay where it is for the conversion (you'd just need to meet the ADU-specific standards: 800 sq ft cap, height limits, exterior standards if over 15 ft, plus building/energy code for habitability). That sidesteps the setback question almost entirely. The "oversized one-car garage" mentioned in the listing is exactly the kind of structure people convert.

2. **A basement ADU dodges the size cap entirely.** If your basement is part of a primary structure that's at least five years old (yours is, by a wide margin), a basement ADU is *exempt* from the 800 sq ft / 75% rule. The Redfin description mentioned an unfinished basement with "amazing ceiling height" — that's potentially the easiest, cheapest, fewest-setback-questions path of all, since basement ADUs have no exterior placement issues.

3. **Free pre-approved plans.** Portland publishes four free pre-approved detached-ADU plan sets (gable/shed roof × slab/crawlspace combinations) at the [ADU Permits page](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/accessory-dwelling-units). They're not right for every lot, but they speed up review noticeably if one fits. Worth a look before commissioning custom plans.

**My suggested next step,** given the userPreferences "double-check before recommending": confirm zoning on PortlandMaps, then book a free 15-minute appointment with a city planner (503-823-7300, or [ppd/early-assistance](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance)). Bring the lot dimensions, the existing garage footprint, and a question about which path (garage conversion / basement ADU / new detached) makes most sense given your specific corner-lot orientation. They'll give you a definitive answer in 15 minutes that no document can.

col2.response:
Great property question. Setbacks are only part of the ADU picture; let me pull your actual property record from Portland Maps and check zoning, lot constraints, overlays, and anything else that could affect what you can build. The "behind the question" answer is: *what ADU can I actually fit, and what could trip me up?* I'll work toward that.Let me verify current ADU rules with an authoritative source — Portland recently overhauled its accessory dwelling regulations and I want to be sure I'm citing the live code, not stale info.Let me confirm exact R5 setback numbers from the official table.I want to draw this out — your lot's geometry is the real story here. Let me set up a diagram.Here's the short version: in the R5 zone, the front setback is 10 ft, side and rear are 5 ft each — but for a **detached ADU** specifically there's a special rule: it has to be either at least 40 ft from the front lot line, *or* located behind the rear wall of the main house. That second condition is the one that matters on your lot, and it's worth showing why.## The setback rules, exactly

**R5 base zone** (33.110.220, Table 110-4):
- Front building: 10 ft
- Side: 5 ft
- Rear: 5 ft

**Detached ADU layer on top of that** (33.205):
- Must be at least **40 ft from the front lot line** *or* **behind the rear wall of the main house** — whichever you can satisfy
- Side/rear setbacks: 5 ft (matches base zone)
- Inside required setbacks (closer than 5 ft): height capped at 15 ft, exterior walls at 10 ft
- Outside setbacks: max height 20 ft
- Floor area: max 800 sq ft *or* 75% of the primary house, whichever is less. Your house is 2,156 sq ft, so 75% would be 1,617 — meaning **800 sq ft is your binding cap**
- Footprint can't exceed the primary house's footprint
- All accessory structures combined ≤ 20% of site area (≈ 1,067 sq ft on your ~5,335 sq ft lot)

**Attached ADU or interior conversion**: just follows the base R5 setbacks (10/5/5) — no special 40-ft rule.

## The thing actually worth knowing for your lot

Your parcel is **~50 ft deep × ~107 ft wide** (the legal description "N 1/2 of Lot 1 & 2, Karlinda Terrace" — north half of two lots, fronting Belmont). That depth is what makes the 40-ft rule basically moot, so a detached ADU lives or dies by **where the rear wall of your existing house actually sits**. If the house rear wall is ~25–30 ft south of the front lot line, you've got 15–20 ft of yard to work with before hitting the 5-ft rear setback. Tight, but a small one-story detached ADU (e.g., 18 × 24 = 432 sq ft, or up to ~28 × 28 if you can squeeze it) is plausible. If the rear wall is closer to the front, even better. If it's deeper, detached gets hard fast.

A few other things worth flagging that go beyond setbacks:

- **No overlay zone problems.** I checked — no environmental overlay, no historic/conservation district, no heritage tree, no nearby right-of-way or stream. Mt. Tabor has historic pockets but yours isn't in one. That removes a whole class of headaches.
- **You're already wired for it.** Your 2023 200 A panel upgrade and 2024 EV charger install mean your service has plenty of capacity to add an ADU subpanel without another utility upgrade. That's a real budget item you've quietly already paid.
- **You probably qualify for an SDC waiver** — Portland waives System Development Charges (~$15–20K depending on size) if you record a 10-year covenant agreeing not to use the ADU as a short-term rental. Worth knowing before you decide on the use case, because the covenant runs with the property.
- **Don't sleep on the alternatives.** R5 lots in Portland post-RIP can host a duplex, triplex, or even attached ADU + interior ADU combinations. If your goal is rental income or aging-in-place, the optimal play might not be detached. A basement conversion of a 1957 house is often the cheapest path per square foot if the foundation cooperates.
- **One verification step before you commit to a design**: pull a recent survey or sketch the actual house footprint, because the rear-wall placement is the binding constraint and Portland Maps doesn't publish that. You can ballpark from satellite imagery, but if you go past schematic design you'll want a real measured site plan.

If you want, I can sketch out what a feasible detached ADU envelope looks like at a couple of plausible house positions, or walk through the SDC waiver tradeoff, or compare the detached-ADU-vs-interior-ADU economics on a 1957 SE Portland house. Any of those useful?

ADU Planning Guide: 5112 SE Belmont St
Property: 5112 SE Belmont St, Portland, OR 97215 Tax Property ID: R194819 | State ID: 1S2E06BA 17100 Prepared: May 5, 2026

How to read this guide. Plain-language explanation comes first; the code citation follows in parentheses so you can verify every claim. I pulled property-specific data directly from the City of Portland's PortlandMaps API for this address; I'll flag clearly where address-specific data wasn't available and where you should verify yourself.


At-a-glance: your property snapshot (verified from PortlandMaps, May 2026)
