Not your problem directly, but flags that historical commercial uses existed nearby; relevant only if you ever excavate near a shared property line.


Verify yourself: go to https://www.portlandmaps.com/, enter the address, expand "Permits & Zoning" → "Zoning & Districts", and "Hazards." That's the same source I used. Free, authoritative, and what BDS plan reviewers look at.


1. Zoning and what's allowed on the lot
1a. Base zone: R5 (Residential 5,000)
Your lot is R5, which is Portland's most common low-density residential zone. R5 is one of six "single-dwelling zones" defined in Chapter 33.110 (full list at 33.110.020). Despite the "single-dwelling" name, R5 today allows quite a bit more than one house thanks to the Residential Infill Project (RIP1, effective Aug 2021) and follow-on amendments. In R5 you can build any of these (Table 110-2 in 33.110.200):

A house
An attached house (sharing a wall with a neighbor)
An ADU (governed by Chapter 33.205)
A duplex (corner lots automatically; other lots subject to 33.110.265.D)
A triplex (subject to 33.110.265.E)
A fourplex (subject to 33.110.265.E)
A multi-dwelling structure (subject to 33.110.265.F)
A manufactured home
A houseboat
A congregate housing facility
1b. Overlays: none apply
No "a", "d", "h", environmental, scenic, or plan-district overlays apply to this lot. I checked the PortlandMaps record directly. This is unusual luck for an SE Portland lot and saves you a lot of complication.
1c. Historic / conservation review
The lot is not in a Historic or Conservation District; the house is not a Historic Landmark, Contributing Resource, or Conservation Landmark. No design or historic review will be triggered (Chapters 33.445 and 33.846 don't apply).
1d. ADU rules: Chapter 33.205 applies
Yes, 33.205 applies. Here's what it says for your specific situation, an R5 lot with a house:

Number of ADUs allowed (33.205.020.B):

One ADU is allowed by right (33.205.020.B.1.a), as long as your lot complies with 33.110.202 "When Primary Structures are Allowed." Your lot does (it has an existing primary structure).
Two ADUs are allowed (33.205.020.B.2) because your lot meets the R5 minimum lot area of 3,000 sq ft in Table 205-2, has frontage on a maintained street (Belmont), and is not in the Constrained Sites "z" overlay. If you build two ADUs, at least one must be detached from the primary structure.

That second point is genuinely useful: the 5,344 sq ft lot is comfortably above the 3,000 sq ft floor for two ADUs. Whether two pencil out economically and physically (given lot coverage limits below) is a separate question, but the zoning permits it.

Maximum size (33.205.040.C.1):

The ADU's living area can be no more than 75% of the primary dwelling's living area or 800 sq ft, whichever is less.

Primary dwelling = 2,156 sq ft. 75% = 1,617 sq ft.
800 sq ft is less.
Your ADU is capped at 800 sq ft of living area.

Important exception: if you convert a basement (or any part of the primary dwelling) into an ADU and the primary dwelling has been on the site for at least 5 years, the 800 sq ft cap does not apply. Your house was built in 1957, so this exception is available. A basement conversion ADU could be larger than 800 sq ft.

Also new in 2025: the city has clarified that you can request an Adjustment Review (Chapter 33.805) to exceed 800 sq ft. Recently approved adjustments have allowed ~1,000 sq ft when design and lot-coverage criteria were met. Discretionary review takes ~8-10 weeks, no guarantee of approval, and there's an additional fee.

Maximum height: 20 ft for a detached or connected ADU (33.110.245.C.1). This is the same cap that applies to garages and other covered accessory structures. (The previous 18 ft cap was raised to 20 ft in 2021.)
1e. About short-term rentals
ADUs can be Type A short-term rentals (rental of bedrooms in the ADU; you live on-site) or Type B short-term rentals (rental of the whole ADU; specific standards in 33.815.040.B.1). See Chapter 33.207 Accessory Short-Term Rentals.

Critical interaction with SDC waiver: if you take the ADU System Development Charge waiver (which is the standard play; saves ~$12,000-$19,000), you sign a 10-year covenant prohibiting any structure on the property from being a short-term rental. Even renting a bedroom in the main house counts as a violation, with a penalty of 150% of the waived fees. Decide early whether you want STR flexibility or the SDC savings.


2. Setbacks: the core question
2a. R5 base zone setbacks (Table 110-4 in 33.110.220)
For the primary structure in R5:

Setback
Required minimum
Front building
10 ft
Side building
5 ft
Rear building
5 ft
Garage entrance
18 ft (the door, not the garage walls)


Maximum height for the primary structure: 30 ft (Table 110-4).
2b. ADU setbacks: this is where it gets specific
ADUs in R5 follow a layered set of rules. There are three rule layers stacked on top of each other:

Layer 1: ADU-specific siting rule (33.205.040.C.2). A detached or connected ADU must be either:

(a) Set back 40 ft from the front lot line, or
(b) Located behind the rear building wall of the primary dwelling (the wall furthest from the main entrance).

This is the "stay out of the front yard" rule. You can't put an ADU in front of or beside your house unless it satisfies (a) or (b).

Layer 2: base-zone covered accessory structure rules (33.110.245.C). Default ADU setbacks are the same as the primary-structure setbacks: 10 ft front / 5 ft side / 5 ft rear. Maximum height 20 ft.

Layer 3: the reduced-setback option for small accessory structures (33.110.245.C.2.b). R7, R5, and R2.5 zones permit you to build right up to (or close to) the side and rear lot lines if all of these are met:

The structure is at least 40 ft from a front lot line (and ≥20 ft from a side street lot line on a corner lot).
The footprint does not exceed 24 ft x 24 ft (576 sq ft footprint), excluding eaves.
If more than one structure is in the setback, combined length along each property line is ≤ 24 ft.
The structure is no more than 15 ft tall, and the walls are no more than 10 ft tall (excluding the gable).
The setback portion is screened from adjoining lots by a fence or landscaping (L3 or F2 standards in Chapter 33.248) unless walls fully enclose it.
No doors or windows on walls within the setback that face the adjacent lot line.
No rooftop deck.
Dormers set back ≥ 5 ft from the side and rear lot lines.

Plain-English summary for your lot: if you put a detached ADU behind the rear wall of the house AND the ADU is at least 40 ft from Belmont AND the footprint is ≤ 24'x24' AND height is ≤ 15 ft AND wall height ≤ 10 ft (with no windows facing the neighbor on those reduced-setback walls), you can effectively build with zero or near-zero side and rear setbacks. This is the standard "backyard cottage" recipe. If you go taller (15-20 ft, e.g., to fit a loft) you must use full base-zone setbacks (5 ft side, 5 ft rear) AND meet the design compatibility rules in 33.110.245.C.4 (matching exterior materials, roof pitch, trim, windows, eaves to the primary house).
2c. Setbacks: detached vs. attached vs. conversion
Detached ADU (its own building): rules in 2b above. The 40 ft front setback or "behind rear wall" rule applies (33.205.040.C.2). Reduced setback option in 33.110.245.C.2.b is available.
Attached ADU (sharing a wall with the house): the new combined structure must meet the primary-structure R5 setbacks (10/5/5/18 from Table 110-4). The 40 ft front / behind-rear-wall rule still applies (33.205.040.C.2 says "detached AND connected").
Conversion of basement / interior of house: no setback issues because no expansion of the building footprint. This is the simplest path. Basement conversions in a house ≥5 years old are also exempt from the 800 sq ft size cap (33.205.040.C.1).
Conversion of an existing detached structure (e.g., a 1957 garage, if there is one): the existing structure's nonconforming location is grandfathered, and a separate provision (33.110.245.C.2.c, the garage-conversion path) lets you reuse a pre-2005 garage in a setback as long as it's ≥ 40 ft from the front lot line and meets specific conditions. If you have a detached garage, this is often the cheapest ADU path because foundation, walls, and roof already exist.
2d. How taller ADUs interact with setbacks
ADU height
Setback regime
Design rules
≤ 15 ft and walls ≤ 10 ft
Reduced side/rear setbacks via 33.110.245.C.2.b
None of the matching-finish rules apply
> 15 ft, ≤ 20 ft
Full R5 setbacks: 10 ft front / 5 ft side / 5 ft rear
Match primary structure on materials, roof pitch, trim, windows, eaves (33.110.245.C.4)


In short: going from 15 ft to 16 ft of height costs you the reduced-setback option AND triggers design compatibility rules. This single distinction drives most ADU floor-plan decisions in Portland.
2e. Lot coverage and building coverage
Maximum building coverage for all covered structures combined (Table 110-5 in 33.110.225):

Lot size 5,000 sq ft to 20,000 sq ft: 2,250 sq ft + 15% of lot area over 5,000 sq ft.
Your ~5,344 sq ft lot: 2,250 + 15% × (5,344 - 5,000) = 2,250 + 51.6 ≈ 2,302 sq ft total combined building coverage.

Additional cap on accessory structures (33.110.245.C.3):

Combined coverage of all detached and connected accessory structures: no more than 15% of the lot area.
Your ~5,344 sq ft lot: 15% × 5,344 = ~801 sq ft for all accessory structures combined.
Also: an accessory structure's coverage cannot exceed the primary structure's coverage.

Why this matters in practice for your lot: if your house's footprint is, say, 1,500 sq ft (likely ballpark for a 2,156 sq ft 1.5-story 1957 house), you'd have roughly 800 sq ft of remaining building-coverage budget. That's enough for a single 24'x24' (576 sq ft) detached ADU footprint with margin to spare. It's tight but workable. You should measure your existing house footprint (or get it from Multnomah County assessor records or a survey) before assuming a specific ADU footprint will fit.
2f. Required outdoor area (33.110.240)
Your lot must preserve a contiguous outdoor area of at least 250 sq ft (10x10 ft minimum dimension, actually a 12x12 ft square must fit in it for R5; Table 110-4). It can't be in the front building setback. This is rarely the binding constraint, but worth checking when you're juggling a footprint, a deck, and an ADU on a small lot.


3. Related rules you probably didn't think to ask about
3a. Trees (Title 11, Chapter 11.50)
Title 11.50 governs trees during development. Important threshold: lots under 5,000 sq ft are exempt from tree preservation requirements. Your lot is ~5,344 sq ft, so the tree code likely applies to you, but it's close enough to the threshold that I'd flag this for verification by Urban Forestry.

What 11.50 means in practice:

A tree plan is required as part of your development permit (11.50.070).
All private trees ≥ 12 inches DBH (diameter at breast height) on your site, and all city trees ≥ 6 inches DBH, must be shown.
You must preserve at least 1/3 of non-exempt trees ≥ 12 inches DBH within the development impact area (11.50.040.C.1).
For trees you don't preserve, you pay into the Tree Planting and Preservation Fund (Table 50-1).
FY 2025-26 mitigation rate: $472 per DBH inch for trees ≥ 20 inches removed in development. A single 24-inch tree = ~$11,328 in fees. Multiple large trees = quickly $20,000-$50,000.
Trees ≥ 36 inches DBH require notice to the neighborhood association before removal.
Root Protection Zones during construction are roughly 1 ft of radius per inch of trunk diameter; you can't trench, grade, or build inside the RPZ without arborist oversight (11.60.030).

Action: walk the lot, measure every tree ≥ 6 inches DBH, including those overhanging from neighbors, and any street trees. Photograph them. You'll need this anyway for the tree plan, and large trees often dictate where the ADU can physically sit. If a tree must come out, factor mitigation into your budget early.
3b. Stormwater / sewer (Title 17, BES rules)
Your property is in the Stark combined sewer basin: sanitary and storm runoff go to the same pipe. New impervious surface (ADU roof, paths, driveway extensions) typically triggers on-site stormwater management.

Key references:

City of Portland Stormwater Management Manual (SWMM) is the operative document; it's updated periodically by BES. New construction generally must "manage" runoff through approved facilities (drywells, swales, rain gardens, stormwater planters, ecoroofs, etc.).
Title 17, Public Improvements, Chapter 17.32 (Public Sewer and Drainage System) governs sewer connections and rates.
Title 17.36 covers Sewer User Charges and connection fees.
System Development Charges (SDCs) are levied separately by sewer/water/transportation/parks bureaus (see section 3g).
Your lot is eligible for the Eastside CSO Clean River Rewards discount on the BES bill if you manage stormwater on-site.

Sewer lateral check: the site already has a sewer lateral (PortlandMaps shows it). The combined gravity main runs in Belmont (compkey 140255). Before adding fixtures, your contractor should ensure the existing lateral is in good condition and sized to carry one or two additional dwelling units. A sewer scope (camera inspection) costs $150-$300 and is cheap insurance. Replacing a failed lateral can run $5,000-$15,000+ depending on depth and length.
3c. Building (Title 24), plumbing (Title 25), electrical (Title 26), mechanical (Title 27)
Portland adopts the Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC) for one- and two-family dwellings, plus state plumbing, electrical, and mechanical codes. Title 24-27 are the local administration of these.

You will need:

Building permit (Title 24): structural, foundation, framing, insulation, weatherproofing, energy code compliance.
Plumbing permit (Title 25): fixtures, drains, water service, sewer connection.
Electrical permit (Title 26): service entrance, panel work, outlets, lighting.
Mechanical permit (Title 27): HVAC, hot water, gas, ventilation.
BES Connection Permit (sewer hookup; sometimes called a sewer connection permit) if you're adding a new connection or upsizing.

These can be combined: BDS calls a combined ADU package an "ADU project" and you submit one application covering all four trade permits plus the building permit. You can also use pre-approved ADU drawings (sometimes called "stock plans") for faster review.

Important for your lot's steep-slope flag: Title 24 may require a soils investigation report and geotechnical recommendations when grades exceed 10% with > 5 ft vertical change, especially if you're cutting into a hillside for foundations. Confirm at intake whether your foundation design needs a stamped geotech report. A geotech report runs $1,500-$4,000.
3d. FAR and density bonuses
R5 has FAR limits (Table 110-4):

1 dwelling unit: 0.5:1
2 dwelling units (including the ADU): 0.6:1
3+ dwelling units: 0.7:1
With "preserving existing dwelling unit" bonus: 0.7:1 for 2 units (33.110.210.D.2)

To qualify for the preservation bonus, your existing house must have received final inspection at least 5 years ago (it has; it was built in 1957) AND you must alter no more than 25% of the existing street-facing facade.

For your 5,344 sq ft lot:

0.5:1 FAR = ~2,672 sq ft total floor area allowed (1 unit)
0.6:1 FAR = ~3,206 sq ft (2 units, no bonus)
0.7:1 FAR with bonus = ~3,741 sq ft (2 units, preservation bonus)

Your 2,156 sq ft house plus an 800 sq ft ADU = 2,956 sq ft, comfortably under the 2-unit FAR with or without bonus. FAR is unlikely to be a binding constraint for a single ADU on this lot.

Density: 33.205.050 says ADUs are counted in minimum density calculations but not maximum density calculations. Your R5 lot would only trigger minimum density (2 dwelling units required) if the lot were ≥ 10,000 sq ft (33.110.205.B.2). Yours isn't, so this doesn't apply.
3e. Parking
No off-street parking is required for an ADU in Portland, anywhere in the city (Chapter 33.266 has been amended; minimum off-street parking for residential uses was eliminated). Existing required parking for the primary dwelling must be maintained or replaced on-site only if it was previously required by code, which is rare on older R5 lots.

In practical terms, you can pave or not pave a parking pad, build a driveway extension or not, and the city won't force the issue. PBOT does still require curb cuts to comply with transportation standards if you change the curb (Title 17 again).
3f. Short-term rental rules (Chapter 33.207)
Type A ASTR (host present, ≤ 5 bedrooms): renting up to 2 bedrooms in your ADU for less than 30 days is allowed with permit. Owner-occupancy of the primary dwelling is required.
Type B ASTR (host not present, or > Type A limits): subject to 33.815.040.B.1 standards.
All ASTRs require a separate ASTR permit and an Inspection.
SDC waiver = 10-year prohibition on ANY STR on the property. Strictly enforced; recorded covenant; 150% repayment penalty for violation.
3g. System Development Charges (SDCs) and the ADU SDC waiver
SDCs are one-time fees assessed at building-permit issuance to fund infrastructure capacity. Roughly four bureaus charge SDCs:

Water (PWB)
Sewer / stormwater (BES)
Transportation (PBOT)
Parks (PP&R)

Typical full SDC for a new ADU in Portland: ~$13,000-$22,000 combined, depending on fixture units, size, and current rates. Rates change annually each July 1.

ADU SDC Waiver Program: in place since 2010, extended indefinitely in 2018 with the STR-prohibition condition. If your ADU is owner-occupied or rented month-to-month-or-longer (no STRs), you can get the full SDC fees waived by signing a 10-year covenant. Apply through the Revenue Division (aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov). Application has a ~99% approval rate when instructions are followed.

New temporary SDC exemption (Aug 15, 2025 - Sept 30, 2028): Portland adopted a 3-year exemption for new dwelling units (single-family, duplex, triplex, fourplex, multifamily). ADUs are explicitly excluded from this temporary exemption (Chapter 17.14.070). For an ADU, you continue to use the existing ADU SDC waiver. So this is informational only; it doesn't change your path.

Also worth knowing: Portland has periodically offered up to $15,000 cash incentives for owners committing to long-term rental (no STR). This program changes annually; check the Portland Housing Bureau and Prosper Portland sites at the time you're ready to apply.


4. Permits and process
4a. Permit types you'll need
For a typical detached ADU:

Building permit (under it: structural, energy, fire, framing, insulation review).
Plumbing permit (sub-permit, often issued under the same project).
Electrical permit (sub-permit).
Mechanical permit (sub-permit).
BES connection permit (sewer hookup).
Water service permit (Portland Water Bureau) if you're upgrading or adding a meter.
PBOT public works permit if you're touching the curb, sidewalk, or right-of-way (e.g., new driveway curb cut).
Tree permit (Title 11) if any regulated tree is to be removed; otherwise, the tree plan is reviewed as part of the building permit.
Erosion control permit (Title 10) for sites that will disturb 500+ sq ft of soil during construction. Most ADU projects need this.
ADU SDC Waiver application (Revenue Division, separate from BDS).

For a basement/interior conversion: simpler. You skip exterior site work, BES connection (usually), and possibly PBOT and tree permits. Building, plumbing, electrical, and mechanical sub-permits still apply.
4b. What triggers what; what can be combined
BDS issues a combined "ADU project" application that bundles building + sub-permits.
Pre-approved ADU drawings dramatically speed up review (a few weeks instead of months).
Tree review is part of the building permit, not separate.
BES connection can be applied for in parallel with the building permit; typically issued before plumbing rough-in.
The SDC Waiver application is separate; submit it early in the permitting process, not after fees are assessed.
4c. Pre-application / Early Assistance
Strongly recommended for your situation. Portland offers two free or low-cost options:

Free 15-minute appointment with a city planner: book online, ask zoning-specific questions. Best for "is this allowed" type questions.
Early Assistance meeting: more substantive, typically 1 hour, costs a few hundred dollars (current fee on the BDS schedule), and you get written feedback you can rely on. Best when you have draft site plans.

Why I'd recommend it for you: the steep-slope flag, the lot near the tree-code threshold (~5,344 sq ft), the combined sewer basin, and the question of one vs. two ADUs all benefit from a preliminary conversation. An hour now saves weeks of rework later.
4d. Typical timeline
Approximate, generally accurate as of 2025-2026:

4e. Typical cost ranges (Portland, 2025-2026 ballparks)
These are estimates from third-party builders and city fee schedules; your specific project may vary substantially.

5. Sequenced action plan: what to do in what order
Here is a numbered checklist starting from "today." Bureaus are noted in bold.

Verify property data at https://www.portlandmaps.com/. Search your address. Confirm the zoning, overlays, hazards, and utilities match what's in this guide. This guide pulled the same data on May 5, 2026, but verify yourself before relying on any specific number.

Read the BDS ADU page and Detached Covered Accessory Structures Worksheet. Free PDFs; takes an hour. Source: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/adu-zoning. Bureau: Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D).

Walk the lot with a tape measure. Document: existing house footprint dimensions; distance from house to each property line; locations and trunk diameters (DBH = ~4.5 ft above grade) of every tree ≥ 6 inches; existing driveway, paved areas, and outbuildings; rough north arrow.

Scope the existing sewer lateral. Hire a plumber (~$150-$300) for a camera inspection. Get a written report. Bureau: Bureau of Environmental Services (BES) (for context); you're working with a private plumber here.

Decide the ADU type early: detached new build vs. attached addition vs. basement conversion vs. garage conversion (if you have a garage). This decision drives everything else. Typical economics: basement conversion cheapest, garage conversion next, detached new build most expensive but most flexible and resaleable.

Book a free 15-minute planning appointment with PP&D for zoning questions. https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/free-15-minute-appointments-permit-code-and. Bureau: PP&D zoning desk.

Optional but recommended: schedule an Early Assistance meeting with PP&D. ~$500 fee (verify current schedule). Bring a rough site plan. Get written feedback that you can rely on through permitting. Bureau: PP&D.

Hire a designer or builder. Get 2-3 bids from Portland-experienced ADU specialists. Look for someone who's done ADUs in your zone in the last 12 months. Ask for references and to see their permit-issuance time.

Decide on the SDC waiver. If you commit to no STR for 10 years, apply through the Revenue Division (aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov) early in the design phase. Saves $13K-$22K. Bureau: Revenue Division (separate from PP&D).

Schematic design + tree plan. Your designer produces a site plan showing setbacks, building footprint, parking, tree protection zones, stormwater management, and outdoor area.

If a regulated tree must be removed, weigh the mitigation fee against re-siting the ADU. Bureau: Urban Forestry (questions: 503-823-4489).

Construction drawings + structural calcs. If steep-slope-related geotech is required, commission a soils report at this stage.

Submit the building permit application through PP&D's online portal (Development Hub at https://www.portland.gov/ppd). Bundle the trade permits in one application. Pay intake fees. Bureau: PP&D.

Submit BES connection permit if a new sewer/storm connection is needed. Bureau: BES.

Erosion control permit if disturbing > 500 sq ft of soil. Bureau: PP&D (administered under Title 10).

PBOT public works permit if you're cutting a new driveway, modifying the sidewalk, or doing any work in the right-of-way. Bureau: PBOT.

Address plan review comments (typically 2-3 rounds; faster with pre-approved plans).

Permit issuance and pay fees / SDC waiver applied. Print permits and start work.

Construction with rough and final inspections at each trade phase. Schedule via PP&D's IVR/web system.

Final inspection and Certificate of Occupancy.

Address-numbering for the ADU (if separate mailing address): apply through PP&D after framing inspection.

Record the ADU SDC Waiver covenant (Multnomah County) if applicable; this happens automatically through the Revenue Division.

Update your homeowner's insurance to add the ADU as a covered structure and consider landlord liability if you'll rent it.

Register as a landlord with the City Revenue Division (Residential Rental Registration program) if you'll rent the ADU long-term.


6. Common gotchas in Portland (and what specifically to watch on your lot)
6a. General Portland gotchas
Front-yard placement. People assume "behind the house" means anywhere not in the front yard. The code is precise: the rear building wall is the wall furthest from the main entrance. If your house has an entrance on a side, your "rear wall" might not be where you think.
Setback line vs. building line. The reduced-setback option in 33.110.245.C.2.b is permissive but strict on every condition. Miss any one (windows facing the side neighbor, dormers within 5 ft, footprint > 24'x24', wall height > 10 ft) and you lose the entire reduced setback.
Height calculation on sloped lots. Height is measured from "average grade," not from the front of the building. On a sloped lot, average grade can be lower than you expect, which can push your ridgeline above the 20 ft cap. 33.930 has the measurement rules.
Tree code surprises. A neighbor's overhanging tree's RPZ extends onto your buildable area. You can be required to protect a tree you don't own.
Sewer lateral failures. Older Portland lateral sewers (cast iron, clay) are often near end-of-life. A failed lateral discovered during ADU plumbing rough-in can add weeks and $5K-$15K. Scope it before you design.
SDC waiver covenant scope. Renting a bedroom in your main house on Airbnb violates the covenant on the entire property. People miss this.
Stormwater on small lots. With a combined sewer basin (yours), BES is increasingly insisting on retention/infiltration even on small additions, not just discharge. Plan for a drywell or rain garden footprint.
Permit-package sequencing. People submit their building permit and then realize their ADU needs the SDC waiver AND a separate erosion control permit AND a tree review AND a BES connection. Bundle all of this from the start.
Energy code compliance (Oregon ORSC). The code requires increasingly tight envelope and HVAC performance. Designs from even 3-4 years ago may not pencil today without tweaks.
6b. Specific to SE Portland / older neighborhoods
Legacy lots. Karlinda Terrace plat (your subdivision's name in the legal description) was platted before modern lot-size standards. The "N 1/2 OF LOT 1&2" legal description means your lot is the north half of two original platted lots (likely 50' x 107' = 5,350 sq ft, consistent with my polygon estimate). Half-lots can complicate property line adjustments later if you ever wanted to split.
Nonconforming structures. Portland houses from the 1940s-1950s are often nonconforming to current setbacks (built before today's R5 standards). Check whether your existing house meets current 10/5/5 setbacks. If not, the "established building line" exception in 33.110.220.D.5 may help, but it's specific.
Underground storage tank (UST) legacy. PortlandMaps shows USTs on file at 4940 SE Belmont, 4914 SE Belmont, 5024 SE Belmont, 5300 SE Belmont, and 5016 SE Taylor (all within a few hundred feet). None on your lot. Mostly historic gas stations. Not your direct issue, but if you're excavating near a shared property line with one of these former-commercial corners, your excavator may flag it.
Steep slope flag. Even at 15% average slope, your lot is flagged as a steep-slope area. This is a Title 24 building-code consideration (potential geotech requirement) more than a Title 33 zoning consideration. Don't let a contractor wave it off; ask in writing whether a soils report is needed at intake.
Combined sewer basin. Stormwater management requirements are more stringent (and the on-site retention options sometimes more limited) in CSO basins. Conversely, Clean River Rewards offers an ongoing utility-bill discount if you manage stormwater on-site. If you're going to install a drywell or rain garden anyway, make sure your designer documents the credit application.
Glencoe Elementary is 145 ft away. Construction noise rules in Title 18 (Noise Control) restrict hours; school-adjacency may add weight to neighbor complaints. Plan deliveries and noisy work outside school hours.
Belmont street trees and overhead utilities. Verify that your construction-access plan doesn't damage either. Pruning of street trees requires a city permit.


7. Accuracy caveats and what to verify
Where this guide may diverge from your specific situation, and where to verify:

Item

I computed ~5,344 sq ft from the City's tax-lot polygon (Web Mercator projection). The actual area is the assessor's record or a survey.
Multnomah County Assessor's office or a survey. PortlandMaps shows the polygon.
Existing house footprint
Not in the API response. Estimate only.
Multnomah County assessor card; or measure it.

I noted Title 24 building-code triggers as "may apply"; I did not verify the specific elevation differential at your foundation locations.
PP&D plans examiner at intake; geotechnical engineer if foundation cuts are >5 ft.
Tree code applicability
Your lot is ~344 sq ft above the 5,000 sq ft Title 11.50 exemption threshold (assuming my polygon math is right). The official lot-size record at the assessor governs.
Multnomah County Assessor; Urban Forestry (503-823-4489).
Construction cost ranges

Permit fee schedules
I cited general ranges; rates change every July 1.
https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules

Recent and pending changes worth knowing
June 1, 2025 (Zoning Code Update Packet #214): consolidated the two-ADU allowance and the 40-ft-setback rule into a single table; clarified Adjustment Review is available for ADUs > 800 sq ft. The PDF I cited (effective 10/1/24) does not yet reflect the Packet #214 amendments. Check https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205 for the most recent PDF.
October 1, 2024 (Packet #211): various zoning-code cleanups affecting 33.205.
November 20, 2024: City Council removed the December 31, 2024 sunset on the Title 11 Large Tree Amendment, making higher mitigation fees for trees ≥ 20" DBH a permanent feature.
Aug 15, 2025 - Sept 30, 2028 (Title 17.14.070.J): Temporary residential SDC exemption. ADUs explicitly excluded. ADU SDC waiver continues to be the tool for ADUs.
Phase 3 Tree Code amendments are in development; future changes may rebalance preservation incentives. Watch https://www.portland.gov/trees for updates.

adu-setbacks.enrichedQuestion:
I want to know what the setback requirements are for building an Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) on my property at 5112 SE Belmont St, Portland, OR 97215. By "setback" I mean how far the new building has to sit from my property lines, my main house, the street, the alley, and anything else the city cares about. I'm a regular homeowner — I don't work in construction, planning, or permits — so please write this answer for me, not for a contractor.

## Maximum fence heights (Title 33.110.275 + BDS Brochure 3)

- **Front yard (within the front building setback)**: 3'6" (3.5 feet) maximum.
- **Side yard (within side building setbacks)**: up to 8 feet.
- **Rear yard (within rear building setbacks)**: up to 8 feet.
- **Outside any building setback** (i.e., the open middle of your lot): taller than 8 feet is allowed, but anything over 8 feet must be engineered by an Oregon-licensed engineer and permitted.

The height includes lattice, trellises, post caps, and anything else on top. If your fence sits on a retaining wall, height is measured from the higher side.

## Setback requirement

This is where most homeowners trip up; "setback" means two different things in this context:

1. **Fence-to-property-line setback**: there is none. You can build directly on the side and rear property lines. On the front, you can also build right on the front lot line; you just can't go above 3.5' there.
2. **The "front building setback" zone**: this is the strip between your front lot line and where your house is required to sit back from the street (typically 10 feet in R5, though averaging with neighbors can change it). The 3.5' height cap applies *anywhere inside that strip*, even on the side property lines within it. Once you're past that imaginary setback line going toward the back of the lot, you can jump up to 8 feet on the side fence.

So in practice on a typical R5 lot: the front ~10' of your side fences must also step down to 3.5'; behind that, they can rise to 8'.

## Things I'd flag specifically for your property

**NE 33rd is the big one.** It's a major arterial, and Portland's right-of-way on arterials often extends 5–10 feet behind the sidewalk. The actual front property line is almost never at the curb and frequently isn't even at the back of the sidewalk. Building a fence on what *looks* like your front line can easily put it in PBOT's right-of-way, which is a tear-it-out situation. Before you place a single post on the 33rd-facing side, call PBOT at 503-823-7002 (they'll help locate the right-of-way line for free) or pay a licensed surveyor to mark it.

**If you're on or near a corner**, vision clearance triangles apply at the intersection (Title 17). Inside that triangle, fences are typically capped much lower than 3.5' so drivers can see across. This is enforced separately from the zoning fence rules.

**Permits**: a wood fence at 7 feet or under is *exempt* from a building permit but still must comply with zoning (heights, setbacks, right-of-way). You need a permit for: wood >7', masonry/concrete >4', chain link/woven wire >8', any pool barrier, or any fence >8' (which also requires engineering).

**Materials gotcha**: any wood touching concrete or within 6" of the ground must be pressure-treated or naturally rot-resistant (cedar, redwood). This catches a lot of DIY rebuilds.

**Property line reality check**: Multnomah County's Office of Survey Records has plats but not exact line locations. The only way to know your true line is a survey by a licensed Oregon surveyor (typically $500–$1,500). Worth it before you build, especially if you'll share costs with a neighbor; a misplaced fence becomes a legal headache that long outlives the wood.

**Free 15-minute appointment**: Portland BDS offers a free planner consultation that will give you definitive answers for your specific address (book through portland.gov; in-person at 1900 SW 4th, Tues/Thurs).

If you tell me whether your front entrance faces NE 33rd (vs. a side street), and whether the lot is mid-block or corner, I can sharpen the front-setback number and flag any vision-clearance issues.

# Fence rules for 3220 NE 33rd Ave

## Confirmation: yes, I looked up your specific address

I pulled the actual zoning record for **3220 NE 33rd Ave** from PortlandMaps. One small surprise up front: your property is in the **Beaumont-Wilshire** neighborhood, not Alameda. Alameda is a few blocks west; the official neighborhood line runs roughly along NE 33rd Ave in places, so it's a very common mix-up. The zoning rules are the same either way (your zone is **R5**, the most common single-family zone in Portland), but I wanted to flag it so you don't get bounced if you ever say "I live in Alameda" to the city.

You can verify any of this yourself in about three minutes at **portlandmaps.com**: type your address into the search bar, click your house, and look at the "Zoning" panel on the right. You should see "R5" and one overlay code, "h" (more on that below; it doesn't affect fences).

Two other quick corrections before we get into it:

- The fence section of the zoning code you mentioned, **33.110.255**, was renumbered in 2020. The current section is **33.110.275**. (33.110.255 is now about flag lots, which doesn't apply to you.)
- The bureau you'll deal with used to be called **Bureau of Development Services (BDS)**. As of 2023 it was renamed to **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)**. A lot of older websites and brochures still say BDS; it's the same office, same phone number.

---

## 1. The short version (read just this if you're in a hurry)

In your **back yard and side yards**, you can build a fence up to **7 feet tall** with no permit needed; you can go up to **8 feet** with no permit if it's chain-link or woven wire, but anything taller than 7 feet in wood or solid material needs a building permit. In your **front yard** (the strip between your house and NE 33rd Ave, which is roughly the first 10 feet in from the sidewalk), the fence can only be **3 feet 6 inches tall**, no matter what it's made of. Fences must sit **entirely inside your property line**; they can't go on the strip of city-owned land between the sidewalk and the street. **Replacing a fence in the same spot at the same height doesn't need a permit**, and a typical 6-foot wood fence on the side and back of your lot is permit-free. Your property has no historic, conservation, or design overlay, so the standard rules apply cleanly.

---

## 2. The details, broken out by location

### Front yard (the part facing NE 33rd Ave)

Your "front yard" for fence purposes is the area between your front property line (which is roughly at the back edge of the sidewalk; more on that below) and an imaginary line **10 feet inside your property** running parallel to the street. That 10-foot strip is called the **front building setback**, the area where the city requires open space in front of your house.

Inside that front strip, **the fence can be at most 3 feet 6 inches tall (3 ft 6 in)**, period (PCC 33.110.275). That's a low picket fence, basically. The material doesn't matter; solid cedar, chain link, decorative iron, lattice, all are capped at 3'6". And remember, the **3'6" includes any topper**: a 3-foot picket fence with a 1-foot lattice on top counts as 4 feet, which is over the limit.

If you go past that 10-foot line and onto the part of the side or back of your yard that's actually behind the front of your house, you can go up to 7 or 8 feet (see below).

**Sight visibility at your driveway**: Even though the city doesn't have a hard sight-triangle rule for mid-block driveways like yours, you're still responsible for not blocking driver visibility (PCC 33.266.130). The practical rule of thumb is: if you're putting a 3'6" solid fence next to your driveway entrance, set it back a few feet from the curb cut so you can see oncoming bikes and pedestrians. NE 33rd Ave is a busy collector street, so this matters more on your block than on a quiet side street.

**Where the fence can sit in relation to the sidewalk**: Your fence has to be **entirely inside your property line, including the post footings**. The edge of the sidewalk is *not* your property line. There's almost always a strip of city-owned land between the sidewalk and your actual front yard (the **planting strip** or **right-of-way**); see the section near the bottom on this. You cannot build any fence in that strip without a special permit from PBOT, which is rarely granted for fences.

**Hedges count as fences when they're tall.** A solid hedge in your front yard taller than 3'6" is technically a code violation under the same fence rule, though enforcement is usually complaint-driven. Trees and shrubs that are clearly individual plantings, not a continuous wall, are governed by the tree code (Title 11), not the fence code.

**Retaining walls change the math.** If you build a fence on top of a retaining wall, the city measures fence height **from the higher side of the wall**, not the lower side. So a 2-foot retaining wall with a 4-foot fence on top counts as a 4-foot fence on the high side, but the wall itself plus the fence may total 6 feet on the low side, which can affect both the permit and what the neighbors see.

### Side yards (between your house and the neighbors on either side)

In R5, the **side building setback is 5 feet**, meaning the city requires a 5-foot strip of open space along each side of your house.

The fence rule splits the side yard into two parts:

- The part of the side yard that's **forward of the front of your house** (in line with the front of your house, between your house and the street): this is treated as part of the front setback, so the **3'6" cap** applies there.
- The part of the side yard that's **alongside or behind the front wall of your house**: you can go up to **7 feet (wood/solid) or 8 feet (chain-link or woven wire)** with no permit. Material matters here only for what triggers a permit, not for the height limit itself; a wood fence at 7'6" is allowed but needs a permit, while a chain-link fence at 7'6" is allowed without a permit (PCC 24.10.072.B).

Practical translation: if your house sits 15 feet back from the front property line, the first ~15 feet of fence on each side of your house can only be 3'6" tall. From there back to your rear property line, you can have your full 6- or 7-foot privacy fence.

### Back yard

Your back fence can be up to **7 feet tall** (wood or any solid material) with **no permit required**, or up to **8 feet** if it's chain-link or woven wire. Going taller than 7 feet in wood requires a building permit. Going taller than 8 feet in any material requires a permit *and* an engineer's design (PCC 24.10.072.B and Brochure 3).

**Alley question**: Many Beaumont-Wilshire blocks have alleys, but I couldn't confirm from the city's data whether your specific block does. Verify on PortlandMaps; if there's a thin gray strip behind your lot on the basemap, that's an alley. **If your back lot line abuts an alley, the rear setback for your house drops to zero** (PCC 33.110.220.D.9), and a fence can sit right on the lot line. You still can't build a fence *in* the alley itself; the alley is public right-of-way managed by PBOT. Watch for sight visibility at the alley exit; same logic as a driveway.

**Material differences again**: solid wood, vinyl, or concrete fences all share the 7-foot no-permit limit. Chain-link and woven wire get an extra foot (8 feet no-permit) because they're see-through. Lattice toppers count toward total height; if you want a 6-foot solid fence with a 1-foot lattice topper, that's a 7-foot fence, which is right at the no-permit limit. A 6-foot fence with a 2-foot lattice topper is an 8-foot fence and needs a permit.

### Quick comparison

Here's the same information in one table, since you're comparing three locations on the same set of dimensions. **In plain words: your front yard is locked at 3'6", and side and back can go to 7 or 8 feet depending on material, with retaining walls measured from the high side.**

| Location | Max height (no permit) | Max height (with permit) | Where the fence can sit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Front yard (within 10 ft of front property line) | 3 ft 6 in, any material | 3 ft 6 in is also the cap with a permit; you can't exceed it | Inside your property line, behind the planting strip |
| Side yard, forward of front of house | 3 ft 6 in (treated as front) | Same; can't exceed 3'6" | Inside your property line |
| Side yard, alongside or behind house | 7 ft (wood/solid), 8 ft (chain-link/wire) | Up to height of base zone, with engineering | Inside your property line, may sit on lot line |
| Back yard | 7 ft (wood/solid), 8 ft (chain-link/wire) | Up to 30 ft (zone max), with engineering and permits | Inside your property line; may sit on lot line; 0 ft setback if alley behind |

---

## 3. Zoning and overlays for your address

Your property is **R5 (Residential 5,000)**, which is Portland's standard single-family residential zone, named for the historical 5,000-square-foot minimum lot size. This is by far the most common zone in close-in northeast Portland and applies to most of Beaumont-Wilshire.

You have **one overlay**: the **"h" Aircraft Landing overlay** (your full zone code reads "R5h"). This is a height-restriction overlay tied to flight paths around PDX. It limits how tall buildings and structures can be to keep airspace clear. **It has zero practical effect on a residential fence**; the height ceiling under "h" is in the hundreds of feet here. I'm only mentioning it because you'll see it on PortlandMaps and it sounds dramatic.

You have **none** of the things that would change your fence rules:

- No **historic district** (no Alameda Park or Irvington-style historic protection)
- No **conservation district**
- No **design overlay**
- No **environmental overlay**
- No **plan district**
- No **National Register district**

So the standard R5 fence rules apply cleanly.

**How to verify yourself, in under 5 minutes**: Go to **portlandmaps.com**, type **"3220 NE 33rd Ave"** in the search bar, click your property on the map, and on the right side click "Zoning." You should see "R5" as the base, "h" as the overlay, and empty rows for historic, conservation, plan district, and design. If anything in those rows is filled in, the rules in section 4 still apply but additional rules may stack on top.

---

## 4. Which Portland City Code Titles govern this

**Title 33 — Planning and Zoning** is the main rulebook for fence height, where it can sit, and what counts as "fence" (lattice toppers, hedges that act as fences, etc.). The specific section for fences in single-family zones is **PCC 33.110.275**. The setback rules for buildings (which define the "front building setback" the fence rules reference) are at **PCC 33.110.220**.

**Title 24 — Building Regulations** is the rulebook for whether a fence needs an actual building permit, separate from zoning. The exemptions from needing a permit (for fences 7 feet and under in wood, 4 feet and under in masonry, or 8 feet and under in chain-link) are at **PCC 24.10.072.B**.

**Title 11 — Trees** is the rulebook that kicks in if a tree (yours or a city-owned street tree) is in the way of where you want to dig post holes. Most front-yard trees in Portland between the sidewalk and the street are city-owned street trees and protected by **PCC 11.50** and **11.60**. Damaging roots without an Urban Forestry permit can mean fines.

If you ever go above 8 feet of fence (or build a retaining wall over 4 feet with a fence on top), you also pull in the **Oregon Residential Specialty Code**, which is the state-level structural code Portland enforces.

---

## 5. Do you need a permit? Plain-language thresholds

Three different answers depending on what you're building:

**No permit needed if all of these are true:**

- The fence is 7 feet tall or less and made of wood, vinyl, or similar material; OR
- The fence is 8 feet tall or less and made of chain-link or woven wire; OR
- The fence is 4 feet tall or less and made of masonry or concrete

…AND it sits entirely on your property, AND it's not in your front building setback above 3'6", AND it doesn't enclose a swimming pool. This covers ~95% of residential fence projects in Portland, including a standard 6-foot cedar privacy fence.

**Building permit required if any of these are true (PCC 24.10.072.B):**

- The fence is taller than 7 feet in wood or solid material
- The fence is taller than 8 feet in any material (also requires an engineer's design)
- The fence is taller than 4 feet in masonry or concrete
- The fence encloses a swimming pool, hot tub, or spa (regardless of height)
- The fence sits on top of a retaining wall over 4 feet tall (the wall itself triggers a permit)
- The fence is electric or has barbed wire (these are heavily restricted in residential zones; barbed wire is essentially banned in single-dwelling zones except for agricultural use, and electric fences are not allowed for residential perimeter fencing)

**A privacy fence with a lattice topper**: lattice counts toward total height. A 6-foot fence + 1-foot lattice = 7 feet, no permit. A 6-foot fence + 2-foot lattice = 8 feet, permit needed.

**Replacing an existing fence in the same spot, same height, same material**: no permit needed, treated as maintenance. If you're going taller, moving the line, or changing the structure, the new fence has to meet current code.

**A fence over a retaining wall**: the city measures from the high side of the wall. A 6-foot fence on a 3-foot retaining wall is a 9-foot structure as the neighbors see it from the low side, but for code purposes it's a 6-foot fence (no permit) plus a 3-foot retaining wall (no permit, since under 4 feet). If the retaining wall is over 4 feet, it needs its own building permit.

---

## 6. What to do next, in order

**Step 1. Verify your zoning and property lines yourself (free, 5 minutes).**
Go to **portlandmaps.com**, search your address, click "Zoning," and confirm "R5" base + "h" overlay + no other overlays. While you're there, click "Property" and look at the lot lines on the basemap. **Have ready**: just your address. **Cost**: free. **Time**: 5 minutes.

**Step 2. Locate your actual property line, especially in front (free or paid, depending on how confident you want to be).**
The sidewalk is NOT your property line. PBOT can tell you for free where the **right-of-way line** is, which gives you a pretty good estimate of your property line in front (about a 5-minute call). **Contact**: Portland Bureau of Transportation (PBOT), 503-823-7002. **Have ready**: your address. **Cost**: free.
For a precise legal property line on all sides, hire a licensed surveyor. **Cost**: roughly $700–$1,200, about the cost of a decent appliance. **Time**: 1–3 weeks for the survey to get scheduled and completed. **This step is optional but cheap insurance** if your fence will sit close to a neighbor's line.

**Step 3. Schedule a free 15-minute appointment with a city planner (free, recommended).**
This is the single best thing you can do, and most homeowners don't know about it. A real planner will look up your property and answer specific questions before you spend a dollar on materials. **Contact**: Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D), book at portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments, or call 503-823-7300. **Have ready**: your address, a rough sketch of where you want the fence and how tall, the fence material. **Cost**: free. **Time**: 15-minute call, scheduled within 1–2 weeks.

**Step 4. (Only if your fence triggers a permit.) Apply online for the building permit.**
**Contact**: PP&D online permitting, portland.gov/ppd. **Have ready**: a site plan (a sketch of your lot showing where the fence goes, distances to property lines, and existing structures), a fence elevation drawing showing height, materials, post spacing, and footing depth. For a fence over 7 feet, you'll need a stamped engineer's drawing. **Cost**: typically $200–$400 for a residential fence permit, about the cost of a nice dinner out for two. **Time**: about 2–4 weeks for issuance for a simple fence.

**Step 5. Call before you dig (free, required by Oregon law).**
Before any post hole gets dug, you must call **Oregon 811** ("Call Before You Dig") at **811** or **503-246-6699**, or request online at digsafelyoregon.com. They'll mark underground gas, water, electrical, and cable lines for free within 2 business days. Skipping this and hitting a gas line is a serious problem; it's also free, so just do it. **Cost**: free. **Time**: one call (10 minutes), then 2 business days for marking.

**Step 6. (If digging near a street tree.) Clear it with Urban Forestry (free).**
Street trees in Portland are city-owned and root protection is real. If your fence post holes will be within about 10 feet of a street tree, call the **Tree Hotline** at **503-823-8733** before you dig. **Cost**: free. **Time**: one call.

**Step 7. Build the fence.** A pro contractor for a typical 6-foot, 100-foot-long cedar fence is roughly $4,000–$8,000 in Portland depending on grade, installed; that's about the cost of a solid used car. DIY the materials run $1,500–$3,000 plus a couple of weekends of labor; roughly two summer weekends for a fit and patient builder, three or four if you're learning as you go. Fence pros are heavily booked May through August in Portland; expect a 4–8 week wait for a contractor in summer, versus 2–3 weeks in late fall or winter.

**Step 8. Schedule the inspection (only if you pulled a permit).**
**Contact**: PP&D 24-hour inspection line, **503-823-7000**. You'll need your IVR/permit number. **Cost**: included in permit fee. **Time**: inspectors usually come within 1–3 business days of request.

---

## 7. Script for calling the city

Here's the actual sentence to read or paste in. Use this on the phone to PP&D General Inquiries (**503-823-7300**) or in your 15-minute appointment:

> "Hi, I own a home at **3220 NE 33rd Ave, 97212**, in the Beaumont-Wilshire neighborhood. PortlandMaps shows my zoning as R5 with the 'h' overlay. I'd like to build a **[6-foot cedar]** fence in my **[back and side yards]** and a **[3-foot picket]** fence in the **[front]**. Can you confirm: (1) the fence can sit on or just inside my property line on the side and back, (2) the front fence is capped at 3 feet 6 inches in the front 10 feet, (3) I don't need a building permit at these heights with these materials, and (4) there are no special overlay or plan district rules I'm missing? Also, can you tell me the width of the public right-of-way along NE 33rd Ave so I know where my front property line actually is?"

That covers it in one shot and prevents you from getting bounced between PP&D and PBOT. **If they say the right-of-way question is for PBOT, that's correct**; call **PBOT at 503-823-7002** for the right-of-way width specifically.

---

## 8. Common gotchas (the mistakes that actually cost Portland homeowners money)

**The sidewalk is not your property line.** This is the single biggest mistake. The city right-of-way usually extends from the curb back about 6 to 12 feet, which often means the sidewalk *and* a strip of grass on the house side of the sidewalk are public land. If you build right up to the back edge of the sidewalk, you may be 5+ feet inside the city's right-of-way, and PBOT can require you to tear it out. Always confirm with PBOT before building anywhere within ~12 feet of the curb.

**Hedges count as fences when they form a wall.** A solid laurel hedge in your front yard at 6 feet tall is a code violation, even though you didn't "build" anything. The city rarely enforces this proactively but a complaining neighbor can trigger a notice.

**Removing a "tree on your property" without checking who owns it.** Almost every tree between the sidewalk and the street is a **street tree**, owned by the city, and removing it without an Urban Forestry permit is a $1,000+ fine plus replanting cost. Even on the house side of the sidewalk, large trees may be regulated by Title 11.

**Damaging tree roots when digging post holes.** Hand-digging within the root zone of a street tree (roughly 10 feet from the trunk) requires Urban Forestry sign-off. Power-augering through tree roots can kill a 60-year-old tree, and you may be on the hook for a multi-thousand-dollar replacement.

**Assuming your neighbor has to chip in for a shared fence.** Portland is clear on this: shared fences are a private matter between neighbors. Your neighbor is not legally required to pay half, allow you onto their side to install, or even agree to the project. If your existing fence sits on the property line, you both technically share it; if you tear it down without their consent and they liked it, that's a civil dispute the city will not get involved in. Talk to them in writing before you do anything.

**Building tall fences too close to a driveway and creating a sight-line problem.** A 6-foot solid fence right at the curb cut on a busy street like NE 33rd makes it impossible to see oncoming bikes when you back out. The city's vision-clearance rules technically only kick in at street intersections, but you can get sued in a private case if a fence you built blocks a driver's view of a pedestrian. A common-sense practice: step the front fence section down to 3'6" for the last 5–10 feet before your driveway exit.

**Pouring footings that extend into the right-of-way or onto the neighbor's lot.** The footing is part of the fence; if your post is right on the line, the concrete blob below ground may extend across it. Set posts at least 6 inches inside the line.

**Forgetting that NE 33rd Ave is a major street.** Some city setback and visibility rules are stricter on collector and arterial streets than on quiet residential streets. NE 33rd is a "Neighborhood Collector" by classification. None of this changes the basic 3'6" front-fence rule, but it's worth mentioning to PP&D when you call.

**Building in summer permit-rush.** PP&D plan-review wait times stretch out from May through August. A simple fence permit that takes 2 weeks in February can take 4–6 weeks in July. If you don't need the permit (most fences don't), this isn't an issue; if you do, plan ahead.

---

## 9. Friendly accuracy caveats

A few things worth double-checking before you spend money:

- **Verify the zoning yourself on PortlandMaps before you commit.** I pulled it from the same database the city uses, and as of today your zone is R5 with the "h" overlay and nothing else, but zoning can change with code updates and you want to confirm on the day you start your project. Type your address into portlandmaps.com.
- **Don't trust me on the alley question.** I couldn't confirm whether your specific block has an alley behind it from the data I had. If your back yard backs onto another house's back yard, no alley; if there's a 12–16 foot strip of pavement behind the lot, that's an alley. PortlandMaps' aerial view will show this clearly.
- **Property lines are legal facts that only a survey can establish.** PBOT can give you the right-of-way width, which gets you a good estimate, but a property survey is the only way to know for sure where your line actually is. If your fence will sit within 12 inches of where you think the line is, get the survey.
- **Code numbers and fees change.** I cited the current section numbers as of today. The rules described here are stable (the current fence section was renumbered in 2020 but the underlying limits have been the same for years), but always confirm with PP&D before pulling a permit.
- **The 15-minute appointment is the safest answer to all of this.** A planner looking at your specific address will catch anything I missed.

---

## A note on the planting strip and the city's land in front of your house

The strip of land between the sidewalk and the street (or, on streets without curbs, the unimproved area between the sidewalk and your property line) is called the **planting strip** or **furnishing zone**, and the legal designation is **public right-of-way (ROW)**. It's owned by the city and managed by **PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation)**. You generally can't build any fence, wall, or permanent structure there without a special permit from PBOT, which is rarely issued for residential fences.

What you *can* do in the planting strip without a permit: maintain landscaping, plant approved street trees (with Urban Forestry permission), and do basic upkeep. What you *can't* do: build a fence, install permanent posts, pour concrete, or otherwise treat it like your yard.

To find out exactly how wide your right-of-way is along NE 33rd Ave, **call PBOT at 503-823-7002**. They'll look up your block and tell you the answer in about five minutes. This is free.

---

## A note on trees and Urban Forestry

If a tree is anywhere near your fence line, **Urban Forestry** is the part of Portland Parks & Recreation that has jurisdiction. The hotline is **503-823-8733**.

You'll want to call them if:

- You'd be digging within roughly 10 feet of any tree larger than ~6 inches in diameter, especially street trees
- You think the tree is in the way of your fence and might need to be pruned or removed
- You're not sure if a tree on your property is regulated under the city's tree code

Spring and early summer (April–June) is when Urban Forestry is busiest, with a ~2-week response window for non-emergency calls. Late fall is faster.

The general rule of thumb: never cut a root larger than 2 inches in diameter on a street tree without Urban Forestry's sign-off. Hand-dig (don't auger) within 5 feet of a street tree trunk.

---

## A note on shared fences with neighbors

Portland's position is that **the city does not get involved in shared-fence disputes**. The city enforces zoning (height, location, setback) and building code (structural safety); everything else is between you and your neighbor.

That means:

- Neither of you is legally obligated to share the cost of building or replacing a fence on your property line
- Neither of you can force the other to allow access to their side for installation or maintenance
- If a fence sits exactly on the property line, both households share ownership and either can act on it (which can get messy)
- Encroachment (a fence that's a few inches over the line) is a civil property issue, not a city issue

The friendly route: a written agreement before you build, even a one-page email confirming who pays what and where the fence sits.

---

## What a typical 6-foot back/side fence costs and takes in Portland

For a roughly **100 linear feet** (the side and back of a typical 5,000-square-foot lot like yours) **6-foot cedar privacy fence**:

- **Contractor-installed**: $4,000–$8,000 total, depending on whether you're getting basic or premium grade. That's about the cost of a decent used car. For just side and back of your lot (call it 100–120 feet), $5,000–$6,500 is a fair midrange Portland quote in 2026; significantly higher than that and you're paying for premium materials or an expensive contractor.
- **DIY materials only**: $1,500–$3,000. About 2–4 weekends of evenings and Saturdays for someone who's reasonably handy and patient. First-time fence builders should plan for 4 weekends.
- **Timeline if hiring**: book in late winter (February–March) for a spring install; expect 4–8 weeks of contractor wait time in May–August, 2–3 weeks November–February.
- **Cheapest month to build**: November through February, after the rainy ground softens for digging but before the spring rush. Wood prices also tend to be lower late-fall.
- **Most expensive month**: June–July, peak demand, contractors at capacity.

---

## 10. Glossary

**BDS / PP&D** — Bureau of Development Services, renamed in 2023 to Portland Permitting & Development. Same office, handles building permits and zoning questions. Phone: 503-823-7300.

**PBOT** — Portland Bureau of Transportation. Owns and manages streets, sidewalks, and the right-of-way (the city-owned strip between the sidewalk and the street). Phone for right-of-way questions: 503-823-7002.

**Urban Forestry** — A division of Portland Parks & Recreation that regulates trees on public property and on private property over a certain size. Phone: 503-823-8733.

**PCC** — Portland City Code. The city's complete rulebook, organized by Title (broad subject) and section (specific rule). "PCC 33.110.275" means Title 33, Chapter 110, Section 275.

**Setback** — How far inside your property line a structure has to sit. The "front building setback" is the strip of yard between your front property line and where your house can legally start; in R5 zones, it's 10 feet.

**Right-of-way (ROW)** — The strip of land owned by the city for streets, sidewalks, and utilities. It usually includes the sidewalk and a planting strip on the house side. You can't build permanent structures here without special permission.

**Planting strip** — Common name for the part of the right-of-way between the sidewalk and the street (or sometimes between the sidewalk and your front property line). City-owned; you maintain it but can't build on it.

**Lot line / property line** — The legal boundary of your property. Almost never the same as the edge of the sidewalk.

**Zoning overlay** — An extra layer of rules added on top of your base zone (in your case, R5). Your one overlay is "h" (Aircraft Landing), which has no effect on residential fences.

**Base zone** — Your primary zone designation, which determines most of the rules for what can be built. Yours is R5, single-family residential.

**Plan district / historic district / conservation district** — Special districts that overlay extra design rules on top of base zoning. Your property has none of these, which is why standard R5 rules apply cleanly.

**Vision clearance** — City rules requiring sight visibility at intersections and driveways. The hard rules apply at street corners; common-sense visibility applies at private driveways.

**Engineering / engineered design** — A drawing stamped by a structural engineer licensed in Oregon. Required for any fence taller than 8 feet and for some unusual designs (heavy masonry, fences on retaining walls). Adds $300–$800 to a project.

---

If you want me to draft your site plan sketch (the simple diagram PP&D wants if you do end up needing a permit), I can walk you through that next; it's basically a top-down drawing of your lot with the fence line, distances to property lines, and the house labeled.

fence-height.enrichedQuestion:
I live at 3220 NE 33rd Ave, Portland, OR 97212, and I want to know the maximum fence height allowed on my property — front yard, back yard, and side yard — and whether there's any setback requirement (how far the fence has to sit from my property line, the sidewalk, or the street). I also want to know if I need a permit before I build or replace a fence, and whether anything about my specific property or neighborhood would change the answer.

Please write your answer for a regular Portland homeowner with no background in city code, zoning, or permitting. Aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level. Treat me as smart but unfamiliar with how the city works. Use everyday words wherever possible — say "how far the fence has to sit back from the property line" before you say "setback," and "the part of your yard between the house and the street" before "front setback area." If you would not use a word in conversation with a neighbor over the fence, define it the first time you use it. Keep paragraphs short. Use bolded keywords inside sentences instead of dense tables. Only use a table if you're comparing three or more fence locations on the same dimensions, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above the table. Do not use words like "shall," "promulgated," or "thereunder."

Please structure your answer this way:

1. **Lead with a plain-English summary (3–5 sentences) that directly answers my question first.** No citations, no code numbers, no jargon in this opening — just tell me, in everyday language, what I can build in each part of my yard and whether I need a permit. I should be able to read only this paragraph and walk away knowing what to do.

2. **Then give me the details, broken out by location:**
   - **Front yard** (the part facing NE 33rd Ave): max height, any rules about how see-through it has to be, anything about corner-vision or sight-distance rules at intersections or driveways, and how close to the sidewalk or street it can sit.
   - **Side yards** (between my house and the neighbors on either side): max height, and any rules about the part of the side yard that's in front of the house vs. behind it.
   - **Back yard**: max height, and anything about alleys if there's one behind my property.
   - For each, explain whether the fence material matters (solid wood vs. chain link vs. open lattice on top), and whether retaining walls or hedges count toward the height.

3. **Tell me the zoning and any overlays for this specific address.** This property is in the Alameda neighborhood in Northeast Portland, which I believe is a single-family residential zone, but please confirm. Flag any historic district, conservation district, environmental overlay, or design overlay that could change the fence rules for my address — and tell me how to verify on PortlandMaps myself. If the zoning could be different from what I assume, say so up front.

4. **Tell me which Portland City Code Titles govern this** — I understand fence rules are mostly in **Title 33 (Zoning Code)**, with possible cross-references to **Title 11 (Trees)** if a hedge or tree is involved, and **Title 24 (Building Regulations)** if the fence is tall enough to need a structural permit. Cite specific section numbers (for example, "PCC 33.110.255") in parentheses *after* the plain-English explanation, not in front of it. Every code citation should follow a sentence that already explained the rule in normal words.

5. **Tell me clearly whether I need a permit, and what triggers one.** Specifically: at what fence height does a building permit kick in? Does replacing an existing fence in the same spot need a permit? Does a fence over a retaining wall change the rules? What about an electric fence, barbed wire, or a privacy fence with a lattice topper? If the permit rules depend on height, give me the height thresholds in plain language.

6. **Give me a numbered "what to do next" list, in the exact order I should do it.** For each step, tell me:
   - Who to contact (name the bureau and what they actually do — e.g., "Bureau of Development Services, or BDS, which handles building permits and zoning questions")
   - The website, phone number, or counter to use
   - What to bring or have ready (address, site plan sketch, property survey, fence height/material)
   - Roughly what it costs (translate dollar amounts into something I can plan around, like "about the cost of a nice dinner out" or "about a week's groceries")
   - Roughly how long it takes (e.g., "one phone call, 15 minutes" or "about one weekend's worth of evenings to fill out")
   - Mark clearly which steps are **free**.

7. **Give me a "what to ask the city" script** — the actual sentence or two I can read aloud on the phone or paste into an email to the bureau. Not just "call BDS" — write out the literal words: "Hi, I own a home at 3220 NE 33rd Ave and I want to build a [X]-foot [material] fence in my [front/side/back] yard. Can you tell me…" Include the specific questions I should ask so I don't get bounced between departments.

8. **Give me a "common gotchas" section in plain language** — the specific mistakes Portland homeowners make with fences. For example: building before checking the property line, assuming the sidewalk edge is the property line when it isn't, putting a tall fence too close to a driveway and blocking driver sight lines, removing a street tree thinking it's on their property, building a hedge that grows past the height limit, or not knowing the neighbor co-owns a shared fence. Tell me what to watch out for *before* I spend money or pour a post.

9. **Friendly accuracy caveats.** Frame these as a friendly warning, not a legal disclaimer. For example: "Zoning for your specific address can vary block by block — confirm on PortlandMaps (portlandmaps.com) before you spend money on materials or a contractor." Tell me what I should personally verify and where.

10. **A glossary at the end** defining every acronym or technical term you used more than once: at minimum **BDS** (Bureau of Development Services), **PBOT** (Portland Bureau of Transportation), **Urban Forestry**, **PCC** (Portland City Code), **setback**, **right-of-way / ROW**, **zoning overlay**, **lot line**, and any others you introduced. One sentence each, plain English.

A few extra things I'd find helpful but are easy to forget:
- If my fence would sit anywhere near the sidewalk or street, tell me about the strip of city-owned land between the sidewalk and the street (sometimes called the **planting strip** or **right-of-way**) and whether I'm allowed to build on it. Name which bureau owns that question (PBOT, I think) and how to reach them.
- If a tree is in the way — mine or a street tree — tell me what Urban Forestry's role is and whether I need their sign-off before digging post holes near roots.
- If my neighbor and I are sharing a fence on the property line, mention briefly what Portland says about shared fences and what's a city issue vs. a private property-line issue between neighbors.
- If there's anything seasonal (e.g., permits taking longer in summer, or tree-root rules being stricter in certain months), flag it.
- Tell me what a reasonable total cost and timeline looks like for a typical 6-foot backyard fence in Portland — translated into everyday terms ("about the cost of a used car," "roughly one summer's worth of weekends") — so I know if a contractor's quote is reasonable.

Please confirm at the top of your answer whether you were able to look up the actual zoning for 3220 NE 33rd Ave, or whether you're answering based on the typical zoning for the Alameda neighborhood. If you couldn't pull address-specific data, say so plainly and tell me how to check it myself in under five minutes on PortlandMaps.

col1.response:
For your address, there's a quick online path and a formal records request path; for a home on SE Ladd Ave you'll probably want both, plus some bonus resources I'll get to in a moment.

**The quick path: PortlandMaps**

Go to portlandmaps.com, search 1719 SE Ladd Ave, click into the property, then open the **Permits & Zoning** section. You'll see active and historical building, trade, land use, and code enforcement records from roughly 2000 to today. There's also a **Historic Permits** subsection that often has scanned older records, and a **Historic Plumbing** section worth checking (it can document original sewer connections and even cesspool records). For any permit issued after June 1, 2012, the actual plans and applications are viewable for free; you just need a free City of Portland account.

**The formal path: Public Records Request**

For anything older than what shows up on PortlandMaps (especially permits issued before mid-2012), submit a Public Records Request through Portland Permitting & Development at portland.gov/ppd/public-records. They use a GovQA portal. Have the property address and the Property ID ready (the "R Number" from PortlandMaps' Assessor Detail). They'll respond within five business days about scope and any fees.

For active code-enforcement cases, call the Property Compliance Help Line at 503-823-2633.

**One thing worth flagging**

1719 SE Ladd Ave sits inside Ladd's Addition, which became one of Portland's first Historic Conservation Districts in 1977 and was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1988. That has real implications: any exterior alteration visible from the street (doors, windows, roofs, similar character-defining elements) typically triggers Historic Resource Review, with its own forms, fees, and timeline. If the records pull is part of planning a project, factor that in early; rear or non-character-defining work usually has more flexibility.

**The deeper path (since you live in one of the most documented neighborhoods in Oregon)**

Most Ladd's Addition houses were built between 1905 and 1930, and the neighborhood is unusually well-archived. Permits are just one slice. A few resources that go well beyond the city records:

- **Historic Resource Inventory of Portland (1984)**, held at the Oregon Historical Society research library. Arranged by street address; each listed building gets legal description, date built, architectural style, and first owner. Ladd's is heavily represented.
- **Digital Sanborn Maps, 1867 to 1970**, free with a Multnomah County Library card via multcolib.org. Shows your home's footprint, construction materials, and number of stories at multiple points in time. Sheets 701 to 799 of the original Portland atlases cover East Portland and Ladd's Addition specifically.
- **Portland City Directories** (Multnomah County Library and OHS). Pre-internet phone books; you can trace who lived at 1719 SE Ladd Ave year by year for over a century, often with their occupations and workplaces.
- **Multnomah County Assessment & Taxation**, 501 SE Hawthorne Blvd, Suite 175. They keep microfilm with construction date and ownership history; bring the exact address.
- **Architectural Heritage Center** (visitahc.org). Portland-specific research archive with photographs, ephemera, and an extensive list of online databases curated for this exact use case.
- **Oregon Historic Sites Database** via SHPO (heritagedata.prd.state.or.us). Worth checking whether your specific house has its own record beyond just being a contributing property in the district.
- **Block Book of Portland (1907)** at OHS. Documents land ownership at a snapshot during the early build-out of the neighborhood.

A couple of evenings with the Sanborns, a city directory or two, and the Historic Resource Inventory will usually let you piece together who built the house, who lived in it through each decade, what construction materials and additions show up over time, and how the lot evolved. For a Ladd's home specifically, the trail tends to be unusually rich.

All of the above are official government or established nonprofit archives; nothing requires paying a third-party "house history" service, which is good because most of those just resell what the libraries above offer for free.
