# Can I Build a Pergola at 4773 SE 52nd Ave, Portland, OR 97206?

## Plain-English Summary

**Yes, you can almost certainly build a pergola here.** Your property is in a regular single-family residential zone with no historic district, no environmental overlay, and no special design rules attached to it, so the path is as straightforward as it gets in Portland. If you keep your pergola **detached from the house, no taller than 15 feet, and no bigger than 200 square feet** (think roughly a 12 ft x 16 ft footprint or smaller), and you keep it **at least 5 feet from your side and rear fences** and **10 feet back from the front sidewalk**, you do not need a building permit. The single biggest thing to know: **even when you don't need a building permit, you still must follow the zoning rules** (setbacks, lot coverage, height); breaking those is what gets people fined or forced to tear things down. Also, **attaching the pergola to your house** flips it from "small accessory project" to "addition" and changes the rules immediately, so plan it as a freestanding structure if you want the simplest path.

---

## Your Property at a Glance

Before walking through the rules, here's what PortlandMaps shows for your address (you can verify any of this at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/4773-SE-52ND-AVE/R336087/):

- **Zoning:** R5 (Residential 5,000), the city's most common single-family zone (https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/base/R5)
- **Lot size:** about 4,792 square feet (0.11 acres)
- **House size:** 810 sq ft, built 1988
- **Neighborhood:** Woodstock
- **Overlays / special districts:** None. No historic, conservation, environmental, plan district, or design overlay applies.
- **Watershed:** Willamette River, Taggart subwatershed (combined sewer area, eligible for Clean River Rewards stormwater discount)

That "no overlays" line is genuinely good news; overlays are where most surprise rules come from, and you don't have any.

---

## The Rules, In Plain Language

### What "zoning" means and what your zone (R5) allows

**Zoning** is just the city's rulebook for what you can build on your land and how big it can be. Your property is zoned **R5 (Residential 5,000)**, which is Portland's standard single-dwelling residential zone built around lots of about 5,000 square feet (PCC 33.110.010, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf). R5 allows houses, accessory dwelling units (small second homes, often called "ADUs"), and **accessory structures** like sheds, garages, covered patios, and pergolas (PCC 33.110.100, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf).

The rules for your zone live in **Title 33** of the Portland City Code (the **Zoning Code**); the building safety rules live in **Title 24** (the **Building Regulations**). Every project has to satisfy both (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits).

### What counts as an "accessory structure"

An **accessory structure** is just a stand-alone building on your lot that supports the main house but isn't lived in; sheds, garages, greenhouses, **covered patios**, and **covered decks** are the city's listed examples (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/garage-shed-and-accessory-structures). A pergola fits in this family.

Portland's Zoning Code splits these into two flavors that matter a lot for pergolas:

- **Detached covered accessory structure** = roof or top that blocks rain (a solid roof, dense slats, polycarbonate panel, fabric stretched tight). These follow PCC 33.110.245 (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf).
- **Detached uncovered vertical structure** = open lattice, "arbor," "trellis," or other "garden structure" without a true cover (PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf).

A classic open-top pergola (rafters with sky between them) sits in the second bucket; a pergola with a solid roof, dense louvers angled to shed water, or a permanent canopy sits in the first.

### How far the pergola has to sit from your property lines (setbacks)

A **setback** is just "how far the building has to sit from each property line." For R5, the standard minimums (PCC 33.110.220, Table 110-3, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf) are:

- **Front: 10 feet** from the front property line (so, roughly 10 feet back from the sidewalk).
- **Side: 5 feet** from each side fence.
- **Rear: 5 feet** from the back fence.

(Verified in a 2008 BDS land use decision for an R5 lot: structures over 6 feet tall must be 5 feet from side and rear and 10 feet from the front, https://efiles.portlandoregon.gov/record/16220213/file/document.)

There's a **side-and-rear setback exception** for accessory structures: you can put a covered accessory structure inside the side or rear setback (right up near the fence) only if **all** of these are true (PCC 33.110.245.C.2.b, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf and https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/detached-covered-accessory-structures-worksheet/download):

- It's at least **40 feet from the front property line** (or 20 feet from a side street on a corner lot).
- The footprint is **24 feet by 24 feet or smaller**.
- The total length of accessory structures along that property line is **24 feet or less**.
- It's **15 feet tall or less**, with walls **10 feet or less**.
- The portion in the setback is screened from neighbors by a fence.
- No doors or windows in the setback walls face the neighbor.
- No rooftop deck.

Plain English: if your pergola is small (10x12, 12x12, 12x14 are typical), in the back yard, under 15 feet tall, and you have a fence between you and the neighbor, you can place it close to the fence. Otherwise, hold the 5-foot setback.

### When you actually need a building permit

This is where Title 24 (the **Building Regulations**) takes over. Portland follows the Oregon Residential Specialty Code (the state building code Portland adopts). The simple rule (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits):

> A building permit is required to build, demolish, or move any structure that is **more than 200 square feet in area** OR **has a height of more than 15 feet** (measured from the ground to the average roof height).

So a freestanding, non-habitable pergola is **exempt from a building permit** if all of these are true (Building Official Determination 21-05, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bod-21-05-habitable-vs-non-habitable-accessory-structures and Oregon BCD permits guide, https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/lbdd/pages/oregon-permits.aspx):

1. **200 sq ft or less** in area;
2. **15 feet or less** in height (measured from grade plane to the average height of the highest roof surface);
3. **Detached** (not attached to your house);
4. **One story**;
5. **Non-habitable** (no kitchen, bathroom, sleeping space).

Cross either threshold (over 200 sq ft, over 15 ft tall, attached to the house, or with utilities and walls turning it into a "room") and you need a residential building permit.

**Important even if no building permit is needed:** the **zoning rules still apply**. Setbacks, lot coverage, height, the accessory-structure standards in 33.110.245, and Title 11 tree rules all govern your project regardless (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-permits). A "no permit needed" pergola that violates a setback is still illegal and the city can make you fix or remove it.

### Other permits that might apply

- **Electrical permit (Title 26).** If you want lights, outlets, a ceiling fan, or any hardwired wiring in the pergola, you need an electrical permit, even if no building permit is required (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-electrical-permits). You can pull this yourself only if you both **own and live in** the home; otherwise a licensed electrical contractor must do it.
- **Mechanical permit (Title 27).** Adding a gas line for a heater or fire feature, or a vented heating/cooling appliance, triggers a mechanical permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-mechanical-permits).
- **Plumbing permit.** Outdoor sinks, drinking fountains, or anything tying into water/sewer lines require a plumbing permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-plumbing-permits).
- **Zoning permit.** If your pergola does not need a building permit, but the city wants confirmation it complies with zoning (for example, you're stretching toward setback limits, or it's an unusually large covered structure inside a setback exception), you may apply for a **zoning permit** (PCC Title 33; https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-permits). A zoning permit is a smaller, paper-only review of your site plan against the Zoning Code.

### Does covering it change anything? (The most common gotcha)

Yes; this is where people trip. The category your pergola falls into depends on the top:

- **Open lattice / spaced rafters / no rain cover:** treated as a **detached uncovered vertical structure** (an "arbor or other garden structure," PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf). Looser setback options.
- **Solid roof, slats angled to shed rain, polycarbonate panels, taut fabric, or fixed shade cloth:** treated as a **detached covered accessory structure** (covered patio family, PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf). Stricter setback options.
- **Retractable canopy:** the city looks at whether, in its closed/extended position, the structure presents a permanent rain-shedding cover. A canopy that's permanently installed and primarily extended is treated as covered; a clearly retractable system that is stowed open most of the time generally is not. Confirm with PP&D for your specific design (no specific public code section answers this; verify with PP&D at 503-823-7300).

**Attaching the pergola to your house** flips it again; it becomes part of the house, not an accessory structure. The relevant rule: a "porch cover, patio cover, carport, or other open-sided roofed structure" attached to the house **and over 200 square feet needs a building permit** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits). If you bolt rafters to your siding or roof, the city is going to look at the loads on your house.

### Lot coverage and floor-area limits

**Lot coverage** = the share of your lot covered by buildings (footprints, basically). **Floor area ratio (FAR)** = the total square footage of all enclosed buildings divided by the lot size; it controls how much building you can stack on a lot.

For accessory structures, the city's Detached Covered Accessory Structures Worksheet sets three coverage tests (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/detached-covered-accessory-structures-worksheet/download):

1. **No single accessory structure** can have a footprint bigger than the smallest primary house on the lot, **or 900 sq ft**, whichever is greater (PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf).
2. **Total accessory structure coverage** can't exceed **20% of lot size** (PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf).
3. **Total building coverage** (house + everything else) must stay within the maximum for your zone in Table 110-5 (PCC 33.110.225, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf).

For your specific lot (4,792 sq ft), 20% = about **958 square feet of total accessory structures allowed**. With your 810 sq ft house, you have plenty of headroom for a pergola plus a future shed or even a future ADU.

A pergola without enclosing walls usually doesn't count toward FAR (FAR measures enclosed buildings), but a covered structure does count toward **building coverage**; the rule of thumb is anything more than 6 feet above grade with an impervious cover (something rain doesn't pass through) counts as building coverage (PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf). Open-rafter pergolas typically don't count, because they aren't impervious.

### Tree rules (Title 11)

Portland's tree code (**Title 11**) is administered by **Urban Forestry** (a division of Portland Parks & Recreation). The big trigger:

- **Private trees 12 inches or more in diameter at breast height (DBH)** (DBH = trunk diameter measured 4.5 feet up from the ground) are protected; removing one needs a tree permit (PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/all and https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/tree-removal-and-replanting-faqs).
- During development (and digging counts), if your **ground disturbance is more than 100 sq ft within the root protection zone** of a private tree 12 inches DBH or larger (or a city tree 6 inches or larger), Title 11.50 development rules kick in, and you may need a tree plan (PCC 11.50.020, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50).
- The **root protection zone** is roughly **1 foot of radius per 1 inch of trunk diameter** (a 12-inch tree gets a 12-foot protection radius). Digging post holes inside that circle can require an arborist's review.
- **Street trees** (the trees in the planting strip between sidewalk and curb) are regulated regardless of size; almost any work on them needs an Urban Forestry permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/tree-removal-and-replanting-faqs).

If you have a notable tree where you want the pergola, walk the project past Urban Forestry **before you dig**: 503-823-TREE (8733), https://www.portland.gov/trees.

### HOAs, easements, and underground utilities

- **HOAs and deed restrictions.** The city does not enforce private homeowners' association rules or **deed restrictions** (the conditions, covenants, and restrictions ("CC&Rs") recorded against a property when it was developed). These are private and you'll need to read your title report or ask a title company. Worth checking; CC&Rs sometimes ban unenclosed accessory structures or require certain materials.
- **Easements.** An **easement** is a recorded right someone else has to use part of your land (a sewer line, a power line, a shared driveway). You can usually find these on your title report and on PortlandMaps under the property detail page (https://www.portlandmaps.com). For sewer easements specifically, you can look them up at https://www.portlandmaps.com using the property search. Building over an easement can force you to tear the structure down later when the utility needs access.
- **Call before you dig.** Oregon law requires you to call **811** (the Oregon Utility Notification Center, https://digsafelyoregon.com) at least **two business days before any digging**, including post holes for a pergola. It is free; utilities come out and mark gas, electric, and communications lines. Skipping this step and hitting a gas line is one of the fastest ways to ruin your weekend (and your day in court).

### Cost and timeline in human terms

A few things to know up front:

- **System Development Charges (SDCs)** are one-time city fees for new construction that adds demand on water, sewer, transportation, parks, and schools. SDCs are tied to creating new dwellings or adding plumbing fixtures; **a non-habitable pergola without water or sewer hookup typically does not trigger SDCs** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sdc). I could not find a published code section that explicitly says "pergolas are SDC-exempt"; verify with PP&D at 503-823-7300 if you plan to add plumbing fixtures.
- **No-permit pergola** (under 200 sq ft, freestanding, no electrical): cost is materials + labor only. **Free** from the city's side. Timeline: a couple of weekends if you're handy; about a week of build time for a contractor.
- **Pergola that needs a building permit** (over 200 sq ft, over 15 ft tall, or attached to the house): residential building permit fees scale with valuation; for a small simple structure expect roughly **$300 to $1,200** in city fees (about the cost of a nice family dinner out for a small one, up to a month of groceries for a bigger one). Online review through Development Hub PDX typically takes **2 to 6 weeks** for a small structure, sometimes longer in busy season; budget a full season (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/get-permit). Verify current fee schedules at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/fees because fees change.
- **Electrical permit** for a couple of outlets and lights: typically **$100 to $250** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-electrical-permits). Same-day or 1-week review online.

### Who to actually contact

- **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)** is the city bureau that handles all the building, zoning, electrical, mechanical, and plumbing permits. (PP&D is the new name; you may still see "Bureau of Development Services" or "BDS" on older pages; same agency.) Phone: **503-823-7300**, Mon-Fri 8 AM to 5 PM. Walk-in (Development Services Center, 1900 SW 4th Ave): **Tuesdays 8 AM-4 PM, Thursdays 8 AM-noon**, appointment recommended (https://www.portland.gov/service-locations/development-services-center). Free 15-minute appointments with a planner: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/15-min.
- **Urban Forestry** (Portland Parks & Recreation) handles all tree rules under Title 11. Phone: **503-823-TREE (8733)**. Web: https://www.portland.gov/trees.
- **Bureau of Environmental Services (BES)** handles sewer, stormwater, and watershed health questions. You'd call BES if you're disturbing a lot of soil or building near a stormwater system. Web: https://www.portland.gov/bes (use the contact page for current numbers; verify before relying on a specific phone number).
- **Portland Bureau of Transportation (PBOT)** handles anything in the **right-of-way (ROW)** (the public sidewalk, planting strip, alley, or street). For most pergolas this isn't relevant unless you're working near an alley or street tree. Phone: **503-823-5185** (alley/curb cut questions, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits).
- **Portland Fire & Rescue (PF&R)** sets fire code rules. For an open pergola in a back yard, PF&R rarely gets involved; but if you build within 3 feet of the property line, additional fire-resistance requirements kick in (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/garage-shed-and-accessory-structures).
- **Oregon 811 (Utility Notification Center):** dial **811** or **1-800-332-2344** at least two business days before digging (https://digsafelyoregon.com). Free.

---

## What To Do Next (In Order)

1. **Look up your property on PortlandMaps.** FREE. About 10 minutes. Visit https://www.portlandmaps.com, type in your address, and confirm: zoning is **R5**, no overlay zones, no historic, no environmental. Click "Permits & Zoning" then "Zoning & Districts." You should see what I've written above.
2. **Locate your property lines.** Free if you already have a copy of your **plot plan** or **survey** from when you bought the house; otherwise expect $400 to $900 for a licensed surveyor. About a week. The city does not determine where your property lines are; the only authoritative source is a survey (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/commonly-asked-questions). Eyeballing from the fence is risky because fences are often a foot or two off the actual line.
3. **Sketch your pergola on a site plan.** FREE; an afternoon. Draw your lot to scale, mark the house, the proposed pergola location, and distances to all property lines. Note size (length x width), height, whether it's attached to the house, and whether the top is open or covered.
4. **Read the city's worksheet.** FREE; about 30 minutes. https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/detached-covered-accessory-structures-worksheet/download. This is the same worksheet city planners use; if you can check the boxes, you're cleared for the zoning side.
5. **Book a free 15-minute appointment with a Portland planner.** FREE. Online booking at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/15-min. Bring your sketch and questions. The planner will tell you whether you need a permit and which one. Do this **before** you buy materials; it can save you a re-design.
6. **Call Oregon 811.** FREE. Phone **811** at least two business days before you dig the post holes. Online at https://digsafelyoregon.com.
7. **Check your title report for easements and CC&Rs.** Free if you still have your closing packet from when you bought; otherwise contact a title company (typically $50 to $150). About a week.
8. **If you need a permit, apply online via Development Hub PDX.** Cost varies (roughly $300 to $1,200 for a small accessory structure plus any trade permits); a few weeks for review. https://www.portland.gov/ppd/permits-online.
9. **Build, then close out the permit with inspections.** If you got a permit, schedule the required inspections at the right stages. Inspection request line: **503-823-7000** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-inspections).

---

## What To Ask the City (Phone or Email Script)

If you call PP&D General Inquiries at **503-823-7300** (or send a message at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/contact), here's a script you can read straight off:

> "Hi, my name is [your name] and I own the property at **4773 SE 52nd Avenue, Portland, OR 97206**. PortlandMaps shows it as zoned **R5** with no overlays. I want to build a **freestanding pergola** in the back yard. It will be **about [length] feet by [width] feet**, **[X] feet tall** at the highest point, **[open lattice / solid roof / retractable canopy]** on top, and **[at least 5 feet] from the side and rear property lines**. It will not be attached to the house, won't be lived in, and will not have plumbing. Can you confirm whether I need a **building permit**, a **zoning permit**, or both? And can you confirm the project meets the standards in **PCC 33.110.245** for detached accessory structures? I'd also like to schedule a free 15-minute appointment with a planner to walk through my site plan."

If you'd rather email, paste that into the contact form at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/contact and attach your site plan sketch and a photo of your back yard.

---

## Common Gotchas (What People Get Wrong)

- **"Under 200 sq ft means no permit, period."** Wrong. The 200 sq ft / 15 ft rule is the **building permit** trigger. Zoning rules (setbacks, height, lot coverage, accessory structure standards in PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf) apply at all sizes. People build a 10x12 pergola without a permit, plant it 18 inches from the fence, and then get a code complaint from a neighbor. The pergola was small enough to skip a building permit, but never small enough to skip the 5-foot side setback.
- **Attaching to the house "to save a post."** Bolting a pergola to your siding or roof turns it into an addition. A "porch cover, patio cover, carport, or other open-sided roofed structure" attached to the house and over 200 sq ft requires a building permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits). Even under 200 sq ft, attachment requires structural review of how the new loads tie into your house.
- **Adding a solid roof or polycarbonate panels later.** People build an open-rafter pergola (technically uncovered) and then "upgrade" with polycarbonate panels. That changes its category to a covered accessory structure, which can change the setback rules and may require a permit and a zoning review (PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf).
- **Stringing lights and outlets without an electrical permit.** Hardwired electrical work needs an electrical permit even when no building permit is required (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-electrical-permits). Plug-in fairy lights, fine; outlets and ceiling fans, permit.
- **Building within 3 feet of the property line.** This triggers fire-protection requirements (1-hour fire-rated walls and other rules) under the Building Code (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/garage-shed-and-accessory-structures). You might still be allowed to build there, but the construction details get more expensive.
- **Forgetting the front setback if you have a corner lot or unusual shape.** A "front" lot line can be confusing; if your house faces SE 52nd, the front yard setback is 10 feet from that line. On a corner lot, the front lot line is the **shorter** of the two street lot lines, regardless of which direction your front door points (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/detached-covered-accessory-structures-worksheet/download).
- **Digging post holes near a tree.** A 14-inch DBH maple has roughly a 14-foot root protection radius. Augering a post hole there can be a Title 11 violation (PCC 11.50.020, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50) and can also kill the tree. Walk the spot with Urban Forestry first (503-823-TREE).
- **Skipping 811.** It's free and required by Oregon law. Hitting a gas or fiber line with a post hole digger is a bad day; hitting a buried electrical service is worse.
- **Assuming HOA / CC&R rules don't apply because Portland says it's fine.** The city does not enforce private deed restrictions, but your neighbor (or HOA) can sue you. Read your CC&Rs before you build.
- **Trusting the fence as the property line.** Fences in Portland are often a foot or two off the legal line. If you're tight on a setback, get a survey.

---

## Friendly Caveats

- Zoning, fees, and even bureau names change. **Verify on PortlandMaps before you spend money on plans:** https://www.portlandmaps.com.
- Some items above are clearly stated in code; others (especially fees, retractable canopies, and SDCs for non-habitable structures) require a quick phone call to confirm against the latest interpretation. When in doubt, ask the planner during your free 15-minute appointment.
- "Bureau of Development Services" (BDS) and "Portland Permitting & Development" (PP&D) are the same agency; the city renamed it but older documents still say BDS. Either name should get you to the right people.
- The setback numbers I cited (10 / 5 / 5) are the standard R5 minimums, but corner lots, **flag lots** (lots with a long driveway and the buildable area set behind another lot), and lots with environmental overlays follow different rules. Your property is none of those, but if any of that changes, the numbers can change too.
- I am not a Portland city employee or a lawyer. Treat this as a well-organized starting point, not legal advice. The 15-minute appointment with a real city planner is the gold standard for your specific design.

---

## A Worked Example for Your Lot (Above and Beyond)

Because you said "regular homeowner," here's the concrete happy-path scenario for **4773 SE 52nd Ave** that lets you build with the **least friction**:

**Build a 12-foot by 14-foot freestanding open-rafter pergola** (168 square feet total) in the back yard, **at least 5 feet from the rear fence and 5 feet from each side fence**, and **at least 40 feet back from the front sidewalk** (your house is already there, so this is automatic if it goes behind the house). Keep the height **at most 12 feet** at the top of the rafters. **Don't attach** any part of it to the house. **Don't add a solid roof or fixed panels** (open rafters or removable shade cloth only). **Run no hardwired electrical**; if you want light, use plug-in string lights from an existing exterior outlet.

In that scenario:

- No building permit (under 200 sq ft, under 15 ft tall, freestanding, non-habitable: BOD 21-05, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bod-21-05-habitable-vs-non-habitable-accessory-structures).
- No zoning permit needed if you're not in an overlay (you aren't, per PortlandMaps R336087 detail).
- No electrical or mechanical permit (no hardwired work).
- No tree permit needed (no tree removal; if you have a 12+ inch DBH tree on the lot, just keep post holes well outside its root protection zone).
- Total city cost: **$0**. Total time: a couple of weekends.
- 168 sq ft adds well under 4% to your lot coverage, leaving you nearly **800 sq ft of accessory structure capacity** in reserve (your 20% limit on this lot is about 958 sq ft) for a future shed, garage, or even a detached ADU later.

If you want bigger or fancier, you can absolutely have it; you'll just pay the toll of permits, fees, and review time. Knowing the threshold lets you choose the trade-off knowingly instead of stumbling into it.

---

## Glossary

- **Accessory structure:** A building on your lot that supports the house but isn't lived in (shed, garage, greenhouse, covered patio, pergola).
- **ADU (Accessory Dwelling Unit):** A small, second dwelling on a lot that already has a main house; basically a legal in-law unit (PCC 33.205).
- **BDS (Bureau of Development Services):** The old name for the agency that handles permits. Now called PP&D.
- **BES (Bureau of Environmental Services):** The city bureau that handles sewer, stormwater, and watershed health.
- **Building coverage:** The footprint of all buildings on the lot; how much of your lot is covered by structure.
- **CC&Rs (Covenants, Conditions & Restrictions):** Private rules recorded against a property by a developer or HOA. Not enforced by the city, but legally binding.
- **DBH (Diameter at Breast Height):** A tree's trunk diameter measured 4.5 feet up from the ground. The standard size measure in Portland's tree code.
- **Easement:** A recorded right that lets someone else use part of your land (a utility line, a shared drive, etc.).
- **FAR (Floor Area Ratio):** Total building floor area divided by lot area. A measure of how much building is allowed on a lot.
- **HOA (Homeowners' Association):** A neighborhood organization that enforces private community rules (CC&Rs).
- **Lot coverage:** The percentage of the lot covered by buildings.
- **Overlay zone:** An extra layer of rules on top of the base zone (historic, environmental, design, plan district). Your property has none.
- **PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation):** Handles streets, sidewalks, alleys, curb cuts, and the public right-of-way.
- **PCC (Portland City Code):** The official numbered code; "PCC 33.110.245" means Title 33, Chapter 33.110, Section 245.
- **PF&R (Portland Fire & Rescue):** The fire bureau; sets fire code rules.
- **PP&D (Permitting & Development, Portland):** The current name for the city's permit agency, formerly BDS.
- **R5:** Portland's most common single-family residential zone, named after its 5,000 sq ft typical lot size.
- **ROW (Right-of-Way):** The public strip including sidewalk, planting strip, street, and alley. Owned or controlled by the city.
- **SDCs (System Development Charges):** One-time city fees for new construction that adds demand on city systems (water, sewer, transportation, parks, schools).
- **Setback:** The minimum distance a structure has to sit back from a property line.
- **Title 11 (Trees):** The Portland code chapter that regulates trees on private property and street trees.
- **Title 24 (Building Regulations):** The Portland code chapter for building safety; this is where building permit rules live.
- **Title 26 (Electrical):** Portland's electrical code chapter.
- **Title 27 (Mechanical):** Portland's mechanical (heating/cooling/gas) code chapter.
- **Title 33 (Zoning):** The Portland Zoning Code; the rulebook for what you can build where.
- **Urban Forestry:** A division of Portland Parks & Recreation that administers Title 11 and is the contact point for any tree question.
- **Zoning permit:** A small permit confirming your project complies with the Zoning Code when no building permit is needed.

---

**Sources I leaned on most heavily** (already cited inline above; collected here for convenience, not as a substitute):

- PortlandMaps property detail for R336087: https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/4773-SE-52ND-AVE/R336087/
- PCC Title 33, Chapter 33.110 (Single-Dwelling Zones, current PDF): https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_1.pdf
- Detached Covered Accessory Structures Worksheet (PP&D): https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/detached-covered-accessory-structures-worksheet/download
- Building Official Determination 21-05 (Habitable vs. Non-habitable Accessory Structures): https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bod-21-05-habitable-vs-non-habitable-accessory-structures
- "Do you need a permit for your home project?" (PP&D): https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits
- "Garages, sheds, and accessory structures" (PP&D): https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/garage-shed-and-accessory-structures
- PCC Title 11 (Trees): https://www.portland.gov/code/11/all and Chapter 11.50: https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50
- Free 15-minute planner appointments: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/15-min
- Oregon 811 / Utility Notification Center: https://digsafelyoregon.com

Good luck with the build; this is one of the gentler permitting paths in Portland, and your specific lot is set up about as cleanly for it as you could ask for.

pergola.enrichedQuestion:
I want to know whether I can build a pergola at my property at 4773 SE 52nd Ave, Portland, OR 97206, and what all the requirements are. I'm a regular homeowner — I have no background in permits, zoning, or building codes, so please write your answer for a Portland resident or small-business owner with no code or permitting background. Aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level. Treat me as smart but unfamiliar with city processes.

Here's the full picture of what I need from your answer:

**Lead with a plain-English summary first.** In the first 3–5 sentences, just tell me: can I build a pergola here, do I need a permit, and what's the single biggest thing I should know before I start? No citations, no jargon, no code section numbers in that opening — just a clear answer in everyday language, like a knowledgeable friend would tell me over the fence. Do NOT lead with a property data table.

**Then walk me through the details, in plain language, defining every term the first time you use it.** Define every acronym and technical term — ADU (accessory dwelling unit), BDS (Bureau of Development Services), BES (Bureau of Environmental Services), PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation), PP&D (Permitting & Development), PF&R (Portland Fire & Rescue), Urban Forestry, PCC (Portland City Code), ROW (right-of-way), SDC (System Development Charges), FAR (floor area ratio), and anything else. Say "how far the building has to sit from the property line" before you ever say "setback." Say "the rules for what you can build on your land" before you say "zoning." Use everyday words wherever possible. Keep paragraphs short. Use bolded keywords inside sentences instead of dense tables. Only use a table when comparing 3+ options on the same dimensions, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above any table.

Please specifically cover, in clear language:

- **What zoning rules apply to my address.** What zone is 4773 SE 52nd Ave in (likely a residential zone — confirm and explain what that zone allows in plain English), and are there any overlays or special districts (historic, environmental, design, plan district) that change what I can do? What does PortlandMaps say for this specific property?
- **Whether a pergola counts as an "accessory structure"** under Portland's rules, and what that means for size, height, and how close it can sit to my property lines (front, side, rear). Translate the setback distances into "X feet from the back fence" language.
- **When a pergola needs a building permit and when it doesn't** — what size, height, and attachment-to-house thresholds trigger a permit under Title 24 (Building Regulations) and Title 33 (Zoning Code). Be specific about the actual numbers (square footage, height in feet) and where they come from.
- **What other permits might apply** — electrical (Title 26) if I want lights or outlets, mechanical (Title 27) if I add a heater or fan, and any zoning review if the pergola is unusually large, tall, or close to the property line.
- **Whether covering the pergola changes anything** — open lattice top vs. solid roof vs. retractable canopy. This is a common gotcha; please flag what tips it from "no permit needed" into "permit required."
- **Lot coverage and floor-area limits** — does adding a pergola eat into how much else I can build on the lot later (a future shed, ADU, addition)? Explain "lot coverage" and "FAR" in plain words.
- **Tree rules** — if there's a tree near where I want the pergola, are there Title 11 (Trees) / Urban Forestry rules about digging or building near a tree's root zone? What size tree triggers a tree permit?
- **HOA, easements, and underground utilities** — remind me to check for private deed restrictions, utility easements on my lot, and to call 811 before digging post holes. Explain what an easement is.
- **Cost and timeline in human terms.** What permits typically cost (give a dollar range and translate it — "about the price of a nice dinner out" or "about a month's groceries"), how long review takes ("roughly two weekends" vs. "a full summer"), and whether System Development Charges (city fees for new buildings) apply to a pergola.
- **Who I actually contact, by name of bureau and how to reach them** — phone number, website, walk-in vs. online, and what each bureau does. At minimum: BDS for permits, BES if any drainage/stormwater question comes up, Urban Forestry if a tree is involved.

**Then give me a numbered "what to do next" list, in the exact order I should do it.** For each step, tell me: who to call or what website to visit (with the full URL), what to bring or have ready, what it costs (mark steps that are FREE), and roughly how long it takes in human terms (an afternoon, a week, a month). The first step should be free and online if possible (e.g., "look up your zoning on PortlandMaps").

**Then give me a "what to ask the city" script** I can literally read aloud on the phone or paste into an email to BDS — not just "call BDS," but the exact sentence to say, including my address and what I'm trying to do.

**Inline citations are required for every factual claim, in the body of the answer itself.** Every rule, number, fee, timeline, height limit, setback distance, square-footage threshold, or contact info must be followed in the same sentence by both:
  - the exact Portland City Code section (e.g., `PCC 33.110.250.E.1`, not just "Title 33" or "the zoning code"), AND
  - a direct, working full URL to the authoritative source on portland.gov, portland.gov/code, the specific Administrative Rule PDF, PortlandMaps, or the relevant bureau page — full `https://...` URLs, not "[here]" or "the city's website" or "[1]".

Format example: "A pergola in a residential zone usually has to sit at least 5 feet from the side property line (PCC 33.110.250.B, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/250)."

I am going to copy this entire answer and paste it into a document, so every citation must survive plain-text copy-paste. Do NOT put citations only in footnotes, hover tooltips, reference numbers like "[1]", or a separate "Sources" list at the end of the answer. They must appear inline, in the same sentence as the claim. A "Sources" list at the end is fine as a bonus, but it does not replace inline citations. If you cannot find a specific code section and URL for a claim, say so directly — for example, "I could not find a specific code section setting the exact pergola height limit; verify with BDS at 503-823-7300" — rather than citing vaguely as "Portland City Code" or "the BDS website."

**Then give me a "common gotchas" or "what people get wrong" section** in plain language — the specific mistakes Portland homeowners make when building pergolas (e.g., assuming "no permit under 200 sq ft" applies to all situations, building too close to the property line, attaching to the house and not realizing that triggers a permit, ignoring overlay zones, forgetting about trees, etc.). Be concrete about what people get wrong here, not generic.

**Then give me friendly accuracy caveats** — for example, "zoning for your specific address may vary; verify on PortlandMaps at https://www.portlandmaps.com before you spend money on plans," framed as helpful warnings, not legal disclaimers.

**End with a short glossary** of every acronym or technical term you used more than once (zoning, setback, accessory structure, lot coverage, FAR, ADU, BDS, BES, PBOT, Urban Forestry, PCC, SDC, easement, ROW, overlay, etc.), in plain English.

Things I do NOT want:
- Do not lead with a property data table — lead with the answer in words.
- Do not stack code section numbers without explaining each one in plain English right next to it.
- Do not assume I know the difference between BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, Urban Forestry, or PF&R — name what each one does the first time you mention it.
- Do not use words like "shall," "promulgated," "thereunder," or any other legal-register language. Write the way a friendly city employee would explain this over coffee.
- Do not use vague citations like "Portland City Code," "Title 33," "the BDS website," "city regulations," "[1]," "see above," or footnote-only references. Every citation must be a specific section number AND a full URL, inline, in the same sentence as the claim.
- Do not bury citations in a Sources list and leave the body uncited.

Please answer for my specific address: **4773 SE 52nd Ave, Portland, OR 97206.**

col1.response:
Short answer: yes, you can probably extend it; but at this address, "yes" comes with a catch most homeowners don't see coming. Your house sits inside the **Ladd's Addition Conservation District**, and front porches are specifically called out in the design guidelines as character-defining features. So this is less a permit question than a design-review question.

## Why this matters for your address specifically

A few things stack up here:

1. **The whole neighborhood is a Conservation District** (locally designated 1977, on the National Register since 1988). Boundaries: SE 12th to 20th, Hawthorne to Division. You're well inside.
2. **Ladd Avenue is one of the two diagonal "boulevards"** in the wagon-wheel plan; it's one of the most visible streets in the district, so anything on your front facade will get extra scrutiny.
3. **Your 1924 bungalow was built squarely in the period of significance** (1905-1930), which makes it almost certainly a contributing structure. That raises the bar further.
4. **The Ladd's guidelines literally name front porches.** From section 5 of the Exterior Rehabilitation guidelines: original entrances, front porches, and projecting features "should be retained or restored." For bungalows, defining elements include "open porch with square wood posts, multi-pane double-hung windows, simple pipe handrails."

So an extension is doable, but the Historic Landmarks Commission (or staff, depending on size) will want to see that:
- The original porch isn't being destroyed; it's being added to in a way that's compatible
- The new portion is differentiated from the old (no faking history)
- It's reversible in principle, so the historic form could be recovered later
- Massing, materials, columns, railings, and roofline match the original Craftsman vocabulary

## The two procedural paths

This is the part most blog posts gloss over. Conservation Districts (unlike full Historic Districts) give you a choice:

**Path A: Historic Resource Review (discretionary).** Type I if the project is small; Type II if it affects more than 150 sq ft of facade elevation. Type I is staff-approved and relatively quick (4-8 weeks); Type II involves notice to neighbors and longer review (8-10 weeks), with fees roughly $991-$6,124 last time the city published them.

**Path B: Community Design Standards (clear and objective).** Conservation Districts in Portland allow you to skip discretionary HRR if your design meets the objective standards in Chapter 33.218. This is the faster, cheaper path if your porch extension fits the box. Most people don't know this option exists; it's worth asking about specifically.

## What I'd actually do, in order

1. **Pull the official record for your address.** Go to [portlandmaps.com](https://www.portlandmaps.com/), enter 2145 SE Ladd Ave, and check the "Zoning and Districts" tab. Confirms zoning (likely R5), the Conservation District overlay, and any other overlays (tree code, etc.).
2. **Read the actual guidelines.** The current document is the [Ladd's Addition Conservation District Design Guidelines (2011 update)](https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2019-07/laddsaddition_conservation_district_guidelines_2011_update.pdf) on portland.gov. It's the approval criteria; the ~25 MB original 1990 PDF is also on the [city's design guidelines page](https://www.portland.gov/bps/planning/design-guideline-documents).
3. **Request a free pre-application conference** with Portland Permitting & Development (formerly BDS). They'll tell you which review type you fall into and flag issues early; this is the single highest-leverage thing you can do.
4. **Talk to a designer who has been through Ladd's review specifically.** Arciform is the name that came up most often in my research as a Ladd's Addition specialist; there are others. Worth getting two opinions before committing.
5. **Loop in HAND** (Hosford-Abernethy Neighborhood Development Association) early. They're the recognized neighborhood association and get notice on Type II reviews; their support smooths things considerably.

## Two things you might not have considered

**Tax credit upside.** Because Ladd's is on the National Register and your house likely contributes, substantial rehabilitation work can qualify for the Oregon Special Assessment of Historic Property program (a 10-year property tax freeze) or federal rehab tax credits if any portion is for income-producing use. If your porch extension is part of a larger rehab, this can change the math meaningfully. Worth asking the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) before you finalize scope.

**Sequencing matters.** If the existing porch has any rot or deferred maintenance, framing the project as "restoration with compatible expansion" rather than "addition" generally lands better in review; restoration is preferred over replacement in the guidelines, and there's a Type Ix restoration track that's faster.

Want me to dig into any of these next: the specific objective standards in 33.218 to see if your project could skip discretionary review, the pre-app process and what to bring, or the SHPO tax-incentive angle?

Great question — and one with more layers than it might seem, because 2145 SE Ladd Ave sits in **Ladd's Addition**, one of Portland's most strictly regulated historic districts. Let me pull the actual property data and figure out what's really possible here, not just what zoning theoretically allows.I have rich data. Let me also check what's happening with porch projects in Ladd's Addition more broadly so I can give Chris real precedents.Let me verify what kinds of porch projects actually get approved in Ladd's Addition by checking the city's design guidelines and recent precedents.I've pulled the actual property record, permit history, and the official Ladd's Addition design guidelines. Here's what's really going on with your house, including one fact I think will surprise you.

## The short answer

**Yes, but with very real constraints.** Your house is a *contributing resource* in the Ladd's Addition Historic District (city-designated and on the National Register since 1988), so any change to the front porch that's visible from the street triggers **Historic Resource Review** before you can pull a building permit. R5 zoning is the easy part; the historic overlay is what governs the design.

## The fact you probably want to know

**There's already a denial in your file.** A Historic Resource Review at 2145 SE Ladd was denied in 2017 (case LU 16-181898). I can't see the description in the public permit data, but a denial on the property is unusual and you should ask the Bureau of Development Services (BDS) for the decision document before you propose anything new. If the prior denial touched the front facade, your application history will be the first thing the reviewer pulls up.

The good news: a later 2022 case (LU 22-108718 HR) at the same address was *approved* for window replacements, with conditions (all-wood windows, simulated divided lites limited to double-hungs, no field changes). That tells me the bureau will work with this house when the proposal respects original materials. The 2025 garage deconstruction also went through cleanly.

## The framework: what's actually approvable

Think of porch work on a contributing Ladd's Addition house as a spectrum:

**Easy (Type I review, ~4–6 weeks, ~$1.5–3.5k in fees):**
- Restoring the existing porch to its original configuration (replacing rotted decking, columns, railings, ceiling boards in-kind with historic-matching materials)
- Repairing or replicating original details based on documentary evidence

**Possible but real work (Type II, 8–10 weeks, $5k+ fees, public notice):**
- Extending the porch *if* you can show photographic or Sanborn-map evidence that it was originally larger
- Wrapping a porch around a side façade *if* there's documentation it once existed that way
- Side or rear additions that don't change the street-facing character

**Very hard or won't be approved:**
- Pushing the porch significantly forward of the established front building line (the guidelines specifically protect "continuity of scale, setback, orientation, and materials" along the block face)
- Enclosing the porch with screens or glazing (the guidelines literally illustrate "enclosed porch" as an *inappropriate change*)
- Adding a porch where none existed historically; falsifying history is a non-starter

Guideline #5 in the 1988 Ladd's Addition Conservation District guidelines is specifically about your situation: original entrances to buildings, front porches and projecting features, such as balconies, bays and dormer windows should be retained or restored. The bureau reads "extended" very differently from "restored."

## What's been approved on neighboring blocks recently

Ladd's Addition is an active permit area. I count at least 8 Historic Resource Reviews in the district since early 2024, most recorded as approved (i.e., Type I administrative approvals). The neighborhood is not a no-go zone; it's a "do it right" zone. Several of those cases are on adjacent blocks to yours.

## The smart first move (and it's free)

Before you spend a dollar on an architect, request a **Pre-Application Conference** (also called Early Assistance) with BDS's Land Use Services. It's free or very low-cost depending on scope, and you'll meet with a historic preservation planner who will tell you, on the record, what they'd want to see in an application. For Ladd's Addition contributing resources, this conversation routinely saves people from spending $20k+ on plans that don't make it through review.

Two specific things to bring:
1. **Historic photos of your house.** The Oregon Historical Society and Multnomah County tax photos (Portland Maps has a "tax assessor photos" layer with historical imagery) sometimes show what the porch looked like in the 1930s–50s. If your proposed extension matches the original, your case writes itself.
2. **The 2017 denial decision document.** Walk in already understanding what the bureau objected to last time.

## Two creative angles you may not have considered

Because the front facade is the most regulated surface on your house, homeowners in Ladd's often get the porch experience they want by *not* fighting the front:

- **Side or rear porch addition.** These face much lighter scrutiny if they're not visible from the public right-of-way. The Arciform-style guidance and BDS practice both note that rear or non-character-defining additions often allow greater flexibility.
- **Sleeping porch reactivation.** Ladd's bungalows and foursquares often have second-story sleeping porches that were enclosed mid-century. Restoring one to its open original form is the *opposite* of falsification; it can be a Type I and you get an outdoor room without touching the front.

## A trust note (per your preferences)

Everything above is sourced from Portland's official permit and assessor APIs, the 1988 city-adopted Ladd's Addition Conservation District Guidelines (handpdx.org and Portland.gov copies), and current land-use case records. I'd still verify the specifics with BDS directly before acting; permit thresholds and fees change, and the exact reading of "contributing" status for your house deserves confirmation from the planner assigned to your case. The free Pre-Application Conference is the right place to do that confirmation.

Want me to dig into the 2017 denial details, pull historic tax-assessor photos for your house, or draft the Early Assistance request?
