col3.response:
# Can You Extend the Front Porch at 2145 SE Ladd Ave?

**Address:** 2145 SE Ladd Ave, Portland, OR 97214
**Prepared:** May 6, 2026
**For:** Chris (homeowner, no zoning/permitting background assumed)

---

## 1. The plain-English answer

**Probably yes, but with extra steps.** Your house is in Ladd's Addition, which Portland treats as a historic district, so almost any change to the front of the house has to be approved by the city's historic review staff before you can pull a building permit. The setback rules in your zone (R5) leave you a little room out front, and there are some special exceptions for porches, but the bigger constraint is going to be the historic review: the porch has to look like it belongs on a 1924 home in Ladd's, with matching materials, columns, railings, and roof pitch. Plan on roughly 4 to 8 months from "I want to do this" to "shovels in the ground," and budget several thousand dollars in city fees on top of construction costs. Doing the work first and asking permission later is the single biggest mistake people make in Ladd's, and the city can force you to tear it out.

---

## 2. Your specific property: what the city's records say

A look at the official city records (https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/2145-SE-LADD-AVE/R200734_did/) shows the following about your address:

- **Base zone: R5 (Residential 5,000)** — a single-family residential zone with a 5,000-square-foot minimum lot size, defined in Portland City Code Chapter 33.110 (PCC 33.110.020, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110).
- **Historic district: Ladd's Addition Historic District (district code "LA")** — your property is in a city-designated historic district, which triggers Historic Resource Review under PCC Chapter 33.846 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).
- **Historic classification: "Contributing"** — your house is officially considered to add to the historic character of the district. The city's record names it the "Guasco, Peter & Firmina, Residence #2," built in 1924 (state of Oregon historic record at https://heritagedata.prd.state.or.us/historic/index.cfm?do=v.dsp_siteSummary&resultDisplay=648173). Being "Contributing" means exterior changes are reviewed more strictly than they would be on a non-contributing house in the same district.
- **No environmental, conservation, design, or other overlays** apply to this specific lot per the city's zoning record (https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/2145-SE-LADD-AVE/R200734_did/).
- **Past land use cases on this exact property:** there have been two Historic Resource Reviews here, **LU 16-181898 HR (2016)** and **LU 22-108718 HR (2022)**, both shown as "Review Complete" (https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/2145-SE-LADD-AVE/R200734_did/). You can pull those decisions from the city's e-files; they're an excellent preview of what the historic review staff cares about on your specific block.

A quick note on a name change you're going to bump into: what used to be the **Bureau of Development Services ("BDS")** was renamed **Portland Permitting & Development ("PP&D")** on July 1, 2024 (https://www.portland.gov/permitimprovement/news/2024/4/24/city-announces-name-and-structure-portlands-new-permitting-bureau). A lot of older webpages, forms, and guides still say BDS. They mean the same office. The phone number is the same: **503-823-7300**.

---

## 3. Is a porch extension an "addition" or an "alteration"?

For the city's purposes, it can be **either**, and which bucket yours lands in depends on a few details:

- **Covered or uncovered?** A covered porch (one with a roof) counts as a "covered structure" and is more restricted than an open deck under PCC 33.110.245 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110).
- **Attached to the house or freestanding?** Attached porches are governed by the rules for the main building; freestanding ones are "detached accessory structures" with their own rules in PCC 33.110.245.
- **How much new floor area does it add?** Porches with a roof and a floor add **floor area** (technically called Floor Area Ratio, or FAR; rules in PCC 33.110.210, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110), and there's a one-time exception that lets you add up to 250 square feet without it counting against your maximum if the house was finalized at least 5 years ago (PCC 33.110.210.C.1, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110).
- **How much of the existing front of the house is being changed?** This drives the type of historic review (covered in section 5 below).

For a typical Ladd's-Addition front-porch extension (think: extending an existing covered porch out 4 to 8 feet, or wrapping it around a corner), the city will most likely treat it as an **exterior alteration plus a small addition**, and it will need a building permit either way.

---

## 4. How close to the street can the porch come?

Your zone (R5) requires the **front of the house to sit at least 10 feet back from the front property line** (Table 110-4 in PCC 33.110.220.B, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110). The "front property line" usually runs along the back edge of the sidewalk; the strip of grass with the street trees is **public right-of-way**, not part of your lot.

There are a few ways the front setback can shrink for porches and decks specifically:

- **Setback averaging.** If your neighbors on either side have houses or porches closer to the street than 10 feet, your porch's setback can be reduced to match the average of theirs (PCC 33.110.220.D.1, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110). This is the single most useful exception for porches in Ladd's, because the front yards on most blocks are pretty consistent. Worth measuring your neighbors before you draw plans.
- **Established building lines.** If the existing house has a wall (including an existing porch wall) already inside the 10-foot setback, additions that follow that same line may be allowed (PCC 33.110.220.D.5, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110). Translation: if your current porch already sticks out 6 feet into the setback, a wider new porch can match that line.
- **Low, uncovered decks** (no more than 2½ feet above ground) can extend all the way to the property line (PCC 33.110.220.C.3.e, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110). A porch with a roof does not get this break.
- **Uncovered decks and balconies** that don't qualify for the rule above can stick out 20% of the depth of the setback (so about 2 feet of a 10-foot setback) and have to stay at least 3 feet from any property line (PCC 33.110.220.C.1.c, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110).

**Important caveat:** Portland's zoning code does *not* contain a specific "porches may project 6 feet into the front setback" rule that some other cities have. People sometimes mistakenly cite one. If anyone tells you about a flat 6-foot porch projection, ask them for the code section; I couldn't find one in the current code. The actual flexibility comes from setback averaging and established building lines, both above.

If you want to push out farther than these exceptions allow, you can apply for an **Adjustment** under PCC 33.805 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/805), which is essentially asking the city for permission to bend the rule. Adjustments cost about $2,600 for a single standard for a house (Land Use Services Fee Schedule, July 1, 2025, p. 1, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/land-use-services-fee-schedule-city-portland-effective-july-1-2025/download).

---

## 5. Historic review: this is the big one

Because Ladd's Addition is a city-designated historic district and your house is "Contributing," **almost any visible change to the outside of the house has to be approved through Historic Resource Review before a building permit can be issued** (PCC 33.445.200.D, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/400s/445; PCC 33.846.060, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).

### The three review types, in plain English

The city sorts every land use review into one of these "types" (PCC 33.730, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/700s/730; summary at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/documents/summary-land-use-procedure-types/download):

- **Type I** — staff decides, no hearing, neighbors within 100 feet get a notice in the mail; decision typically within **21 days** of a complete application.
- **Type Ix** — staff decides, no hearing, neighbors within 100 feet get a notice; decision typically within **45 days**. (The "x" is just an internal code; not extra exciting.)
- **Type II** — staff decides, neighbors within 150 feet get notice and 14 days to comment; decision within **42 days**. Can be appealed to the Historic Landmarks Commission (volunteer board of preservation experts, sometimes called "the historic review board").
- **Type III** — public hearing in front of the **Historic Landmarks Commission ("HRC")**, which then decides. Hearings are within 51 days of a complete application; pre-application conference is required first. This is the slowest, most expensive route.

### Which type applies to your porch?

For a residential property in a Historic District like Ladd's, Table 846-3 in PCC 33.846.060.B.3 sets the thresholds (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846):

- **Exterior alteration with affected facade or roof area under 150 square feet** → **Type I** (staff, ~21 days; minimum fee about $1,333).
- **New structure with floor area 800 square feet or less** → **Type Ix** (staff, ~45 days; minimum fee about $1,739).
- **Anything else not exempt** with project value at or under $547,400 → **Type II** (~42 days; minimum fee about $3,809).
- **Anything else not exempt** with project value over $547,400 → **Type III** (hearing).

For a typical front-porch extension (6 to 8 feet deep, 12 to 20 feet wide), the "affected facade or roof area" almost always exceeds 150 square feet once you count the new front face, the new roof slope, and the disturbed existing facade. So **plan on Type II** unless your contractor or architect can convince the planner the project is small enough to qualify as Type I, or stylistically counts as a small "new structure" of 800 sf or less for Type Ix. **A free 15-minute appointment with a city planner is the fastest way to find out which type applies to your specific design** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments).

### What the historic reviewer will care about

In Ladd's, the rules are the **Ladd's Addition Conservation District Design Guidelines** (originally adopted Feb. 10, 1988; updated 2011; full PDF at https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2019-07/laddsaddition_conservation_district_guidelines_2011_update.pdf). Confusingly, the guidelines are still named "Conservation District" even though the city's current zoning map labels Ladd's as a Historic District; the rules apply either way. The reviewer will look at:

- **Front facade integrity.** The guidelines specifically discourage major changes to street-facing facades; additions are supposed to go on the back or side and be minimally visible from the street. A visible front-porch extension is exactly the kind of thing they scrutinize most carefully.
- **Materials.** Original siding, trim, and porch materials should be matched on additions, not switched out for vinyl or composite that doesn't read as period-correct (Ladd's guidelines, exterior siding section).
- **Columns, railings, and balustrades** that match the original style and proportions.
- **Roof pitch and overhang depth** consistent with the existing roof and the neighborhood pattern.
- **Compatibility with the neighborhood.** A 1924 craftsman bungalow porch should not get a 21st-century glass-and-steel addition; nor should it grow a Victorian gingerbread porch that pretends to be from a different era.

Beyond the district-specific guidelines, the reviewer also applies the citywide approval criteria in PCC 33.846.060.G — protecting historic character, differentiating new from old without overpowering it, making sure the addition could be removed in the future without harming the original house, and matching massing/scale (PCC 33.846.060.G.1 through G.10, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).

**Pro tip:** look up the two prior Historic Resource Reviews on your property (LU 16-181898 HR and LU 22-108718 HR) on the city's e-file system at https://efiles.portlandoregon.gov/. The decision documents will show you exactly what the reviewers approved on your house before, and what conditions they attached. That's a free preview of the bar you'll need to clear.

---

## 6. Building permit path

After (and only after) historic review approval, you'll apply for a **building permit** through Portland Permitting & Development. The permit gets recorded with the County before construction starts (PCC 33.700.080, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/700s/700). For a porch extension you'll most likely apply for a **Residential Addition/Alteration permit** through the city's online system, Development Hub PDX (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/development-hub-pdx).

### What plans does PP&D want to see?

For a porch extension, expect to submit:

- **Site plan** drawn to scale showing the lot, the existing house, the new porch footprint, all property lines, and setbacks (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-permits).
- **Floor plan** of the porch.
- **Front and side elevations** showing how the new porch looks from the street and side, with materials and dimensions called out.
- **Foundation details** (porch footings have to meet the building code).
- **Structural details** (post sizing, beam sizing, roof framing).
- **Photos of the existing house** for the historic review portion.

### Can you do this as a homeowner, or do you need an architect?

Oregon law allows homeowners to design and submit plans for their own single-family residence under the "homeowner exception" (Oregon Revised Statutes 671.030, https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_671.030). For a porch extension, that's legally permitted. **Practically speaking, however,** for a Type II historic review on a contributing resource in Ladd's, most people hire either a residential designer or an architect with historic experience, because the drawings need to clearly demonstrate compliance with the design guidelines and the historic approval criteria. Plans that don't read as professional-quality often get more questions and slower reviews. A residential designer typically costs less than an architect.

For the actual construction, you'll need a **licensed contractor registered with the Oregon Construction Contractors Board ("CCB")** unless you're doing the work yourself as the owner-occupant under the homeowner exemption (ORS 701.010, https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_701.010).

---

## 7. Other city offices that might get pulled in

A front-porch extension can trigger more than just permitting and historic review. Here's who, and when:

- **Bureau of Environmental Services ("BES")** runs the city's sewer and stormwater system (https://www.portland.gov/bes). For a porch with a roof, BES gets involved if the new roof area increases impervious surface; you may have to manage the runoff (a small bioswale or downspout disconnect, for example). The threshold and rules are in the city's Stormwater Management Manual (https://www.portland.gov/bes/stormwater/swmm). For a porch under a few hundred square feet, this is usually solved with a downspout disconnection or a small planter and is not expensive.
- **Portland Bureau of Transportation ("PBOT")** runs streets, sidewalks, and the public right-of-way (the strip of grass and the sidewalk in front of your house) (https://www.portland.gov/transportation). PBOT comes in if any part of your project (including stairs, a stoop, or even scaffolding during construction) lands in the right-of-way; you'd need a separate permit (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permits). On a typical Ladd's lot, the porch itself stays on private property, but check that your stairs don't land on the city's strip.
- **Urban Forestry / new Tree Permitting division.** Ladd's Addition is famous for its mature American elm and Norway maple street trees. If construction will dig within the **root protection zone** of any street tree (a circle around the trunk roughly equal to the tree's drip line), you'll need a Tree Permit and tree-protection measures under Title 11 (https://www.portland.gov/code/11). The Tree Hotline is **503-823-TREE (8733)** (https://www.portland.gov/permitimprovement/news). This is a real concern in Ladd's; many porch projects there end up needing a tree-protection plan before they get a building permit.
- **Portland Fire & Rescue ("PF&R")** mostly cares about new construction with respect to fire access and fire separation distances (https://www.portland.gov/fire). For a residential front porch, this almost never comes up unless you're extending toward a property line or doing something unusual.
- **Portland Water Bureau** doesn't usually weigh in on porches, but if construction touches the water meter or service line in your front yard, they will.

You don't have to call all of them. PP&D coordinates the review across bureaus internally; you submit one application and they route it.

---

## 8. Costs and timelines you can actually plan around

Below is a single comparison of the realistic paths your porch project could take, depending on which historic review type ends up applying. **Plain-English summary:** the cheapest, fastest path is a Type I historic review (about a month and roughly the cost of a decent used couch) if your design touches less than 150 square feet of facade; the typical Ladd's path is Type II (a couple of months and about the price of a used Honda Civic in fees alone); Type III with a public hearing is rare for a porch but adds another couple of months and several thousand dollars.

| Review path | Likely fit for | Time from complete app to decision | City fees (historic review only) | Plain-English equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type I HRR | Small touch-up; under 150 sf of facade affected | ~21 days (3 weeks) | Min ~$1,333; goes up with project value (LUS Fee Schedule, p. 1, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/land-use-services-fee-schedule-city-portland-effective-july-1-2025/download) | About the price of a high-end appliance |
| Type Ix HRR | New "structure" 800 sf or less | ~45 days (6-7 weeks) | Min ~$1,739 | About the price of a nice mountain bike |
| Type II HRR | Most porch extensions in Ladd's (the realistic default) | ~42 days from complete app + ~30-day notice period (so ~10-12 weeks total in practice) | Min ~$3,809; up to ~$21,466 max | About the price of a used Honda Civic |
| Type III HRR | Big or unusual proposals; public hearing | ~51 days from complete app + pre-app conference + ~20-day notice (so ~4-6 months) | Min ~$6,895; up to ~$37,219 max | About the price of a small kitchen remodel |

Add to that:

- **Building permit fees:** for a porch extension, residential building permit fees scale with the value of the work. For a $25,000 to $50,000 porch project, plan on roughly **$1,000 to $3,000** in combined building, plumbing, and structural plan-review fees (current PP&D fee schedule at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/fees-schedules). Confirm at the time you apply.
- **System Development Charges ("SDCs"):** these are one-time fees the city charges new construction to pay for impacts on parks, transportation, water, sewer, etc. (https://www.portland.gov/sdc). Most front-porch extensions to an existing house do **not** trigger meaningful SDCs because they don't add a dwelling unit, but porch areas that add livable square footage may trigger small charges. Confirm with PP&D.
- **Free steps:** PortlandMaps property lookup, the 15-minute planner appointment, the Zoning Information Hotline, and the e-file lookup of past land use cases on your property are all $0.

**Realistic full timeline** for a typical Type II porch extension in Ladd's:

- Weeks 1-2: free 15-minute appointment, design sketches, talk to a contractor or designer.
- Weeks 3-8: design development and drawings.
- Weeks 9-10: submit Historic Resource Review application; pay fees.
- Weeks 11-22 (about 3 months): completeness review, public notice, staff decision.
- Weeks 23-30: building permit application, plan review, corrections.
- Weeks 31+: construction.

Total of about **6 to 9 months** from "I want to do this" to swinging hammers, roughly two summer's worth of weekends if you DIY the design phase.

---

## 9. What to do next, in order (start with the free stuff)

1. **Look up your property** on PortlandMaps at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/2145-SE-LADD-AVE/R200734_did/. You'll see the zoning, the historic district designation, and the past land use cases. **Cost: free. Time: 10 minutes.**
2. **Pull the past Historic Resource Review decisions on your house** (LU 16-181898 HR and LU 22-108718 HR) from https://efiles.portlandoregon.gov/. Read them. They'll tell you what previous reviewers approved or rejected on your specific property. **Cost: free. Time: 30-60 minutes.**
3. **Read the Ladd's Addition Conservation District Design Guidelines** at https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2019-07/laddsaddition_conservation_district_guidelines_2011_update.pdf (focus on the "Exterior Rehabilitation" section). **Cost: free. Time: about an hour.**
4. **Schedule a free 15-minute appointment with a city planner** at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments. Bring sketches, photos of the existing porch, and your address. Ask the questions in section 10 below. **Cost: free. Time: 15-minute meeting + scheduling lead time of usually 1-2 weeks.**
5. **Walk the block with a tape measure.** Note how far back each neighbor's porch sits from the sidewalk. If the average is less than 10 feet, you may qualify for setback averaging (section 4 above). **Cost: free. Time: 30 minutes.**
6. **Schedule an Early Assistance meeting** if you want a deeper conversation than 15 minutes (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance). For a meeting plus written info on a residential project this is currently $1,761 (Land Use Services Fee Schedule p. 2, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/land-use-services-fee-schedule-city-portland-effective-july-1-2025/download). **Worth it for a Type II project; skip if your design is simple.**
7. **Hire a residential designer or architect** with historic experience in Portland (the **Architectural Heritage Center** in Portland keeps an informal list, https://visitahc.org/, and the Bosco-Milligan Foundation runs preservation referrals). **Cost: design fees typically 8-12% of construction cost. Time: design phase 4-8 weeks.**
8. **Submit Historic Resource Review** application through Development Hub PDX (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/development-hub-pdx). Pay the fee. Wait for the staff decision.
9. **After HRR approval, submit your building permit application** through the same Development Hub system.
10. **Hire a licensed Oregon contractor** (verify at the Oregon CCB site, https://www.oregon.gov/ccb), and start construction only after the building permit is issued and posted.

---

## 10. Scripts you can use to ask the city

### A. For the free 15-minute appointment with a city planner

> "Hi, I'm Chris, owner of 2145 SE Ladd Ave, property ID R200734. The house is in the Ladd's Addition Historic District and is a Contributing resource, built in 1924. I'm thinking about extending the existing front porch by about [X] feet, [covered/uncovered], using materials that match the original [siding type / column style]. I'd like to ask: (1) Is my project most likely a Type I, Type Ix, or Type II Historic Resource Review under PCC 33.846.060? (2) Does the front-setback rule apply, and would setback averaging or established building lines help me here? (3) Do you see any obvious red flags in the design before I spend money on drawings? (4) Any street-tree concerns from your end?"

### B. For the Zoning Information Hotline (503-823-7526)

> "I'm calling about 2145 SE Ladd Ave, property ID R200734. Could you confirm the base zone, the historic district designation, and any overlay zones that apply to this lot? I'm planning a front-porch extension and want to confirm the front setback that applies and any porch projection rules I should know about."

### C. For Portland Permitting & Development general intake (503-823-7300)

> "I'd like to start the permitting process for a front-porch extension on a Contributing house in Ladd's Addition Historic District at 2145 SE Ladd Ave, property ID R200734. Can you walk me through the order: do I need Historic Resource Review approval first, then a building permit? And which Tree Permit and stormwater rules I should be aware of?"

### D. For the Tree Hotline (503-823-TREE / 8733)

> "I'm planning a front-porch extension at 2145 SE Ladd Ave. There's a [species, if you know it] street tree about [X] feet from where my porch foundation would go. Do I need a Tree Permit, and what root-protection requirements would apply?"

### E. For the historic review staff (after the planner appointment, if needed)

> "I'm working on a front-porch extension at 2145 SE Ladd Ave, a Contributing resource in Ladd's Addition. I've reviewed the Ladd's Addition Conservation District Design Guidelines and the criteria in PCC 33.846.060.G. Could I send you the design for an early read before I submit a formal Historic Resource Review application?"

---

## 11. Common gotchas in Ladd's Addition for front porches

- **Building first, asking later.** Ladd's Addition is one of the most actively-watched neighborhoods in Portland. Neighbors do call in on visible work. A complaint can result in a stop-work order, a fine, and a requirement to remove unapproved work and re-do it after historic review. Don't even pour a footing without your HRR approval and building permit in hand.
- **Wrong materials.** Vinyl trim, composite "wood-look" decking on a front porch, plain pressure-treated railings, or off-the-shelf big-box columns are common rejection reasons. Matching the original is usually cheaper than re-doing it after the inspector flags it.
- **Wrong column style.** Replacing tapered craftsman columns with skinny round colonial columns is the single most common style mismatch on porch reviews in Portland's historic districts. Match what's already on your house and what's typical of the original 1920s craftsman/bungalow vocabulary.
- **Roof pitch that doesn't match.** A front porch roof that's much flatter or much steeper than the existing house roof reads wrong from the street and gets called out in review.
- **Encroaching into the public right-of-way.** Porch stairs, planters, or bay-window-style projections that creep onto the city's strip of grass or sidewalk need a separate PBOT encroachment permit, and PBOT can deny it.
- **Setback math errors.** The front lot line is usually at the back edge of the sidewalk, not at the curb. Many homeowners mismeasure and end up with a porch that "looks fine" but technically violates the setback by a foot or two; that gets caught at plan review.
- **Street-tree damage.** Digging within the root-protection zone of a Ladd's elm without a Tree Permit and an arborist's plan can mean fines, a required tree-protection bond, and worst case a delay until the tree's health is confirmed.
- **Underestimating timelines.** People budget for "a couple of months" and then discover it's six. Plan accordingly; the historic review staff is small and Ladd's is busy.
- **Treating the design guidelines as suggestions.** They're approval criteria. The reviewer has to find that your design *meets* them; vague gestures toward "it's kind of period appropriate" don't fly.
- **Skipping setback averaging when it would help.** If your neighbors are already at 7-8 feet of setback, you may not even need an Adjustment to push out further; setback averaging is your friend, but you have to document the neighbors' setbacks on your site plan.
- **Mixing up "Conservation" and "Historic."** The current zoning map labels Ladd's as a Historic District, but the design guidelines are still titled "Conservation District Guidelines" because that's their original 1988 name. Reviewers know what you mean. Don't let the inconsistency throw you.
- **Using stale forms.** Forms and webpages still say "BDS" all over the place. The agency is now Portland Permitting & Development. Same office, same phone number, new name as of July 1, 2024.

---

## 12. Friendly accuracy warnings

- Zoning, historic status, and overlay zones are **specific to your address** and can change. Re-confirm everything on PortlandMaps (https://www.portlandmaps.com/) before you spend money on drawings; this guide reflects what was on the city's record on May 6, 2026, but they update it.
- **City fees and timelines change at least once a year.** The Land Use Services Fee Schedule is updated each July (next refresh around July 1, 2026). Confirm current numbers with PP&D before budgeting.
- **The 6-to-9-month timeline is typical, not guaranteed.** Backlogs at the historic review staff or building plan review can stretch this. Type III hearings are scheduled when commission calendars allow.
- **Code references in this document point to current Portland City Code as of May 2026.** The city updates Title 33 multiple times per year (most recent update packet was October 2025, https://www.portland.gov/charter-code-policies/changes/2025/10/zoning-code-update-packet-215). If you're reading this more than a few months after May 6, 2026, double-check the relevant sections.
- **I am not a city planner, lawyer, or architect.** Treat this as a starting map, not a final word. The free 15-minute appointment with a real city planner (step 4 above) is the single most important sanity-check you can do.

---

## 13. Glossary

- **Adjustment** — a request for permission to bend a specific zoning standard, like a setback. Decided through a Type II review under PCC 33.805 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/805).
- **BDS** — Bureau of Development Services; the old name for what is now Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D).
- **BES** — Bureau of Environmental Services; runs sewer and stormwater. Phone 503-823-7740, https://www.portland.gov/bes.
- **Building permit** — the city's permission to actually build; obtained from PP&D after any required land use reviews.
- **Conservation District** — historically, a slightly less-protective category than a Historic District. Ladd's was first designated as a Conservation District in 1977; the city's zoning map currently labels it a Historic District. The original design guidelines retain the "Conservation District" name.
- **Contributing resource** — a building inside a Historic District that the city has identified as adding to the district's historic character. Subject to the strictest review level inside the district.
- **CCB** — Oregon Construction Contractors Board; the state agency that licenses contractors. https://www.oregon.gov/ccb.
- **Established building line** — when an existing wall sits inside the required setback, you can sometimes match that line on a new addition (PCC 33.110.220.D.5).
- **FAR / Floor Area Ratio** — the ratio of total building floor area to lot size. R5 maxes out at 0.5 to 1 for one dwelling unit (Table 110-4, PCC 33.110.210, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110).
- **HRC** — Historic Landmarks Commission; the volunteer board that decides Type III historic reviews and hears appeals on Type II decisions (https://www.portland.gov/bps/historic-landmarks-commission). Sometimes informally called "the historic review board."
- **Historic District** — a city-designated area subject to historic review under PCC 33.445 and 33.846. Ladd's Addition is one.
- **Historic Resource Review ("HRR")** — the city's process for approving exterior changes inside historic districts, defined in PCC 33.846.060 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).
- **PBOT** — Portland Bureau of Transportation; runs streets, sidewalks, and public right-of-way (https://www.portland.gov/transportation).
- **PCC** — Portland City Code. Title 33 is Planning and Zoning; Chapter 33.110 is single-dwelling zones; Chapter 33.846 is Historic Resource Reviews.
- **PF&R** — Portland Fire & Rescue (https://www.portland.gov/fire).
- **PP&D** — Portland Permitting & Development; the bureau that issues building permits and processes most land use reviews. Phone 503-823-7300, https://www.portland.gov/ppd. Replaced BDS on July 1, 2024.
- **Projection** — when part of a building, like a bay window, eave, or stairs, sticks out into a required setback. Rules in PCC 33.110.220.C.
- **R5** — Residential 5,000-square-foot zone; Portland's most common single-dwelling zone (PCC 33.110, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110).
- **ROW** — Right-of-way; the publicly-owned strip in front of your property that includes the sidewalk and parking strip. Different from your private property even though it's often unmarked.
- **SDC** — System Development Charges; one-time fees on new construction (https://www.portland.gov/sdc).
- **Setback** — how far the building has to sit back from a property line. Front setback in R5 is 10 feet (Table 110-4, PCC 33.110.220).
- **Setback averaging** — a rule that lets your front setback shrink to match the average of your neighbors' setbacks (PCC 33.110.220.D.1).
- **Type I, Ix, II, III** — the four levels of land use review procedure, going from fastest/simplest (Type I, ~21 days, staff decision) to slowest/most public (Type III, ~3-4 months, public hearing) (PCC 33.730, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/700s/730).
- **Urban Forestry** — the Portland Parks & Recreation division that manages street trees and the city's tree code (Title 11, https://www.portland.gov/code/11). Tree permitting itself moved to PP&D in 2024.

---

## Sources (for reference, in addition to the inline citations above)

- PortlandMaps property record: https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/2145-SE-LADD-AVE/R200734_did/
- Portland City Code Title 33 (Planning and Zoning): https://www.portland.gov/code/33
- PCC 33.110 Single-Dwelling Zones (current PDF): https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/110-sd-zone_3.pdf
- PCC 33.846 Historic Resource Reviews (current PDF): https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/33.846-historic-resource-reviews.pdf
- PCC 33.445 Historic Resource Overlay Zone: https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/33.445-historic-resource-protection-overlay-zone.pdf
- Land Use Services Fee Schedule (effective July 1, 2025): https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/land-use-services-fee-schedule-city-portland-effective-july-1-2025/download
- Summary of Land Use Procedure Types: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/documents/summary-land-use-procedure-types/download
- Historic Resource Review Approval Criteria Matrix: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/documents/historic-resource-review-approval-criteria-matrix/download
- Ladd's Addition Conservation District Guidelines (2011 update): https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2019-07/laddsaddition_conservation_district_guidelines_2011_update.pdf
- Free 15-minute appointment scheduling: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments
- Early Assistance: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance
- PP&D bureau homepage: https://www.portland.gov/ppd
- PP&D phone: 503-823-7300; Zoning Information Hotline: 503-823-7526; Tree Hotline: 503-823-8733 (TREE)

front-porch.enrichedQuestion:
I want to know whether I can extend my front porch at 2145 SE Ladd Ave, Portland, OR 97214, and what it would actually take to do it legally — permits, rules, costs, who to call, and the gotchas. This property is in the Ladd's Addition area, which I've heard is a historic district, so I need that factored in.

Please write your answer for a Portland resident with no background in zoning, permitting, or building code. Aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level. Treat me as smart but unfamiliar with city processes. Lead with a plain-English summary that directly answers "can I extend my front porch?" in 3–5 sentences before any tables, citations, or code language. Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it (BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, HRC, PCC, ROW, FAR, SDC, ADU, ORSC, etc.). Use everyday words instead of code language — say "how far the porch has to sit from the property line" before saying "setback"; say "the city's historic review board" before "Historic Landmarks Commission." Keep paragraphs short. Use bolded keywords inside sentences instead of dense tables. Only use a table if you're comparing 3+ options on the same dimensions, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above any table. Translate dollar figures and timelines into something I can plan around (e.g., "about the price of a used car," "roughly one summer's worth of weekends").

Specifically, please cover:

1. **The plain-English answer first.** Can I extend my front porch at this address? In the first paragraph, answer in everyday language with no citations or jargon. Be honest if the answer is "probably yes but with extra steps" or "probably no without a special approval."

2. **What zoning and overlays apply to my specific address.** Look up 2145 SE Ladd Ave on PortlandMaps and tell me the base zone, any historic district designation (Ladd's Addition is a National Register and likely a Portland historic district), conservation overlays, and anything else that changes the rules for a porch extension. Explain in plain English what each one means for me.

3. **Whether a front porch counts as an addition, an alteration, or something else** under Portland's rules, and how that changes what I have to do. Explain how the porch's size, height, whether it's covered or uncovered, whether it's attached to the house, and how far it sticks out from the existing house wall affect the answer.

4. **Front setback and projection rules** — how close to the street my porch can come, and any special rules that let porches stick out farther than the rest of the house. Cite the exact code section and URL.

5. **Historic review** — because Ladd's Addition is a historic district, will I need approval from the city's historic review staff or commission before I can build? What's the difference between a Type I, Type II, and Type III historic review in Portland, and which would likely apply to a porch extension? What design rules will they care about (materials, columns, railing style, roof pitch, matching the original house)?

6. **Building permit path** — do I need a building permit, and if so what kind (residential addition, alteration, or trade permit)? What plans or drawings will the Bureau of Development Services (the office that issues building permits, "BDS") want to see? Is this something I can do as a homeowner, or do I need a licensed contractor or architect?

7. **Other bureaus that might get involved** — does the porch extension trigger anything from BES (sewer/stormwater), PBOT (right-of-way / sidewalk), Urban Forestry (street trees in front of the house), or PF&R (fire)? What would trigger each one?

8. **Costs and timelines I can actually plan around** — rough permit fees, historic review fees, and how many weeks/months each step typically takes. Translate the dollars into "about the price of X" so I can budget. Mark which steps are free.

9. **A numbered "what to do next" list, in the order I should do it.** For each step: who to call or what website to visit, what to bring, what it costs (or "free"), and roughly how long it takes. Start with the free steps (PortlandMaps lookup, historic-district verification, calling BDS for a free 15-minute consultation if that's a thing) before anything that costs money.

10. **A "what to ask the city" script** I can read on the phone or paste into an email when I contact BDS, the historic review staff, and any other bureau. Don't just say "call BDS" — give me the exact question to ask, including my address and what I'm trying to do.

11. **Common gotchas in Portland for front porch extensions**, especially in Ladd's Addition. Things like: front-yard setback surprises, encroaching into the public right-of-way (the sidewalk/parking strip area the city owns), needing matching historic materials, replacing original columns with the wrong style, building before historic approval and being forced to tear it out, street trees blocking the work, and so on.

12. **Accuracy caveats**, framed as friendly warnings — e.g., "zoning and historic status for your specific address may vary; verify on PortlandMaps before spending money on plans," and "fees and timelines change; confirm current numbers with BDS before budgeting."

13. **A glossary at the end** of every acronym and technical term used more than once (BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, HRC, PCC, ROW, setback, projection, historic resource review, etc.).

**Citations are non-negotiable.** Every factual claim — every rule, number, fee, timeline, contact, or code reference — must be followed in the SAME sentence by both the exact code section (e.g., `PCC 33.445.610`, not just "Title 33") AND a direct, working full URL to the authoritative source on portland.gov, the specific Title chapter on portland.gov/code, the specific Administrative Rule PDF, the PortlandMaps page, etc. Use the format: "Front porches can stick out up to 6 feet into the required front setback (PCC 33.110.220.B, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/220)." Do not use vague references like "Portland City Code," "Title 33," "the BDS website," "[1]," "see source above," or footnote-only citations. Do not put citations only in a "Sources" list at the end — they must appear inline next to the claim they support, in the same sentence. I will be copying this entire answer as plain text into another document, so every citation must survive copy-paste. If you cannot cite a specific section + URL for a claim, say so explicitly ("I couldn't find a specific code section for this — verify with BDS at 503-823-7300") rather than citing vaguely. A sources list at the end is fine as a bonus, but inline citations are required.

Do not lead with a property data table — lead with the answer in words. Do not stack code section numbers without a plain-English explanation right next to them. Do not assume I know the difference between BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, Urban Forestry, the Historic Landmarks Commission, or PF&R — name what each one does the first time it appears. Do not use words like "shall," "promulgated," or "thereunder." Write the way a friendly city employee would explain it to a neighbor over coffee.

col1.response:
I'll dig into this for you. Cesspool questions get tangled up between Oregon DEQ rules, City of Portland sewer requirements, and what's actually feasible at your specific lot, so let me check all three angles plus look up your property directly.Now let me check Oregon DEQ's specific rules about cesspool failure, and then look up your specific property on Portland Maps.Now let me look up your specific property in Portland Maps to see what's actually available at 6624 N Missouri Ave.Interesting — the MLS listing from when this property last sold (June 2024) lists the sewer as "Public Sewer." That's a really important detail to flag. But let me double-check the regulatory specifics and connection costs.Let me get the specific connection costs and verify the property's actual sewer situation on Portland Maps.I have enough to give you a solid answer. The short version: yes, almost certainly. But there's a wrinkle at your specific address that's worth checking before you get too deep into planning.

## The regulatory short answer

Within Portland city limits, if your cesspool fails you're required to connect to the public sewer, not replace with a new septic system. Three layers of rule lock this in:

1. **Oregon DEQ (OAR 340-071-0215)**: A failing cesspool is a public health hazard and must be repaired immediately. DEQ will not permit a brand-new cesspool, and a new septic is only allowed where sewer isn't available.
2. **Portland City Code Chapter 17.33**: Whenever a public sewer is available, properties must connect; new septic/cesspool systems aren't an option. Any deferral is automatically terminated the moment a cesspool fails ("in the event of cesspool failure all deferrals will be terminated and connections must occur immediately").
3. **City Code Title 29**: A failing cesspool is treated as a property nuisance, with code enforcement authority to compel abatement.

Public sewer is essentially everywhere in your neighborhood (Piedmont/Humboldt, north of Rosa Parks Way), so the "is sewer available" question won't save you.

## The wrinkle worth checking before you go further

Here's the thing I think is worth flagging: the MLS listing from when 6624 N Missouri last sold in June 2024 reports **Sewer: Public Sewer**. That doesn't necessarily mean it's right (MLS sewer fields are notoriously sloppy), but it raises a real question. Your house was built in 1926, well before public sewer reached much of north Portland, so it almost certainly *started* its life on a cesspool. The question is whether it was already converted to sewer at some prior point and the cesspool merely abandoned in place.

Three ways to settle this in under an hour:

1. **Look at your current water/sewer bill**: If BES is charging you sewer + stormwater (the sewer line is ~$13.54 per 100 cu ft of metered water, plus stormwater fees), you're connected to public sewer. If you're only paying for water, you may still be on a cesspool.
2. **Pull historic plumbing records on portlandmaps.com**: Search the address, scroll to Permits & Zoning > Permits, and check "Historic Plumbing." If there's an old permit titled "Change from cesspool to sewer," the conversion happened. (Sometimes the records are frustratingly handwritten and faint; a $15 in-person trip to PP&D Trade Permits at 1900 SW 4th Ave gets you the full file.)
3. **Get a sewer scope**: $100 to $200, takes about an hour, and gives you a video that definitively shows whether your line goes to a public sewer in the right-of-way or to a buried cesspool. Worth doing regardless; it can also catch root intrusion or cracked clay pipe before they become $20K problems.

A subtle but important catch: even if you're already on city sewer, an old cesspool can still be sitting abandoned on your lot. Portland uses "decommissioned" loosely in historic records; many of these were just stopped up at the building, not actually filled. ## What it actually costs if you do need to connect

- **Permits**: Septic decommissioning permit, plumbing trade permit, and a UR/UC right-of-way permit. A few hundred dollars total.
- **Possible sewer line / branch charges**: Only if BES has to extend a public main; very unlikely at your address since Missouri is fully sewered.

- **City Loan Contract for SDCs**: Up to 20 years to finance the connection charges themselves.

---

# If your cesspool fails at 6624 N Missouri Ave: what actually happens

## The short version

If your cesspool truly fails and you don't already have an active sewer connection, you will almost certainly end up connecting to the City of Portland sewer system, because Oregon banned new cesspools decades ago and your urban lot is too small for a modern septic system. You won't be forced to connect by a "300-foot rule" anymore (Portland retired that program in 2024), but a failing cesspool counts as a public health problem, and the only legal fixes available to you in practice are connect to public sewer or, in rare cases, install an alternative onsite system that DEQ will approve. The good news for your address: the public sewer is already in N Missouri Ave a stone's throw from your front door, your lot has no environmental or historic overlays that complicate things, and your historic permits suggest a connection may already exist. Realistically, plan for **a few months of work and somewhere between roughly the cost of a used Honda Civic and the cost of a small kitchen remodel** ($6,000 to $20,000 all-in for most homeowners), with city loans available if you don't want to write a check.

---

## The rule itself: who decides, and what triggers a required connection

A **cesspool** is a primitive waste disposal system: a buried pit, often brick-lined, that takes raw sewage from a house and lets the liquid seep into surrounding soil. Most Portland cesspools were installed before public sewer reached the neighborhood. Per the Portland Permitting & Development information sheet, a cesspool is typically about 3 to 4 feet across and 8 to 15 feet deep (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2020-09/septic_decommissioning_041117.pdf).

The **state ban** on new cesspools is in **Oregon Administrative Rule (OAR) 340-071-0335**, which says plainly that no person may construct a new cesspool sewage disposal system in Oregon (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0335). So if yours fails, "build a new one" is off the table.

**Decommissioning** means officially shutting down and filling in an old cesspool, septic tank, or seepage pit so it's no longer a hazard. Under **OAR 340-071-0185 (Decommissioning of Systems)**, septic systems and cesspools that are no longer in use are required to be decommissioned (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0185). Under **OAR 340-071-0215 (Repair of Existing Systems)**, a failing onsite system must be repaired immediately to eliminate the public health hazard (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0215).

**Who decides what counts as "failed"?** In practice, the inspector who looks at it does. Common signs of failure include sewage surfacing on the ground, sewage backing up into the house, the pit collapsing, or the lid failing structurally; this is consistent with the Marion County onsite program guidance summarizing **OAR 340-071-0215** (https://www.co.marion.or.us/PW/BuildingInspection/Pages/onsite.aspx). I could not find a single, neat code-defined dollar-bright "failure" definition that lists all conditions; the call is made by the permitting agent on the ground. For your situation, that agent is **Portland Permitting & Development's Septic Program, which handles septic decommissioning inspections for all of Multnomah County under contract** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/septic-decommissioning-permits).

**DEQ (Oregon Department of Environmental Quality)** writes the statewide rules for onsite wastewater (cesspools, septic tanks, seepage pits) under OAR Chapter 340, Division 71 (https://secure.sos.state.or.us/oard/displayDivisionRules.action?selectedDivision=1479). DEQ delegates day-to-day permitting and inspections in Multnomah County to the City of Portland under an "agent" agreement, the structure of which is described in **OAR 340-071-0120 (Jurisdiction and Policy)** (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0120). So DEQ writes the rulebook; the City enforces it for you.

**The City of Portland uses two different bureaus** for this work:

The **Bureau of Environmental Services (BES)** runs the public sewer pipes themselves, the connection charges, and the permit for any work in the street where the city's pipes live. Their authority over public sewer connections is set out in **Portland City Code (PCC) Chapter 17.32 (Public Sewer and Drainage System Permits, Connections, Maintenance, and Damage)** (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32) and **PCC Chapter 17.33 (Connection to the Public Sewer)** (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33).

The **Bureau of Development Services (BDS)** has been merged into **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)** as of recent reorganizations; PP&D handles plumbing permits on private property, the septic decommissioning permit, and inspections, and their septic program web page is the authoritative page for the decommissioning permit at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/septic-decommissioning-permits.

**Is connection mandatory if your cesspool fails?** Until July 2024, yes, in most cases, automatically. **Effective July 1, 2024**, Portland City Council passed Ordinance 191725 which retired the mandatory sewer connection program (https://www.portland.gov/council/documents/ordinance/passed/191725). The current rule, **PCC 17.33.030 (Sewer Connections)**, lists five circumstances that trigger required connection (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33/030):

The first, **17.33.030.A**, is a general policy that all plumbing fixtures discharging wastewater must connect to public sewer, with an exception "where it can be demonstrated to the satisfaction of the City that an onsite wastewater treatment system or other alternative means of sewage disposal can otherwise be lawfully permitted" (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33/030). In practice, on a small urban lot in inner North Portland, that "lawfully permitted onsite system" exception is hard to satisfy because a modern septic system needs setbacks and drainfield area you simply don't have on a 5,000 square foot lot.

The second, **17.33.030.B**, says if your existing onsite system is in place and public sewer becomes available, the city may require connection at its discretion in consultation with the sanitarian, and any expansion, remodel, or change in use that increases capacity will require you to abandon the onsite system and connect (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33/030).

The fifth, **17.33.030.E**, is the safety valve: the City may require connection to protect public health, water quality, or the environment (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33/030). A failing cesspool that's leaking sewage onto the ground or into groundwater fits squarely in this category.

So the short answer: there is no automatic 300-foot trigger anymore, but a **failed cesspool plus an available sewer main practically always results in a required connection** under PCC 17.33.030.A and E (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33/030), combined with the DEQ requirement under OAR 340-071-0215 to immediately fix a failing system (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0215).

**Could you install a new septic system instead?** Theoretically yes; in practice, almost never on an inner-Portland lot. New cesspools are banned (OAR 340-071-0335, https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0335), and **OAR 340-071-0220 (Standard Subsurface Systems)** requires several feet of separation between absorption facilities and groundwater, with significant setbacks from property lines (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0220). Your lot won't meet those.

---

## Which Titles of Portland City Code govern this

In plain English, here's what each Title actually controls.

**PCC Title 17 (Public Improvements)** is the title that owns sewer pipes, sidewalks, and streets. Sewer-specific chapters that matter for you are: **Chapter 17.32** which governs permits and access to public sewer pipes (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32); **Chapter 17.33** which governs when you must connect (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33); and **Chapter 17.36** which governs the city's sewer fees and SDCs (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/36).

**PCC Title 25 (Plumbing Regulations)** governs plumbing on private property, which includes the pipe from your house to the property line and the plumbing inside your walls (https://www.portland.gov/code/25). Permitting & Development administers this title.

**PCC Title 29 (Property Maintenance Regulations)** is the nuisance-and-keep-it-livable title; if a property's sewage situation creates a public nuisance, this title is how the city brings hammer down (https://www.portland.gov/code/29). Failure to connect to city sewer can be enforced as a nuisance, as referenced in the BES Mandatory Sewer Connection program documents (https://efiles.portlandoregon.gov/Record/10877612/File/Document/).

**Administrative rules** (lower than code, but still binding) that matter:

- **ENB-4.17** is the BES rule on sewer connection and lateral repair permitting in the right-of-way (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/citycode/article/154965).
- **ENB-4.20** is the annual BES rate ordinance setting current fees (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-420-sewer-and-drainage-rates-and).
- **ENB-4.05** is the BES rule on how System Development Charges are calculated (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-405-system-development-charges).
- **ENB-4.27** is the BES rule on Nonconforming Sewer Conversion (relevant only if you have a "party sewer" or shared lateral) (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-427-administrative-rules-bureau).

---

## What's special about your address (6624 N Missouri Ave, R232318)

Your taxlot record at PortlandMaps for property R232318 lists the neighborhood as **PIEDMONT**, the year built as **1926**, the zoning base as **R5 (Residential 5,000)**, with **no plan district overlay, no environmental (e-zone) overlay, no historic district, and no conservation district** (https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/zoning/). That's a clean property profile from a permitting standpoint; nothing extra to deal with.

The watershed is **Columbia Slough**, the combined sewer basin is **Albina**, and the sewer in your street is a **combined sewer** that carries both sanitary sewage and stormwater (per the property's environmental detail at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/environmental/). That has two practical effects for you. First, after connection you'll pay both sanitary sewer and stormwater charges on your water bill (PCC 17.36, https://www.portland.gov/code/17/36). Second, if you ever later add roof area or driveway you'd be in a system where stormwater impact matters.

The property is in the **Interstate Corridor Urban Renewal Area** (per the zoning detail at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/zoning/). That doesn't directly change cesspool rules, but it can be relevant for some city programs.

**Sewer availability is excellent.** Per the sewer assets layer at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/sewer/, the closest public combined-sewer manhole (unit AAK484, compkey 10456) sits about **90 feet** from the property, with a combined gravity main running along N Missouri Ave. Several sewer lateral records also appear at distance zero from the parcel, which can mean a lateral exists or extended into the right-of-way at this lot. That brings me to the most important point about your specific address.

**There appear to be two "BES Connection" historic plumbing records on file for 6624 N Missouri Ave** (per the plumbing section of the property's permit detail at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/permits/). I cannot tell from the metadata alone whether these reflect a long-completed connection or partial work, but it's a strong signal that the house may already be on the public sewer and the cesspool may be a legacy feature that was never formally decommissioned. If that's the case, the conversation with BES becomes "I have an old abandoned cesspool that's now collapsing or causing problems, and I need a decommissioning permit," not "I need to connect to the sewer." That's a much smaller, much cheaper job.

**How to verify this yourself in 2 minutes on PortlandMaps**: go to https://www.portlandmaps.com, type **6624 N Missouri Ave** in the search box, click your property, then click **Permits & Zoning** in the left sidebar, then scroll to **Permits** and look for two things: **"Historic Plumbing"** records (the old hand-drawn cards from before the digital era), and **"Permits"** showing modern permit history. Specifically look for a permit description like **"Change from cesspool to sewer"** which would confirm a prior connection (https://www.cozyhomeinspection.com/cesspools.html). If you can't find a digitized copy or it's unclear, a $15 records request through Portland Permitting & Development Trade Permits at 1900 SW 4th Ave will get you the actual paper plumbing card (https://www.cozyhomeinspection.com/cesspools.html).

---

## What permits you'll likely need

Three main permits, depending on what's actually happening on your property.

The **septic decommissioning permit** is what you need to officially close down a cesspool, septic tank, or seepage pit. It's issued by **Portland Permitting & Development's Septic Program**, the fee is **$935** per the official application form (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/septic-decommissioning-permit-application/download), and the city does the inspection. You don't need a trade license to get one; homeowners can perform the work themselves, though most people hire it out (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/septic-decommissioning-permits). Per **OAR 340-071-0185**, the work itself involves pumping the cesspool with a DEQ-licensed sewage disposal provider, then filling with approved material (sand, gravel, or concrete; not common dirt), with the top 12 to 18 inches left open for inspection (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0185 and https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2020-09/septic_decommissioning_041117.pdf).

The **plumbing permit (PT permit)** covers any sewer pipe work on private property between your house and the property line. It's issued by **Portland Permitting & Development**, listed on their fee schedule (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules). I could not find a single fixed dollar figure on the public fee schedule page that maps cleanly to "cesspool-to-sewer plumbing permit"; the fee depends on number of fixtures and labor valuation. Confirm with PP&D Trades Permits at 503-823-7310.

The **sewer connection permit** (also called a **UC permit, "Utility Connection"**) is what your contractor needs to actually tap into the public sewer in the street. It's issued by **BES** in coordination with **PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation)** because the work is in the public right-of-way. **A new $295 inspection fee was added to UR/UC permits effective July 1, 2025** (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting), which sits on top of an existing permit fee that varies. The full guide to UR (utility repair) and UC (utility connection) permits is at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits, and the application goes to URUCpermits@portlandoregon.gov or by phone at 503-823-1026.

The **right-of-way (ROW) permit**: ROW is the public strip that includes the sidewalk, planting strip, and street itself. The UC permit above is the ROW permit for your sewer connection; if your contractor needs to close a sidewalk or street or reserve parking during the work, that's a separate **Temporary Street Use Permit** from PBOT (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting). Most single-lateral connections don't trigger a separate Temporary Street Use Permit; the contractor typically handles this if needed.

---

## Phasing and sequencing

The order matters, and this is where homeowners trip up.

**Step A: Pay or finance your System Development Charges (SDCs)** before any permit can be issued. **PCC 17.36** requires that SDCs be paid or that a financing agreement be in place before issuance of a building permit, sewer connection permit, or plumbing permit (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-405-system-development-charges).

**Step B: Get your plumbing permit and your UC sewer connection permit issued.** These can run in parallel; the UC permit is processed in 1 to 2 business days for typical applications (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits/uc-permit).

**Step C: Make the sewer connection in the street.** Your contractor digs from the city sewer main to your property line, ties in, and PBOT inspects it.

**Step D: Run the new private sewer line to your house** under the plumbing permit, abandon any line going to the cesspool.

**Step E: Decommission the cesspool**, which means pumping it (with a DEQ-licensed pumper) and filling per OAR 340-071-0185 (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0185), with the top 12 to 18 inches left open for inspection (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/septic-decommissioning-permit-application/download).

**Step F: Get the decommissioning inspection.** Inspection code **842**, requested through 503-823-7000 using the IVR number on your permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/septic-decommissioning-permit-application/download).

The City's official guidance is that the connection and the decommissioning generally happen at the same time on the same project, with the contractor coordinating both (https://www.portland.gov/bes/improvements/construction/se-claybourne-sewer-extension-project). You don't have to choose one order legally, but **the practical sequence is: connect to sewer first, then decommission the cesspool**, because once you connect, the cesspool is clearly "no longer in use" which is the trigger for decommissioning under OAR 340-071-0185 (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0185).

---

## Real cost and timeline ranges

**System Development Charges (SDCs)**: The current rate for fiscal year 2025-2026 is **$572 per drainage fixture unit (DFU) for Tier 1 occupancies, with residential developments charged at 60 percent of the Tier 1 rate, working out to about $343 per DFU** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/documents/fy-2025-26-sewer-and-stormwater-charges-and-fees/download and https://www.portland.gov/bes/full-list-environmental-services-rates-and-charges). A typical single-family house has 15 to 25 DFUs, which would put a full SDC at roughly $5,000 to $9,000.

**Important nuance on whether you owe SDCs at all**: ENB-4.05 and PCC 17.36 say SDCs apply to "all development projects that create new or increased demand on the public sewer and drainage system, except for additions and alterations to existing single- and multi-dwelling residential developments that do not add new dwelling units" (https://www.portland.gov/bes/full-list-environmental-services-rates-and-charges). The historical practice on cesspool-to-sewer conversions was to charge full SDCs, as documented in the Claybourne project page (https://www.portland.gov/bes/improvements/construction/se-claybourne-sewer-extension-project). Whether the post-July-2024 rules treat a same-fixture cesspool conversion as "no new or increased demand" is something I'd push BES specifically to confirm in writing for your project; I could not find a single clean-cut authoritative source saying the conversion exemption applies in your exact situation. **Ask BES directly and get the answer in writing before you pay.**

**Septic decommissioning permit fee**: **$935** flat (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/septic-decommissioning-permit-application/download).

**UC sewer connection permit fee**: variable base fee plus the **new $295 inspection fee starting July 1, 2025** (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting). I could not find a single published flat dollar amount for the UC base fee on the public-facing pages; confirm directly via URUCpermits@portlandoregon.gov.

**Private plumbing costs (contractor labor and materials)**: BES says these typically range from **$5,000 to $12,000** for a single-family connection (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/sewer-costs). One Portland homeowner who blogged the experience reported the contractor work for cesspool decommissioning alone (not connection) at $1,800 to $2,500 (https://pdxadditions.com/2017/08/decommissioning-a-cesspool-in-portland/). Older news reports of full conversions in 2017-2018 documented $5,000 to $7,000 in private plumbing costs (https://katu.com/news/local/neighbors-on-the-hook-to-pay-big-fees-to-connect-to-citys-sewer-system).

**All-in real-world range** for a Portland house already adjacent to a sewer main (which is your situation): plan for **$6,000 to $20,000** all-in, with most homeowners landing in the **$10,000 to $15,000** range. That's roughly **the price of a used car to the price of a small kitchen remodel**.

**Timeline**: Septic Program decommissioning permits are typically issued the next business day after payment is posted (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/septic-decommissioning-permit-application/download). UC permits in 1 to 2 business days for normal applications (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits/uc-permit). Contractor scheduling and the actual dig and inspection typically takes **a few weeks to a couple of months from first call to final inspection**, or **roughly one summer's worth of weekends if you're patient**. PP&D's Septic Program is currently noting "occasional delays" due to budget-related staffing shortages (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/septic-decommissioning-permits), so add a buffer.

**Financial assistance**: The City offers two main loan programs for this work, described at https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/financial-assistance-sewer-connection-and-conversion. The **Private Plumbing Loan** carries a **fixed 6.1% simple interest rate, terms of 5 or 10 years, a one-time $90 finance fee, and a $3-per-bill transaction fee**, with no down payment, no credit check, and no early payoff penalty (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/financial-assistance-sewer-connection-and-conversion). The **Safety Net Private Plumbing Loan** is a **1.5% interest hardship loan with a deferred payment option based on age**, qualified to homeowners meeting hardship criteria (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/financial-assistance-sewer-connection-and-conversion). For the SDC portion, the City also offers loans **of up to 20 years secured by a city lien on the property, with no pre-qualification based on income or credit history** (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/sewer-costs).

## Who to contact (and who to call first)

**Call first: Portland Permitting & Development's Septic Program**, because they're the people who run the cesspool/septic decommissioning inspections for all of Multnomah County and can quickly tell you what's actually on file at your address. **Email: septic@portlandoregon.gov. Phone for general questions: 503-823-7300.** Web page: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/septic-decommissioning-permits.

**Call second: BES Sewer & Stormwater questions / PP&D Utilities Engineering at 503-823-7761** for connection questions, SDC questions, and what existing connection records show (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/sewer-costs).

**For sewer lateral / public right-of-way permits (UC and UR): URUCpermits@portlandoregon.gov, phone 503-823-1026** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits).

**For inspections after permit is issued: 503-823-7000** with your permit's IVR number; the decommissioning inspection code is **842** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/septic-decommissioning-permit-application/download).

**Trade permits in person: 1900 SW 4th Ave, Suite 5000, Portland, OR 97201, available Tuesdays 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. and Thursdays 8 a.m. to noon** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/septic-decommissioning-permits).

**DEQ Northwest Region (covers Multnomah County)**: most homeowners will not interact with DEQ directly because Portland acts as DEQ's agent; if you do need DEQ, the onsite wastewater program landing page is https://www.oregon.gov/deq/Residential/Pages/Septic.aspx. I could not find a single direct-line homeowner phone number for DEQ onsite questions on the page itself; the DEQ NW region main number is 503-229-5263.

**Contractor licensing verification: Oregon Construction Contractors Board (CCB) at 503-378-4621 or https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/Pages/index.aspx**, which lets you check if a contractor's license, insurance, and bond are current and whether they've had complaints (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475).

## Common mistakes Portland homeowners make on cesspool failures

**Hiring an unlicensed contractor.** Oregon law requires contractors who do sewer work to be registered with the Oregon CCB, and the city won't issue permits to unregistered ones (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475). Always verify the CCB number, insurance, and bond before signing anything.

**Skipping the DEQ-licensed pumper for the cesspool.** OAR 340-071-0185 requires a DEQ-licensed sewage disposal provider to pump residual liquid and solids before filling, with a copy of the pump receipt provided for inspection (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2020-09/septic_decommissioning_041117.pdf). If your contractor "fills it in" without this step, you'll fail inspection.

**Filling the cesspool with regular dirt.** OAR 340-071-0185 requires "suitable material" (sand, gravel, or approved fill) installed in 1- to 5-foot lifts and tamped or watered for compaction; common soil or yard dirt is not acceptable (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2020-09/septic_decommissioning_041117.pdf).

**Filling all the way up before inspection.** Per the Septic Decommissioning Information Sheet, you must leave the top 12 to 18 inches of the cesspool unfilled and the original pipe exposed so the inspector can see the work; if you cover it, you'll have to dig it back up (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2020-09/septic_decommissioning_041117.pdf).

**Paying SDCs you might not owe.** As noted above, current code language only charges SDCs on "new or increased demand" (https://www.portland.gov/bes/full-list-environmental-services-rates-and-charges). If your house has the same fixtures before and after, push BES on whether the conversion truly creates new demand under post-July-2024 rules. Get the answer in writing.

**Doing right-of-way work without a UC permit.** Any work in the public street touching the city sewer requires a UC permit issued by BES with PBOT inspection; doing it without a permit is a code violation under PCC 17.32 and exposes you to nuisance enforcement and lien risk (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32).

**Hiring a "sewer connection" contractor who isn't bonded for ROW work.** Even properly licensed plumbers may not have the right ROW bonding to work in the street; the BES UR/UC permit page lists insurance and bond requirements at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits.

**Missing the ten-foot foundation rule.** If you ever build new construction within 10 feet of an abandoned cesspool, you'll be required to decommission it as part of that project, sometimes with structural fill and compaction testing per the Septic Decommissioning Information Sheet (https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/2020-09/septic_decommissioning_041117.pdf). If you might do an addition later, decommissioning now (and documenting it) saves headaches.

**Cesspool as a "ticking" hazard even when not in use.** Even an abandoned cesspool can collapse, creating a sinkhole risk in the yard if the lid or sides fail; the PDX Additions account documents this exact failure mode (https://pdxadditions.com/2017/08/decommissioning-a-cesspool-in-portland/). If you suspect yours is failing structurally rather than functionally, decommissioning is still the right answer.

## What to do next (in order)

**1. From your couch, today, look up the historic plumbing records for your address on PortlandMaps.** Go to https://www.portlandmaps.com, search **6624 N Missouri Ave**, click **Permits & Zoning > Permits**, and look for any record describing **"Change from cesspool to sewer"** or **"BES Connection."** Note the IDs and dates. **Cost: FREE. Time: 5 to 10 minutes.** This step alone may tell you that your cesspool is already legally abandoned and your job is just decommissioning, not full connection.

**2. Email PP&D's Septic Program with your findings.** Send to **septic@portlandoregon.gov**. The email script is below. **Cost: FREE. Time: 5 minutes to write, expect a reply within 1 to 5 business days based on current staffing notes** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/septic-decommissioning-permits).

**3. Call BES / PP&D Utilities Engineering at 503-823-7761** to confirm whether your house currently has an active sewer service connection on file with BES, what your sewer billing address shows (a connection on the bill is a strong tell), and whether SDCs would apply to your cesspool decommissioning given current code (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/sewer-costs). **Cost: FREE. Time: 15 to 30 minutes including hold time.**

**4. Have a sewer contractor camera-scope the existing sewer line if your house already shows a connection on file.** A 30-minute video inspection of your private sewer lateral is typically $250 to $500 from local sewer companies and confirms whether your line goes to the public sewer or to the cesspool. Several Portland sewer companies do this; check the **CCB at 503-378-4621 or https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/Pages/index.aspx** to verify each one. **Cost: $250 to $500. Time: 1 day to schedule, 30 minutes on site.**

**5. Get three written bids from CCB-verified contractors** for whatever scope the previous steps tell you is needed (decommissioning only, full conversion, or partial). The City explicitly recommends three bids and provides a sewer contractor list at https://www.portlandoregon.gov/bes/article/455516 (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475). **Cost: FREE. Time: 1 to 3 weeks for site visits and quotes.**

**6. Apply for permits.** Septic decommissioning permit application goes to **septic@portlandoregon.gov**, $935 fee (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/septic-decommissioning-permit-application/download). UC permit application goes to **URUCpermits@portlandoregon.gov**, fees vary plus the $295 inspection fee (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits/uc-permit). **Cost: $935 + UC fees + $295 inspection. Time: 1 to 5 business days.**

**7. If financing, apply for a BES Private Plumbing Loan or Safety Net Loan** before you sign with a contractor; loans are calculated based on the lowest of three bids (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/financial-assistance-sewer-connection-and-conversion). **Cost: $90 finance fee plus 6.1% interest (or 1.5% if you qualify for Safety Net). Time: a few weeks for underwriting.**

**8. Schedule and complete the work, then call inspections at 503-823-7000** with your permit IVR number and inspection code 842 for decommissioning (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/septic-decommissioning-permit-application/download). **Cost: included in permit. Time: a few days from request to inspector arrival.**
