## What to ask the city: ready-to-paste scripts

**Email script for PP&D Septic Program (paste to septic@portlandoregon.gov)**

> Subject: Records and decommissioning question for 6624 N Missouri Ave, Portland OR 97217
>
> Hi,
>
> I'm the resident at 6624 N Missouri Ave, Portland, OR 97217 (Multnomah County tax property R232318). I'm trying to figure out whether my property still has an active cesspool, or whether the cesspool was already legally decommissioned when the house was connected to the public sewer.
>
> When I look up the property on PortlandMaps, I see two historic "BES Connection" plumbing records on file. Can you confirm whether either of those records reflects a completed cesspool-to-sewer conversion, and whether a decommissioning permit was previously issued and inspected?
>
> If the cesspool has not yet been formally decommissioned, I'd like to start a Septic Decommissioning Permit application. Please send me the application form and let me know the next steps.
>
> Thanks very much.

**Phone script for BES / PP&D Utilities Engineering (call 503-823-7761)**

> Hi, I have a question about a single-family house at **6624 N Missouri Ave, 97217**. I think there's a cesspool on the property and I'd like to confirm three things.
>
> First, is this property currently billed for sanitary sewer service, or is it still on a private system?
>
> Second, when I look on PortlandMaps I can see a public combined sewer main about 90 feet away in N Missouri Ave with a manhole tagged unit ID AAK484. Can you confirm whether a sewer connection from my property to that main has already been made and inspected?
>
> Third, if I do need to make a new connection because of a failed cesspool, would I owe full residential System Development Charges, given that the post-July-2024 code only applies SDCs to "new or increased demand" and I wouldn't be adding any new fixtures? I'd appreciate getting the answer in writing if possible.
>
> Thanks for your help.

## Common gotchas (specific to Portland cesspool conversions)

**Old plumbing records say "Decommissioned" but the cesspool is still there.** "Decommissioned" historically meant "abandoned" not "physically filled in," per the PDX Additions account; the brick pit can still be sitting underground (https://pdxadditions.com/2017/08/decommissioning-a-cesspool-in-portland/). If you're doing any future addition or major landscaping, you may need to physically locate, fully fill, and inspect that cesspool before construction.

**Dual cesspools (sanitary plus rain gutter "drywells").** Many old Portland homes have separate cesspool-style pits for rain gutter drainage, also brick-lined and identical-looking; these are often still in use and are not always covered by your sanitary decommissioning (https://pdxadditions.com/2017/08/decommissioning-a-cesspool-in-portland/). If you find one in your yard, ask whether it's still active stormwater infrastructure before touching it.

**Choosing a contractor based on price alone.** BES specifically warns "the lowest bid is not necessarily the best bid" and recommends checking CCB history for complaints (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475). A bad sewer contractor can leave you with a backfilled trench that settles, a leaky tie-in that the city flags during the next sewer line camera survey, or a permit that never gets final-inspected and lingers as an open issue at sale.

**The "party sewer" trap.** If your sewer lateral runs across a neighbor's property or shares a line with another house, you may be on a **nonconforming sewer** under PCC 17.32.020 and may be required to convert to your own conforming connection under PCC 17.33.030.C (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020 and https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33/030). The portal's lateral records at distance zero from your parcel are worth confirming aren't a shared line. ENB-4.27 governs nonconforming sewer conversion (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-427-administrative-rules-bureau).

**Permit expiration.** UR and UC permits expire 180 days after issue, with one extension of up to 180 more days available before expiration only (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits). If your contractor stalls and the permit lapses you may have to re-apply and re-pay.

**Open permit at sale time.** A common nasty surprise during a real estate transaction is an unfinaled plumbing or decommissioning permit. Make sure each permit gets its **Final Inspection Approved** stamp before considering the project complete.

**Tree roots and street trees.** If your sewer lateral passes near a street tree, the work may require a tree permit from Portland Urban Forestry, separate from the UC permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits).

---

## Verify before you spend money

**Pull the actual permit cards before paying anyone.** I gave you property R232318 with two BES Connection records on file, but the metadata could mean a completed connection, abandoned application, or partial work. Don't pay a contractor until you've seen the actual scanned documents at https://www.portlandmaps.com or in person at 1900 SW 4th Ave (https://www.cozyhomeinspection.com/cesspools.html).

**Confirm zoning and overlays at PortlandMaps.** Your zoning summary at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/zoning/ shows R5 base, no plan district, no historic overlay, no environmental overlay. Verify this hasn't changed before paying for plans or contractors; overlays can add design constraints and review fees that materially change project cost.

**Confirm your contractor's CCB status the same week you sign.** Licenses can lapse. Run their CCB number at https://search.ccb.state.or.us/search/ and check insurance and bond status (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475).

**Get the SDC determination in writing.** As noted, whether your conversion triggers full SDCs under post-July-2024 rules is not crystal clear from the public-facing materials; ask BES for a written determination at https://www.portland.gov/bes/full-list-environmental-services-rates-and-charges before you finalize loan paperwork.

**Don't trust contractor permit-fee estimates without checking the city fee schedule.** Permit fees change every July 1; the FY2025-26 rate ordinance is at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/documents/fy-2025-26-sewer-and-stormwater-charges-and-fees/download.

**Be skeptical of "we'll just decommission it for cash" offers.** Skipping the permit means: no DEQ-required pump receipt, no inspection, no record of decommissioning, and a future buyer's inspector or the city's records system will eventually catch it. Title issues and post-sale lawsuits are far more expensive than the $935 permit fee.

## Glossary

**BDS / Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)**: Bureau of Development Services, now operating as Portland Permitting & Development. Issues building, plumbing, mechanical, and septic decommissioning permits on private property. Phone: 503-823-7300. Web: https://www.portland.gov/ppd.

**BES**: Bureau of Environmental Services. Runs the public sewer pipes, stormwater facilities, and treatment plants for the City of Portland. Issues permits for work on public sewer infrastructure. Phone: 503-823-7761. Web: https://www.portland.gov/bes.

**Cesspool**: A buried pit, often brick-lined, that takes raw sewage from a house and lets the liquid seep into surrounding soil. Banned for new construction in Oregon under OAR 340-071-0335 (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0335).

**Combined sewer**: A sewer pipe that carries both sanitary sewage (from toilets and sinks) and stormwater runoff (from streets and roofs) in the same pipe, defined in PCC 17.32.020 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020). Your N Missouri Ave sewer is combined.

**Decommissioning**: The official process of shutting down and filling in an old cesspool, septic tank, or seepage pit so it's no longer a hazard. Required by OAR 340-071-0185 (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0185).

**DEQ**: Oregon Department of Environmental Quality. State agency that writes Oregon's onsite wastewater rules under OAR Chapter 340, Division 71 (https://secure.sos.state.or.us/oard/displayDivisionRules.action?selectedDivision=1479) and delegates day-to-day permitting to local agents like the City of Portland.

**DFU (Drainage Fixture Unit)**: A standardized unit for measuring how much wastewater a plumbing fixture produces. Used to calculate sanitary SDCs under PCC 17.36 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/36).

**E-zone (Environmental Overlay)**: A zoning overlay used in Portland to protect natural resources like streams, wetlands, and wildlife habitat. Your address has none, per https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/zoning/.

**Lateral / side sewer**: The pipe that runs from your house to the city sewer line in the street, also called a building sewer. The portion on your private property is your responsibility; the portion in the public right-of-way has split responsibility between the city and the property owner per PCC 17.32 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32).

**Nonconforming sewer**: A private sewer system that crosses property lines, drains more than one property, or doesn't meet current city standards. Defined in PCC 17.32.020 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020) and governed for conversion by ENB-4.27 (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-427-administrative-rules-bureau).

**OAR**: Oregon Administrative Rule. State-level rules issued by Oregon agencies; cesspool and septic rules are in OAR Chapter 340, Division 71 (https://secure.sos.state.or.us/oard/displayDivisionRules.action?selectedDivision=1479).

**PBOT**: Portland Bureau of Transportation. Owns and inspects the public right-of-way (streets, sidewalks). Inspects your sewer connection work in the street under the UC permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits).

**PCC**: Portland City Code. The City of Portland's local laws, indexed at https://www.portland.gov/code.

**Right-of-way (ROW)**: The strip of public land between your house and the street, including the sidewalk, planting strip, and the street itself. Defined in PCC 17.32.020 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020). Work in the ROW requires a permit.

**SDC (System Development Charge)**: One-time fees the city charges to "hook into" their utility system, intended to recover a property's share of the cost of building and operating major sewer and stormwater facilities. Governed by PCC 17.36 and ENB-4.05 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/36 and https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-405-system-development-charges).

**Seepage pit**: A type of onsite disposal pit similar to a cesspool but used after a septic tank as the absorption component. New seepage pits can replace failing cesspools or seepage pits only on lots too small for standard septic systems, per OAR 340-071-0335 (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0335).

**Septic tank**: A watertight tank that holds sewage from a house, lets solids settle and decompose, and discharges treated liquid effluent to a drainfield. Different from a cesspool, which has no separate tank-and-drainfield distinction.

**UR / UC permit**: Utility Repair (UR) or Utility Connection (UC) permit issued by BES to allow work on the sewer in the public right-of-way. UR is for repairs to existing pipes; UC is for new connections (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits).

---

cesspool-sewer.enrichedQuestion:
I live at 6624 N Missouri Ave, Portland, OR 97217, and my cesspool may be failing. I want to know whether I'm required to connect to the City of Portland sewer system if it fails, and what that whole process actually looks like for me as a homeowner — not as a contractor or engineer.

Please write your answer for a regular Portland homeowner with zero background in city code, permits, or plumbing regulations. Aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level. Treat me as a smart adult who has simply never dealt with this before. Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it (cesspool, decommissioning, BES, BDS, PCC, DEQ, ROW, SDC, lateral, side sewer, etc.). Use everyday words wherever possible — say "the pipe that runs from my house to the city sewer line in the street" before you say "side sewer lateral", and say "fees the city charges to hook into their pipes" before "System Development Charges." Keep paragraphs short. Use bolded keywords in sentences instead of dense tables. Only use a table if you're comparing 3+ real options on the same factors, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above any table. Translate every dollar figure and timeline into something I can plan around (e.g., "about the cost of a used car," "roughly one summer's worth of weekends," "about as long as a kitchen remodel").

**Lead with a plain-English summary in the very first paragraph** — 3 to 5 sentences — that answers directly: if my cesspool fails, do I have to connect to City sewer, and if so, what's the short version of what happens next? No citations, no jargon, no tables in that opening. Just the answer the way a knowledgeable neighbor would tell me over the fence.

Then go deeper and cover the things I probably didn't know to ask:

- **The rule itself.** Is connecting to City sewer mandatory when a cesspool fails in Portland, or are there situations where I can repair, replace with a septic system, or do something else instead? Who decides what counts as "failed"? What role does the **Oregon Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ)** play vs. the City of Portland? What role does the **Bureau of Environmental Services (BES — the city bureau that runs sewers and stormwater)** play vs. the **Bureau of Development Services (BDS — the city bureau that issues building and plumbing permits)**?
- **Which Titles of Portland City Code govern this** — likely Title 17 (Public Improvements), Title 25 (Plumbing), and possibly Title 29 (Property Maintenance), plus any administrative rules on cesspool decommissioning. Tell me what each Title actually controls in plain English next to the number.
- **What's special about my specific address.** 6624 N Missouri Ave is in the **Overlook / Arbor Lodge area of North Portland**. Is there a city sewer line already running in N Missouri Ave that I can connect to, or would I need a longer connection? Are there any zoning overlays, environmental overlays (like the **e-zone / environmental overlay**), historic overlays, or stormwater requirements that affect this property? Tell me how to verify this myself on **PortlandMaps (https://www.portlandmaps.com)** in 2 minutes.
- **What permits I'll likely need and what triggers each one** — the cesspool decommissioning permit, the sewer connection permit, any plumbing permit, any right-of-way (ROW — the strip of public land between my house and the street, including the sidewalk and the street itself) permit if the work crosses the sidewalk or street. Who issues each permit, and roughly what does each one cost?
- **Phasing and sequencing.** What order do these things have to happen in? (E.g., do I have to get the sewer connected and inspected before the cesspool can legally be decommissioned, or the other way around?) What can I do in parallel vs. what has to be sequential?
- **Real cost and timeline ranges** for a typical Portland single-family home doing this — including the connection fee, **System Development Charges (SDCs — one-time fees the city charges to hook into their utility system)**, contractor cost, decommissioning cost, and any financial assistance programs like the BES sewer connection loan or hardship program if those exist.
- **Who I should actually contact**, with phone numbers, email addresses, and web pages — BES, BDS, DEQ, and any specific program (like a cesspool/sewer connection program or a financial assistance program). Tell me which one to call *first*.
- **Common mistakes Portland homeowners make on cesspool failures** — picking the wrong contractor, hiring someone who isn't licensed for decommissioning, missing the DEQ filing, paying SDCs they could have avoided or deferred, doing work in the right-of-way without a permit, etc.

Then give me these specific sections, in this order:

1. **A numbered "What to do next" list, in the order I should actually do it.** Each step should say: who to call or what website to visit, what to bring or have ready, roughly what it costs (mark **FREE** clearly when it's free), and roughly how long it takes. Step 1 should be something I can do today from my couch.

2. **A "What to ask the city" script** I can literally read aloud on the phone or paste into an email — one short script for BES and one short script for BDS, with the actual sentences to say, including my address and what I'm trying to find out. Not "call BES" — the actual words.

3. **A "Common gotchas" section in plain language** — the specific traps Portland homeowners fall into on cesspool-to-sewer conversions.

4. **A "Verify before you spend money" section** with friendly warnings (not legal disclaimers) — things like "zoning and sewer availability for your exact address can differ from what's typical for the neighborhood; check PortlandMaps and call BES before paying for any plans or contractors."

5. **A glossary at the end** defining every acronym and technical term you used more than once (cesspool, decommissioning, septic, lateral / side sewer, BES, BDS, DEQ, PCC, SDC, ROW, e-zone, etc.), in case I jumped to a section.

**Citation rules — this is the most important part, please follow it exactly:**

I am going to copy your entire answer and paste it into a document. Every factual claim — every rule, fee, timeline, phone number, code section, contact — must have its citation written **inline, in the same sentence as the claim**, in this exact format: the specific code section AND a full working `https://...` URL to the authoritative source on portland.gov, oregon.gov/deq, or portlandmaps.com.

Example of what I want: "Cesspools that fail must be decommissioned according to DEQ rules (OAR 340-071-0300, https://secure.sos.state.or.us/oard/displayDivisionRules.action?selectedDivision=1454), and properties within 300 feet of a public sewer are generally required to connect (PCC 17.32.020, https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020)."

Do **not** do any of these:
- Vague references like "Portland City Code", "Title 17", "the BDS website", "city regulations", "DEQ rules" with no specific section number or URL.
- Footnote-style citations like "[1]", "see source above", or numbered references that point to a list at the end.
- A "Sources" or "References" list at the end **instead of** inline citations. (You can include a sources list in addition, but the body must already be fully cited inline.)
- Link text like "[here]" or "[learn more]" — I need the full `https://...` URL written out as plain text so it survives copy-paste.

If you genuinely cannot find a specific code section or URL for a claim, say so directly in that sentence — for example, "I could not find a specific code section for this fee — please confirm with BES at 503-823-7740" — rather than citing something vague. That's much more helpful to me than a fake-looking general citation.

Do not lead with a property data table. Do not stack code section numbers without explaining in plain English right next to them what the rule actually says. Do not use words like "shall", "thereunder", "promulgated", or other legal language — write the way a friendly city employee would explain this to me over coffee.

col1.response:
I'll dig into this carefully. "Tiny house" can mean a few different things in Portland's code, so I want to figure out what's actually possible on your specific lot before answering. Let me first check that I can reach the city's GIS layer, then pull your property data.This is interesting - I'm finding that 174th Avenue is right on the boundary between Portland and Gresham city limits. The exact line matters a lot here because it changes which code applies. Let me dig deeper.I have a strong picture now, but I want to make sure I'm giving you the most current ADU rules and the THOW (tiny house on wheels) policy, since those have changed in recent years. Let me verify those.I have enough now to give you a substantive answer; the short version is that "tiny house" maps to three very different code paths in Portland, and which one fits depends on details about your lot. I'll lay out all three, but first there's a wrinkle worth flagging.

## First: confirm which city you're in

SE 174th Ave is literally the city boundary between Portland and Gresham in your stretch. The City of Portland's official zip-code description for 97233 says Portland extends only "west of 174th, south to Division"; the east side of 174th Ave in the 800 block is Gresham. Your address (831, odd) is most likely on the west side and therefore in Portland, but please don't take my word for that; pull up <https://www.portlandmaps.com/search/?query=831+SE+174th+Ave+Portland+OR> and confirm. If the Property panel returns a base zone like R5 / R7 / R2.5, you're in Portland and everything below applies. If it returns "Gresham" or shows no Portland zoning, the rules are stricter and we'd need a different approach. (Gresham allows permanent foundation tiny homes but treats THOWs as RVs only, with no long-term backyard occupancy.)

## Path 1: tiny house on wheels (by far the easiest)

Portland is unusually permissive here, and this is probably the answer most people don't realize they have. Under the Occupied RV / THOW guidelines tied to Title 33.262, you can place one occupied tiny house on wheels on a residential lot that already has a primary dwelling (you have one). Wheels stay on; it must sit behind the front wall of your house; minimum 5 ft from the main dwelling; RV-style hookups (20-amp GFCI electrical, potable water, sewer tie-in by a licensed plumber). It does not count against density, no SDCs, no big permit set; you can host family or rent long-term (no short-term rentals). This is the path that delivers a livable second unit fastest and cheapest.

## Path 2: detached ADU (permanent backyard cottage)

Title 33.205 governs this. The size cap for your specific property is the gotcha: maximum is the lesser of 800 sq ft or 75% of your primary dwelling's living area. Your house is reportedly 960 sq ft (per public records; verify), so 75% = **720 sq ft** would be your detached-ADU ceiling. Two exceptions worth knowing: (a) basement conversions in a primary dwelling that's been on the site at least 5 years aren't subject to the size cap (yours easily qualifies on age, but a 1947 home may not have a usable basement); (b) recent code updates allow ADUs up to 1,000 sq ft on lots over 5,000 sq ft in some configurations, and an Adjustment review can push beyond 800 sq ft. Other key rules: 20 ft height (15 ft within setbacks), 5 ft side/rear setbacks, must sit behind the rear wall of the main house, and SDC waivers are available if you record a 10-year covenant against short-term rentals (saves ~$15K-$25K).

## Path 3: middle housing (the path most people miss)

Under Portland's Residential Infill Project, R2.5 / R5 / R7 lots can have **up to four units** total (counting the existing house). So instead of one ADU, you might be able to add a triplex-style configuration, or two ADUs plus a duplex conversion of your existing structure. This is the highest-value path if your lot supports it, and the FAR bonus actually grows as you add units (0.5 → 0.6 → 0.7 in R5). Worth at least sketching out before you commit to a single-ADU mindset.

## What you need to look up to lock in answers

From the PortlandMaps Property panel, please grab and paste back: **base zone** (R5 / R7 / R2.5 / etc.), **overlay codes** (any lowercase letters; "c" or "p" environmental zones would significantly constrain things), **lot size in sq ft**, and the **Hazards** panel (flood / landslide / liquefaction would matter for any foundation work). With those four things I can tell you exactly what's allowed, what setbacks apply, and roughly what your buildable footprint looks like.

## Practical next step

If you want zero ambiguity, Portland Permitting & Development offers free 15-minute planner consultations at 503-823-7300; bring your address and a rough idea of what you want to build and they'll tell you which path is cleanest. Worth its weight in gold before you spend money on plans.

What's the actual goal here, by the way: family member, rental income, downsize-and-rent-the-main-house, or something else? That changes which of these three paths is genuinely best, not just which is technically allowed.

Cites Title 33.262 and Title 33.205 by Title+chapter but no exact section numbers or URLs to specific code sections.

col2.response:
I pulled the public data for your lot, then cross-referenced it against the current Title 33 zoning code. Here's what I found, what it means, and a path you may not have considered.

## Your lot in numbers

| Field | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Base zone | **R7** (single-dwelling, 7,000 sq ft min) | PortlandMaps Zoning layer |
| Overlay | **`h`** (Aircraft Landing) | PortlandMaps Zoning layer |
| Plan district / Historic / Conservation | None | PortlandMaps Zoning layer |
| Lot area | **8,083 sq ft** (0.186 acres) | Multnomah taxlot record |
| Approximate dimensions | ~75 ft frontage on 174th × ~108 ft deep | Computed from parcel geometry |
| Existing house | 960 sq ft, built 1947, 1 unit | Multnomah assessor |
| Natural hazards on parcel | None (no flood, landslide, or 10%+ slope polygons hit) | PortlandMaps Natural Hazards layer |

Yes, you can build something here. The interesting question is **which kind** of "tiny house," because Portland has three different legal paths and they have very different costs, timelines, and tradeoffs.

## Path 1: Detached ADU (foundation-built tiny house)

This is what most people mean by "build a tiny house in the yard." Permanent structure, real foundation, full residential code compliance.

**Maximum size on your lot: 720 sq ft.** The rule is "lesser of 800 sq ft or 75% of primary dwelling living area." Your primary is 960 sq ft, so 75% × 960 = 720 sq ft caps you. There's one workaround: a **basement ADU inside the existing 1947 house** is exempt from the 75% rule (since the house is more than 5 years old) and can go up to 800 sq ft (Title 33.205.040.C). That's not "in the yard" but it's a legitimate way to get to the full 800.

**Other R7 standards that will matter** (verify each on the current chapter at portland.gov/code/33/100s/110 before you design; section numbers drift):
- Front setback: 20 ft (the 1947 house likely already defines this)
- Side setbacks: 5 ft
- Rear setback: 5 ft
- Detached ADU must sit at least 40 ft from the front lot line, OR behind the rear wall of the main house (33.205.040.C.3); on a 108 ft deep lot this is easy
- Height: 25 ft for a detached accessory structure (per current 33.110)
- Building coverage and FAR: well within limits for adding ~720 sq ft on this lot
- One ADU is allowed by right; **two** are allowed if 174th Ave is a city-maintained street (very likely yes for a named arterial, but confirm at portland.gov/transportation/development/maintenance-rip)

**Permitting:** Full BDS building permit, design review against the Community Design Standards (33.218), system development charges (which Portland may waive if you commit not to use it as a short-term rental for 10 years).

## Path 2: Tiny House on Wheels (THOW) as an occupied RV

This is the **easier and cheaper** path; most people don't realize it's available. Portland treats THOWs as occupied RVs under Title 33.262, **not** as ADUs, so you skip the building permit and SDC entirely.

**What's required:**
- One occupied RV/THOW per residential lot, and the lot must already have a primary dwelling (you do)
- Parked **behind the front façade** of your house (i.e., not in the front yard)
- At least **5 ft from the main house**
- If non-motorized (most THOWs), can sit on compacted gravel; if motorized RV, must be on paved surface
- 20-amp GFCI electrical hookup, potable water connection, and approved sewage disposal (sewer hookup, holding tank pump-out, or composting toilet under specific rules)
- Must remain **mobile** (wheels stay on, on its chassis); does not count toward density
- **Long-term occupancy is allowed; short-term rentals are not** under this program

**Caveat to double-check:** the city's policy guidance document on this (separate from Title 33) is updated periodically. Search portland.gov for "Guidelines for Occupied RVs and Tiny Homes on Wheels on Private Property" before committing. The version in force when you apply is what matters; some details (sewage handling especially) have moved over the last few years.

## Path 3: The one most people miss — a cottage cluster

Your 8,083 sq ft R7 lot is a textbook candidate for a **cottage cluster** (Title 33.110.265 references), which is a development of **3 to 16 small units around shared open space**, allowed by right in R5/R7/R2.5/R10 on sites under 40,000 sq ft. This is a route to multiple tiny houses on one lot, not just one.

The catch: it's a **redevelopment**, not an addition; the site usually gets planned as one project rather than added to incrementally. The 1947 house's role would need a careful design pass (sometimes existing structures can stay, sometimes they're demolished). This path requires real architectural and planner involvement, but it's how you'd get from "one tiny house" to "three or four tiny houses generating rental income or housing extended family." Worth knowing about before you commit to Path 1.

## The aircraft landing overlay is mostly a red herring here

The `h` overlay flags your lot as being in the PDX flight path. For **residential zones**, Title 33.400.030 says the base zone height limit governs, not the overlay's height surface. So the practical effect on a single-story or two-story tiny house is **zero**. The overlay would only bite if you tried to build something tall enough to trigger FAA review (you won't).

## What I'd verify before spending money

1. **City-maintained street status of 174th Ave** at portland.gov/transportation/development/maintenance-rip. Affects whether a second ADU is on the table.
2. **Sewer connection** (BES). Public data confirms sewer mains in the area but not your specific connection; check your utility records or call BES.
3. **Current setback and ADU figures** in the live Title 33.110 and 33.205 chapters; section numbers shift across code updates and what I cited above is current as of recent updates but moves.
4. **Free 15-minute planner consultation** at PP&D (503-823-7300). Genuinely useful; they'll tell you about constraints public data doesn't show (e.g., easements, prior land use decisions, neighbor disputes on file). For your specific lot this is the highest-leverage 15 minutes you can spend before drawing anything.

## Quick comparison

| | Foundation ADU | THOW (RV path) | Cottage cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max size per unit | 720 sq ft (or 800 if basement) | ~399 sq ft typical THOW | Varies by design |
| Permits | Full BDS building permit | None (RV, not building) | Full land use review |
| Cost order of magnitude | $150K-$300K+ | $60K-$150K | $500K+ |
| Timeline | 6-18 months | Weeks (after pad/hookups) | 12-24+ months |
| Counts as a dwelling unit | Yes | No | Yes |
| Reversible | No | Yes | No |

If "tiny house" to you means a movable, simple, fast solution, **Path 2 is the best-kept secret**. If you want a real second dwelling that adds long-term value to the property, **Path 1**. If you're thinking bigger picture (income property, family compound, a small infill project), **Path 3** is genuinely on the table for this lot in a way it isn't for most Portland properties.

Want me to dig deeper into any of these — for example, sketch out where on the lot a detached ADU could actually sit given the setbacks, or pull the specific sewer/water proximity data for utility hookup planning?

# Tiny House Feasibility: 831 SE 174th Ave, Portland, OR 97233

*Prepared with current PortlandMaps data and a check against current Title 33 chapters on portland.gov. Effective date of this snapshot: May 1, 2026. Code amends multiple times per year; verify any rule before relying on it.*

---

## Your lot, confirmed from city data

| Field | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Address | 831 SE 174TH AVE, Portland, OR 97233 | PortlandMaps geocode |
| Property ID | R337949 | PortlandMaps assessor |
| State ID (taxlot) | 1S3E06AB 8800 | PortlandMaps |
| Neighborhood | Centennial (East Portland) | BDS Property layer |
| Council District | 1 | BDS Property layer |
| Base zone | R7 (Single-Dwelling 7,000) | PortlandMaps Zoning |
| Overlay | `h` (Aircraft Landing) | PortlandMaps Zoning |
| Plan district | None | PortlandMaps Zoning |
| Historic / Conservation district | None | PortlandMaps Zoning |
| Comp Plan designation | R7 (matches zone, no pending change) | PortlandMaps Zoning |
| Lot area | 8,083 sq ft (BDS) / 0.19 ac (assessor) | BDS Property + assessor |
| Existing dwelling | 960 sq ft, built 1947 | Multnomah assessor |
| FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area | No | BDS Property |
| FEMA Flood Way | No | BDS Property |
| Earthquake Liquefaction Hazard | No | BDS Property |
| Regulated Landslide Hazard | No | BDS Property |
| Twenty Percent Steep Slope | No | BDS Property |
| Drainage district | None | BDS Property |
| Permit history | 2000 electrical (100 A service); 2003 zoning confirmation; 2009 weeds complaint (closed) | PortlandMaps permits |
| Listed owner of record | KARAM, CHAFEK & JANOURA, ANESA | BDS Property layer |

**One thing to verify before you do anything else.** The assessor / BDS Property layer lists the owner of record as "KARAM, CHAFEK & JANOURA, ANESA" with the mailing address at the property itself. You described 831 SE 174th as "my property"; assessor records sometimes lag, transfers occasionally fail to record, or the property may be held in a name you do not recognize at first glance (e.g., spouse's pre-marriage name, family member, LLC). If the listed owner is not you or someone you control, stop reading and resolve that first; nothing below matters until ownership is confirmed.

## Quick read (the headline answers)

You have a clean lot for either an **ADU on a permanent foundation** (the conventional path; up to ~720 sq ft here) or an **occupied tiny house on wheels** (the much faster, much cheaper path; not a permanent dwelling, but legal long-term). All FEMA, landslide, liquefaction, and steep-slope flags are clear; the only overlay (`h`, Aircraft Landing) does not restrict residential height in R zones; the lot exceeds the 7,000 sq ft R7 minimum; and there is no plan district or historic overlay to navigate. The fastest, cheapest path by far is a tiny house on wheels in the back yard. The path that produces a permanent, financeable dwelling that adds resale value is a detached ADU. The two are governed by entirely different parts of the code.

---

## 1. What kind of "tiny house" can you build?

Portland treats these as three completely different things. The law sees no overlap.

### A. Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) on a permanent foundation

**Governing chapter: Title 33.205 (Accessory Dwelling Units).** Live link: `https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205`. City plain-language page: `https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/adu-zoning`.

A real, permanent dwelling unit; counts as a building under Title 24; permitted, inspected, financeable, taxed as part of the property. Allowed by right in R7 on a lot with an existing house.

Key rules that bind your project:

- **Maximum size: the lesser of 800 sq ft, or 75% of the primary dwelling's living area** (33.205.040). With your existing house at 960 sq ft assessor-reported, your ADU cap is **720 sq ft** (75% × 960). Worth confirming the 960 figure with BDS or your county tax record before designing; if your house is actually larger than the assessor shows (additions, finished basement that did or did not get permits), the cap can move up to 800.
- ADUs are allowed in R2.5, R5, R7, R10, R20 by right.
- After Portland's 2025 RIP2-related code update, **two ADUs may be allowed** on a single lot if the lot meets the minimum size in Table 205-2 and fronts a city-maintained street. Your lot fronts SE 174th Ave (a city street); whether you qualify for two units depends on the table values and the 40-foot placement rule, which the city consolidated into 33.205 in 2025. For a single detached ADU in R7 you are not in question; for two units, ask BDS to confirm.
- Cannot be in the `z` (Constrained Sites) overlay. You are in `h` (Aircraft Landing) only, so that exclusion does not apply.
- Use as primary residence: yes. Long-term rental: yes. Short-term rental (STR): yes, as a Type A accessory short-term rental under **33.207**, but using STRs forfeits the ADU-SDC waiver (see §5).

### B. Tiny House on Wheels (THOW) / occupied recreational vehicle

**Governing material: Title 33 RV / Tiny House on Wheels provisions** (currently linked from city plain-language page; the legacy chapter pointer was 33.262, the 2021 Shelter-to-Housing-Continuum amendments updated the placement rules, and the city's authoritative reference today is the policy page at `https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/zoning-code-overview/occupied-rvs-and-tiny-houses-wheels`). Verify the current code section number with that page; section numbers have moved.

A vehicle, not a building. Not a Title 24 structure, no ADU permit, no SDCs, not part of the residential density count. Treated as an occupied RV.

Key rules that bind your project:

- **One occupied RV / THOW per lot** that already has a primary dwelling (your 1947 house qualifies). Vacant lots: not allowed.
- Must be parked **behind the front architectural plane of the primary dwelling**.
- **At least 5 feet from the main dwelling** (per current city guidance).
- Motorized vehicles must park on a paved surface; non-motorized THOWs may park on compacted gravel or similar stable ground.
- If the unit has internal plumbing, the site needs a **permitted RV sewer dump (cleanout) and a permitted water connection**; if the unit draws power, it needs a **permitted electrical hookup**. The hookups need permits, not the THOW itself.
- **Long-term occupancy: yes.** Use as primary residence: legally allowed. Long-term rental income: yes. **Short-term rental: NOT permitted under this program.**
- Does **not** count toward the lot's residential density. You can have your house plus a THOW plus an ADU, in principle, on the same lot.
- No certifications or inspections required of the THOW itself; ANSI A119.5 / RVIA-built units are typical.

### C. "Tiny home" as primary (or only) dwelling

**Governing material: Title 33.110 single-dwelling standards + Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC) Appendix Q (small homes ≤ 400 sq ft) or the standard ORSC for larger units.** State code, not city code, governs the actual building specs; Title 33 governs siting, setbacks, height.

A "tiny home" built on a permanent foundation, occupied as the only dwelling on the lot, is just a small single-family house. There is no separate "tiny home" zoning category; it is a house, and 33.110 applies to it identically. ORSC Appendix Q allows small homes ≤ 400 sq ft with a relaxed code (one loft, ladder access, smaller stair / ceiling allowances). Above 400 sq ft, full ORSC applies. There is no minimum house size in Portland's code that would block a small primary dwelling, but you would still meet R7 setbacks, height, and FAR.

You already have a primary dwelling, so this option is not relevant to you unless you demolish the existing house.

### Comparison

| | ADU (foundation) | THOW (wheels) | Tiny home as primary dwelling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Code | 33.205 | Title 33 occupied-RV provisions + city guidance | 33.110 + ORSC |
| Building permit | Yes (Title 24) | No (vehicle) | Yes |
| SDCs | Yes (waiver available) | No | Yes |
| Max size on your lot | ~720 sq ft (75% of 960) | Limited by ANSI / DMV class | R7 standards |
| Primary residence | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Long-term rental | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Short-term rental | Yes (loses SDC waiver) | **No** | Yes |
| Counts toward density | Yes | No | Yes |
| Typical timeline | 4-9 months | weeks | 6-12 months |
| Rough cost (Portland 2026) | $150K-$300K+ | $30K-$120K incl. hookups | varies, ~$200/sf+ |

---

## 2. Zoning detail: R7 + `h` overlay

### Base zone R7 (Single-Dwelling 7,000)

**Chapter 33.110.** Live link: `https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110`. PDF: `https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/33.110-sd-zones.pdf`.

R7's development standards live in tables in Chapter 33.110 (typically referenced as Table 110-2 et al.). The values that drive a tiny-house project are:

- **Minimum lot size for development: 7,000 sq ft.** Your lot is 8,083 sq ft (over).
- **Maximum building height: 30 feet** for a primary dwelling. Detached accessory structures (and detached ADUs) are typically capped lower per 33.110.250 and 33.205; the long-running practical cap for a detached ADU is around 20-24 ft, but the exact current number must be read off the live chapter (Portland updated accessory structure / ADU height rules during the 2024-2025 amendment cycle).
- **Setbacks (typical R7):** front 10 ft; sides 5 ft; rear 5 ft for accessory structures, larger for primary structures. Confirm the table values when you fetch 33.110, since RIP changed several of these.
- **Maximum building coverage and FAR:** check Table 110-3 / 110-5 in the live chapter. With a 960 sq ft existing footprint plus a ~720 sq ft ADU footprint on an 8,083 sq ft lot, you are well under any plausible coverage ratio.
- **Detached accessory structures:** see **33.110.250** for the per-zone rules and **33.205** for ADU-specific siting (which often relaxes the accessory structure height cap for ADUs).

### `h` Aircraft Landing Overlay

**Chapter 33.400.** Live link: `https://www.portland.gov/code/33/400s/400`.

Plain reading of 33.400 confirms that **in residential, commercial/mixed-use, IR, and CI1 zones, structures are regulated by the base zone height limits, not the overlay's heights**; the overlay only applies its own height limits in employment, industrial, CI2, and open-space zones. So `h` is informational here. It does not change what you can build at this lot. (If you ever wanted to put a ham radio tower or similar structure that approached the overlay cap, the overlay would matter; for an ADU or THOW, it does not.)

### Density: how many units?

You are not in a plan district, so 33.110 governs density. With the post-RIP and post-RIP2 framework, your R7 lot allows:

- 1 house (existing) + 1 ADU: yes, by right.
- 1 house + 2 ADUs: possibly, after the 2025 update; depends on lot size threshold in 33.205 Table 205-2 and the 40-ft placement rule. Confirm with BDS.
- Duplex / triplex / fourplex via RIP: middle-housing options under 33.110 are theoretically open in R7, but the practical economics on a 1947 single-family lot of 8,083 sq ft usually do not pencil unless you are demolishing or doing a middle-housing land division (33.644). Outside scope of a tiny-house question, but worth knowing the door exists.

A THOW is not counted toward density, so it stacks on top of any of the above.

---

## 3. Permits and approval order

### Likely permits (ADU path)

| Permit | Bureau | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Building permit (NSFR / ADU) | Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D, formerly BDS) | Title 24; structural, ORSC, energy code |
| Zoning review | PP&D Land Use Services | 33.205, 33.110 standards check |
| Plumbing permit | PP&D | Title 25 |
| Electrical permit | PP&D | Title 26 |
| Mechanical / HVAC permit | PP&D | Title 27 |
| Sewer connection / Branch | Bureau of Environmental Services (BES) | side-sewer connection, may require a new lateral |
| Stormwater review | BES Site Development | 2020 Stormwater Management Manual; small-site form path is typical for an ADU |
| Water service | Portland Water Bureau | meter sizing, possible new tap |
| Driveway / curb cut (only if changing access) | PBOT | encroachment / curb-cut permit |
| Tree review | Urban Forestry (under PP&D; Title 11) | required if the ADU footprint or grading is within a regulated tree's root protection zone, or any street tree is affected |
| Fire / Title 31 review | Portland Fire & Rescue | typically rolled into building permit; access and water flow checks |
| Address assignment | PP&D | a new ADU gets its own address (e.g., 831A) |

### Likely permits (THOW path)

Far simpler; the unit itself needs no city permit. You need:

- **Electrical permit** (Title 26) for the dedicated outdoor hookup.
- **Plumbing permit** (Title 25) for the water spigot and the RV-style sewer cleanout / dump.
- Possibly a **mechanical permit** if you tie in propane / gas.
- No zoning / building permit; the zoning check is informal verification that the placement (behind front plane, 5 ft from house, paved or compacted-gravel pad) complies.

### Sequence (ADU path)

1. **Free 15-minute PP&D consultation** (do this first; details in §6). Walk in with your address and a one-page sketch.
2. (Optional) **Early Assistance** meeting if you want a written summary; small fee.
3. **Pick a design.** Strongly consider Portland's free pre-approved detached ADU plans (`https://www.portland.gov/ppd`; search "ADU pre-approved plans"). They cut weeks off review and reduce design costs.
4. **Submit building permit + zoning review** through Portland Permitting Online (DevHub).
5. **Submit trade permits** (plumbing, electrical, mechanical) at the same time or after; one set of inspections is easier if filed together.
6. **BES / Water / PBOT / Urban Forestry** reviews run in parallel during the building permit review.
7. **Permit issuance.** Pay SDCs (or record the SDC-waiver covenant; see §5).
8. **Construction with inspections at framing, plumbing rough-in, electrical rough-in, mechanical rough-in, insulation, drywall, final.**
9. **Final inspection / Certificate of Occupancy.** ADU now legal to occupy.

### Type II / III review possibility

Almost no chance for a standard detached ADU on this lot. Type II (Adjustment, 33.805) is only triggered if you cannot meet a development standard and need a discretionary deviation (e.g., a setback you cannot meet because of an existing structure). Type III (Conditional Use, design review) is not in play in R7 for an ADU. Environmental review (33.430) does not apply because you have no `c` or `p` overlay.

---

## 4. Site-specific gotchas for THIS lot

The good news first; the city's data on your parcel says:

- **No FEMA flood hazard, no flood way.** Johnson Creek is south of you but does not reach this lot.
- **No regulated landslide hazard, no liquefaction hazard, no >20% steep slope.** Unusual cleanliness for east Portland; treat it as a gift.
- **No environmental zone (`c` or `p`).** No environmental review path triggered.
- **No historic / conservation district, not a contributing structure.** No design review.
- **No plan district.** 33.110 + 33.205 are the ruling chapters, full stop.
- **No drainage district.** No special stormwater jurisdiction.
- **Permit history is light** (electrical service upgrade in 2000, a zoning confirmation, a weed complaint). No prior unpermitted ADU work to clean up; the property looks straightforward in this respect.

Now the things that warrant active attention:

- **Trees (Title 11).** The PortlandMaps data layer I queried does not include private trees on the parcel; it only shows public street trees. Any tree ≥ 12" DBH on the lot, or any street tree in the right-of-way along SE 174th Ave, can pull Title 11 into the project. Walk the lot, measure trunks at 4.5 ft above grade, and bring the count to your PP&D consultation. Mitigation can require replanting or fee-in-lieu if removal is unavoidable.
- **Stormwater (BES SWMM).** New impervious area triggers stormwater management. For a typical ADU under ~1,500 sq ft of new impervious (footprint + driveway extension), the **Simplified Approach** in the 2020 Stormwater Management Manual usually applies: a sized soakage trench or drywell on-site. If you can avoid expanding the driveway, the rule gets easier.
- **Sewer.** SE 174th Ave is built-out; the city sewer main is almost certainly in the street. Confirm via PortlandMaps' Utilities panel or by asking BES Permit Center (503-823-7740). Expect a Sewer Branch Permit for either a new lateral (if the ADU is too far from the existing house lateral to share) or a re-tap. If the existing house is on a private septic system (uncommon at this address but worth checking), the rules change materially; you would need to confirm or extend public sewer service first.
- **Foundation.** A detached ADU requires a permanent foundation per Title 24 / ORSC. If you build on a permanent foundation, you have an ADU (33.205). If you build on a trailer with wheels remaining attached, you have a THOW. The foundation/no-foundation distinction is the legal hinge between the two paths; you cannot "convert" a THOW to an ADU later without going back through the full ADU permit and effectively rebuilding to building code.
- **Setback between dwellings.** For an ADU, the setback to the existing house follows accessory-structure standards in 33.110.250 plus any 33.205-specific separation. Typical practical minimum: 6 ft, sometimes 10 ft when one structure has windows facing the other (fire code Title 31 also drives this). For a THOW: 5 ft minimum from the main dwelling per current city guidance.
- **Parking.** Portland eliminated minimum off-street parking requirements citywide in 2022. No on-site parking is required for an ADU at this address. Existing parking serving the primary house can remain, but it does not need to be replaced or augmented for the ADU.
- **Utilities: separate vs. shared meters.** ADUs may share or have separate water and electrical meters. Separate metering enables sub-metered tenant billing but adds significant cost ($5-15K typical). Sewer is always one connection per parcel.
- **Existing 100 A electrical service.** The 2000 permit shows your house has a 100 A service. A new ADU typically requires a service upgrade to 200 A or a separate ADU subpanel fed from an upgraded main; budget $4-10K for that work plus PGE coordination. This is a common surprise; you read it here first.
- **Address assignment.** PP&D will assign a unit address (probably 831A SE 174th Ave) at permit issuance.
- **Aircraft Landing overlay (`h`).** As covered, no restriction on residential structures here. Mentioning only because PortlandMaps will show it and it can look alarming until you read 33.400.

---

## 5. Cost and timeline reality check (Portland 2026)

These are typical ranges, not quotes for your project. Real numbers come from a contractor walk-through and current PP&D fee schedules.

**SDCs (System Development Charges) for a new ADU.** Portland has historically waived ADU SDCs entirely if the owner records a 10-year covenant against using the unit as a Type A short-term rental (`https://www.portland.gov/ppd`; search "ADU SDC waiver"). Without the waiver, expect SDCs in the **$15K-$25K** range across BES, Water, Parks, and Transportation. Confirm waiver mechanics and current covenant form at intake; the waiver has been periodically extended by city council.

**Permit fees.** Building, plumbing, electrical, mechanical, and zoning fees on an ADU typically total **$6K-$12K** including plan review. Add ~$1,500-$3,500 for sewer branch permit and ~$500-$2,000 for water service work depending on whether a new tap is needed.

**Plan review timeline.** Pre-approved-plan ADU: roughly **6-10 weeks** start to permit issuance once a complete application is submitted. Custom-design ADU: **3-6 months** typical. THOW hookup permits: **a few weeks**.

**Common reasons projects get stuck.**

1. Incomplete site plan; missing existing structure dimensions, tree locations, or grading information.
2. Stormwater approach not specified or inappropriate to the soils (drywell sizing).
3. Sewer lateral location unknown; BES asks for an as-built or a video inspection.
4. Setback off by inches; a survey would have caught it.
5. Tree-protection-zone overlap with the proposed footprint.
6. Existing unpermitted work on the primary house surfaced during review (records check often catches this).

---

## 6. Who to call, in what order

1. **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D), General Information: 503-823-7300.** Hours Monday-Friday 8 AM - 5 PM. Schedule a **free 15-minute planning consultation** before doing anything else. Ask specifically about: ADU vs. THOW for this lot, two-ADU eligibility, current SDC waiver status, and whether your lot needs a survey before design.
2. **PP&D Inspections: 503-823-7388** (PPDinspections@portlandoregon.gov). For inspection scheduling once you have permits.
3. **BES Permit Center: 503-823-7740** (sewer branch and stormwater questions, 1900 SW 4th Ave).
4. **Portland Water Bureau Customer Service: 503-823-7770** (meter sizing, new tap for ADU).
5. **PBOT Development Permitting: 503-823-7002** (only if you change the curb cut; for sharing the existing driveway, not needed).
6. **Urban Forestry: 503-823-8733** (tree permits, regulated trees on the parcel or in the ROW; Title 11).
7. **Portland Fire & Rescue Plan Review:** typically routed through PP&D; a separate call is rarely needed for a single ADU.

The free 15-minute appointment is the single most valuable thing you can do before spending a dollar on plans. Bring: address, lot dimensions, a hand-sketched site plan showing the existing house and proposed ADU / THOW location, and your questions. They will tell you within those 15 minutes whether anything in your plan kills it. Schedule via `https://www.portland.gov/ppd` (search "schedule appointment").

---

## 7. Plain-language next steps

Numbered. Do them in this order. Items 1-5 are free or near-free.

1. **Confirm ownership.** Pull a Multnomah County tax statement (`https://multcoproptax.com`) for parcel R337949 / 1S3E06AB 8800 and confirm the listed owner is you. If not, resolve before doing anything else.
2. **Verify on PortlandMaps yourself.** Go to `https://www.portlandmaps.com/search/?query=831+SE+174th+Ave` and click the parcel. Spot-check the zoning (R7), overlay (`h`), and the hazard flags (all "No") against this document. Save screenshots or print the property report.
3. **Decide ADU vs. THOW.** ADU = financeable, permanent, ~$200K+, 4-9 months. THOW = ~$30-100K, weeks not months, but not a permanent dwelling. This is the single biggest decision; everything else flows from it.
4. **Walk the lot with a tape measure and a notebook.** Note: setbacks from existing house to lot lines, dimensions of the existing house footprint, location and approximate trunk diameter of every tree, location of the existing driveway, location of the sewer cleanout (often a white plastic cap near the house), location of the electrical meter and main panel, slope of the back yard.
5. **Schedule the free 15-minute PP&D consultation** at 503-823-7300 (also book Early Assistance if you want it in writing). Bring the items from step 4 plus this document; ask the planner to confirm the 720 sq ft ADU cap, the two-ADU eligibility for this lot, and the current SDC waiver status.
6. **(ADU path only) Survey the lot.** $1.5K-$3K. A current survey eliminates the most common review delay (setbacks off by inches).
7. **(ADU path only) Choose between pre-approved plans and custom design.** Pre-approved saves weeks and design fees; custom gets you exactly what you want.
8. **Get bids from at least two contractors.** Look specifically at: experience with Portland ADU permitting, electrical service upgrade scope, sewer connection scope.
9. **Submit the building permit application** (DevHub) with full plan set, site plan, and trade permits if filing concurrently. Pay intake fees.
10. **Plan review correction loop.** Expect one or two correction cycles. Respond fast; reviewers move on if you delay.
11. **Permit issuance.** Pay SDCs or record the SDC-waiver covenant.
12. **Construction with inspections** at each milestone (framing, rough plumbing, rough electrical, rough mechanical, insulation, drywall, final).
13. **Final inspection and Certificate of Occupancy.** ADU is now legal to occupy.

THOW path replaces steps 6-13 with: build or buy the unit, prep the pad, file electrical and plumbing hookup permits, install hookups, pass inspections on the hookups only, move in.

---

## 8. Accuracy caveats

### What is verified from authoritative city data (Path A: PortlandMaps ArcGIS)
- Address geocoding, property ID, state ID
- Base zone R7
- Overlay `h` (Aircraft Landing) only
- No plan district
- No historic / conservation district
- No environmental zone
- All hazard flags (FEMA flood, flood way, liquefaction, landslide, steep slope) returning "No"
- Lot area 8,083 sq ft per BDS, 0.19 ac per assessor
- Existing dwelling 960 sq ft, built 1947
- Permit history (3 records since 2000)
- Owner of record (KARAM, CHAFEK & JANOURA, ANESA)

### What is cited from current Title 33 / city pages on portland.gov
- 33.205 ADU sizing rule (lesser of 800 sq ft or 75% of primary dwelling)
- 33.205 zone eligibility (R2.5 through R20)
- 33.205 Constrained Sites (`z`) overlay exclusion
- 33.205 short-term-rental allowance via 33.207
- 33.400 Aircraft Landing overlay non-application to residential height
- City's "Occupied RVs and Tiny Houses on Wheels" guidance page (current rules; the underlying code section number for THOWs has moved between 33.262 and other locations across amendments; verify the current section at the city's policy page)
- 2025 RIP2 update introducing two-ADU allowance

### What you must verify with the city before relying on it
1. **The specific 33.110 R7 standards table values** (front/side/rear setbacks, height, FAR, building coverage, accessory structure rules at 33.110.250) at the time you apply. I have given typical numbers; the live chapter is the source of truth and amendments do happen mid-year.
2. **The current ADU-SDC waiver mechanism and whether it is still active.** Council renews this; status can change.
3. **The current 33.205 minimum lot sizes** in Tables 205-1 and 205-2 (drives whether your lot qualifies for one ADU or two).
4. **The current code section governing occupied RVs / THOWs.** The city's plain-language policy page is the authoritative reference; it will name the current section number.
5. **Whether your existing house is actually 960 sq ft or has unpermitted additions** that change the 75% calculation.
6. **Whether you are on city sewer** (the BDS Property layer does not directly answer this; PortlandMaps' Utilities panel or a BES inquiry will).
7. **Tree inventory on the parcel.** I cannot see private trees; you need to walk the lot.
8. **Current PBOT requirements** if you ever change the curb cut.
9. **Pre-approved plan availability and current list.** Portland updates this catalog periodically.

### General disclosure
None of this is legal advice; I am not a lawyer or a permit consultant. Code amendments ship multiple times per year. Any rule cited here should be verified against the live chapter on portland.gov before you commit money to plans or construction. The free 15-minute PP&D consultation exists exactly for this purpose; use it before spending a dollar.

---

*Sources: PortlandMaps ArcGIS (Public/Zoning, Public/BDS_Property, Public/Natural_Hazards), PortlandMaps assessor + permit APIs (R337949), Title 33 chapters via portland.gov, City "Accessory dwelling unit (ADU) zoning requirements" page, City "Occupied Recreational Vehicles and Tiny Houses on Wheels" page. Snapshot date: May 1, 2026.*

Plain language with jargon defined, comparison table, and explicit verification caveats make it accessible to a non-expert homeowner.

tiny-house.enrichedQuestion:
I want to build a tiny house at my property at 831 SE 174th Ave, Portland, OR (in the Hazelwood/Centennial area of east Portland). I'm a homeowner with no code or construction background, and I want plain-language guidance I can actually act on, plus specific citations so I can verify everything with the city.

Please answer the following, citing specific Portland City Code sections (Title 33 Zoning, Title 24 Building, Title 25 Plumbing, Title 26 Electrical, Title 27 Mechanical, Title 11 Trees, etc.) and explaining in plain English what each rule means for me:

**1. What kind of "tiny house" am I actually allowed to build?**
- Define the difference, in Portland's terms, between: an Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) on a permanent foundation, a tiny house on wheels (THOW / RV-classed), and a "tiny home" as a primary dwelling. Which Title 33 chapter and section governs each? (e.g., 33.205 Accessory Dwelling Units, 33.110 Single-Dwelling Zones.)
- For each option, can it legally be my primary residence, a rental, a short-term rental, or only used by family?
- Are tiny houses on wheels allowed as permanent dwellings on a residential lot in Portland, or only as temporary/RV use? Cite the specific code.

**2. What is the zoning of 831 SE 174th Ave, and what does it allow?**
- Look up the base zone (likely R-something in east Portland — please confirm) and any overlay zones (e.g., "a" alternative design density, "d" design, "p" environmental protection, environmental conservation, plan district).
- What are the development standards that apply: minimum/maximum lot size, lot coverage, floor area ratio, building height, front/side/rear setbacks (cite Title 33.110 tables).
- Is this lot in a plan district (e.g., Columbia South Shore, Gateway, etc.) or special overlay that changes the rules?
- Does Portland allow more than one dwelling unit on the lot here, and if so under what configuration (ADU, duplex, triplex via Residential Infill Project / RIP — cite 33.110.240 and related)?

**3. What permits will I need, and in what order?**
- List every permit type likely triggered: Zoning permit / Type I land use review, Building permit (Title 24), Plumbing (Title 25), Electrical (Title 26), Mechanical/HVAC (Title 27), Sewer connection, Stormwater (BES requirements), Driveway/curb cut if needed, Tree permit (Title 11) if any tree is within reach of the build.
- Which bureau handles each: Bureau of Development Services (BDS), Bureau of Environmental Services (BES), Portland Water Bureau, PBOT, Urban Forestry, Fire Bureau (Title 31).
- What is the typical sequence: pre-application → zoning confirmation → site plan → building permit → inspections → final occupancy?
- Are there any Type II or Type III land use reviews that could be triggered (adjustment, variance, environmental review)?

**4. Site-specific gotchas I should ask about:**
- Trees: any regulated trees on the lot trigger Title 11 review and possible mitigation/replanting.
- Stormwater: does my lot fall under simplified or full stormwater management (BES Stormwater Manual)?
- Sewer: am I on city sewer or a private system? Is a sewer line extension or capacity charge ("System Development Charges" / SDCs) likely?
- Floodplain, landslide hazard, or environmental zone overlays (especially relevant in east Portland with Johnson Creek nearby).
- Setback from existing house: minimum separation between primary dwelling and ADU.
- Parking: is on-site parking still required for an ADU at this address?
- Utilities: separate vs. shared meters, address assignment.
- Foundation requirement: is a permanent foundation required to count as a dwelling (vs. a THOW)?
